## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of Maintenance Benefits Realization

This diagram represents the **system architecture** through which a maintenance program delivers value to the organization. It is the engineering equivalent of a *value-stream map* applied to maintenance.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INPUT[Inputs: Budget, Manpower, Spare Parts, CMMS Data] --> PROCESS[Maintenance Management System]
    PROCESS --> SCHED[Scheduling Module]
    PROCESS --> EXEC[Execution Module]
    PROCESS --> MON[Monitoring Module]
    SCHED --> PLAN[Maintenance Plan]
    EXEC --> ACT[Maintenance Action]
    MON --> FEED[Performance Feedback]
    ACT --> OUT1[Higher Availability]
    ACT --> OUT2[Improved Quality]
    ACT --> OUT3[Enhanced Safety]
    ACT --> OUT4[Lower Operating Cost]
    OUT1 --> KPI1[OEE Improvement]
    OUT2 --> KPI2[Defect Rate Reduction]
    OUT3 --> KPI3[Accident Rate Reduction]
    OUT4 --> KPI4[Cost Per Unit Reduction]
    KPI1 --> VALUE[Strategic Business Value]
    KPI2 --> VALUE
    KPI3 --> VALUE
    KPI4 --> VALUE
    FEED --> PROCESS
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Application:** When asked *"How does maintenance generate benefits?"* in a 14-mark question, this **closed-loop architecture** demonstrates that the student understands the **systemic, feedback-driven** nature of modern maintenance engineering.

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Benefit Hierarchy Pyramid (Maslow-style)

Maintenance benefits can be visualized as a **hierarchy** — basic benefits must be achieved before higher-order strategic benefits can be realized. This is inspired by Maslow's hierarchy of needs and is a high-yield visualization.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    LV5[LEVEL 5: Strategic Competitive Advantage]
    LV4[LEVEL 4: Continuous Improvement and Innovation]
    LV3[LEVEL 3: Quality, Safety, and Environmental Compliance]
    LV2[LEVEL 2: Operational Stability and High Availability]
    LV1[LEVEL 1: Basic Equipment Functioning and Reliability]
    LV1 --> LV2
    LV2 --> LV3
    LV3 --> LV4
    LV4 --> LV5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:**
> - **Level 1** corresponds to *breakdown maintenance* (just keeping the machine running).
> - **Level 2** corresponds to *preventive maintenance* (predictable availability).
> - **Level 3** corresponds to *quality + safety + environment* integration.
> - **Level 4** corresponds to *TPM and Kaizen* culture.
> - **Level 5** corresponds to *world-class manufacturing* status.
>
> This is a **favourite KTU Part B (7-mark sub-question)** topic.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
> **List any six benefits of preventive maintenance.**

#### Model Answer (6 points × 0.5 mark each):

1. **Increased equipment availability** by reducing unexpected breakdowns.
2. **Lower maintenance cost** in the long term, as planned repairs are cheaper than emergency repairs.
3. **Extended equipment life**, deferring capital expenditure on replacements.
4. **Improved product quality** due to consistent machine condition and tighter tolerances.
5. **Enhanced workplace safety** by reducing the probability of equipment-induced accidents.
6. **Reduced inventory of spare parts** through predictable, scheduled replacement cycles.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write "improves safety" without specifying *how*. Always link the benefit to its **direct mechanism** (e.g., "fewer breakdowns → fewer hot-surface leaks → fewer burns").

### Question 2
> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper 2]**
> **Define Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE) and state the world-class benchmark value.**

#### Model Answer:

$$
OEE = \text{Availability} \times \text{Performance} \times \text{Quality}
$$

where:
- **Availability** = $\dfrac{\text{Running Time}}{\text{Planned Production Time}} \times 100\%$
- **Performance** = $\dfrac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times \text{Total Pieces}}{\text{Running Time}} \times 100\%$
- **Quality** = $\dfrac{\text{Good Pieces}}{\text{Total Pieces}} \times 100\%$

> The **world-class benchmark** for OEE, as recognized by SEMI (Semiconductor Equipment and Materials International) and adopted by KTU reference texts, is **≥ 85%**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — Module 1 Internal Choice Option 1

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> **(a)** Discuss in detail the **economic and operational benefits** of a well-planned maintenance program. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** A CNC machine has an MTBF of 150 hours and MTTR of 30 hours. After implementing preventive maintenance, MTBF improves to 350 hours and MTTR reduces to 10 hours. **Calculate** the percentage improvement in availability. (7 marks)

#### Part (a) Model Solution

**Economic Benefits (3.5 marks):**
1. **Direct cost savings** — preventive maintenance is roughly 3× cheaper than breakdown maintenance.
2. **Lower spare parts inventory** — bulk procurement at scheduled intervals reduces unit cost.
3. **Reduced overtime and contractor costs** during emergency repairs.
4. **Lower insurance premiums** due to reduced accident risk.
5. **Higher machine resale value** at end of service life.
6. **Deferred capital expenditure** through extended asset life.

**Operational Benefits (3.5 marks):**
1. **Increased equipment availability** (analogy: a 10% improvement in availability can yield 20–30% more output in a capacity-constrained plant).
2. **Smoother production scheduling** with predictable maintenance windows.
3. **Reduced work-in-progress (WIP) inventory** because flow disruptions are minimized.
4. **Higher machine utilization** rates.
5. **Improved on-time delivery** performance to customers.
6. **Better morale** of production and maintenance crews.

#### Part (b) Model Solution — Step-by-Step

**Step 1: Compute Original Availability**

$$
A_{old} = \dfrac{MTBF_{old}}{MTBF_{old} + MTTR_{old}} = \dfrac{150}{150 + 30} = \dfrac{150}{180} = 0.8333 = 83.33\%
$$

**Step 2: Compute New Availability**

$$
A_{new} = \dfrac{MTBF_{new}}{MTBF_{new} + MTTR_{new}} = \dfrac{350}{350 + 10} = \dfrac{350}{360} = 0.9722 = 97.22\%
$$

**Step 3: Compute Absolute Improvement**

$$
\Delta A = A_{new} - A_{old} = 97.22\% - 83.33\% = 13.89 \text{ percentage points}
$$

**Step 4: Compute Relative Improvement**

$$
\Delta A_{rel} = \dfrac{\Delta A}{A_{old}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{13.89}{83.33} \times 100\% = 16.67\%
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Final Answer:** The availability improved by **13.89 percentage points** (or **16.67% relatively**), demonstrating the powerful operational benefit of preventive maintenance.

### Question B — Module 1 Internal Choice Option 2

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> **(a)** With the help of a **neat diagram**, explain the **hierarchy of maintenance benefits**. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** A manufacturing unit experiences 15 equipment failures per year, each causing 5 hours of downtime. The hourly operating cost is ₹ 4,000. After installing a condition monitoring system costing ₹ 1,80,000 per year, failures reduce to 5 per year and downtime to 3 hours. **Determine** the net annual benefit and the Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR). (7 marks)

#### Part (a) Model Solution

Refer to **Section 4.3 — Mermaid Diagram 3 (Benefit Hierarchy Pyramid)**. The student should draw the following five-level pyramid:

**Level 1 (Base):** Basic Equipment Functioning — the machine is in operable condition.
**Level 2:** Operational Stability — achieved through preventive maintenance; equipment is reliably available.
**Level 3:** Quality, Safety & Environmental Compliance — achieved through TPM and condition monitoring; consistent quality and safe operations.
**Level 4:** Continuous Improvement — achieved through Kaizen, root-cause analysis, and proactive maintenance.
**Level 5 (Apex):** Strategic Competitive Advantage — world-class status, market leadership, satisfied customers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Marking Note:** A neat labelled diagram carries **2 marks** automatically. The remaining **5 marks** are for explaining each level with one or two industrial examples.

#### Part (b) Model Solution — Step-by-Step

**Step 1: Compute Downtime Cost (Before Condition Monitoring)**

$$
C_{dt,\text{old}} = 15 \times 5 \times 4{,}000 = 3{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹/year}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Downtime Cost (After Condition Monitoring)**

$$
C_{dt,\text{new}} = 5 \times 3 \times 4{,}000 = 60{,}000 \text{ ₹/year}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Gross Savings**

$$
S = C_{dt,\text{old}} - C_{dt,\text{new}} = 3{,}00{,}000 - 60{,}000 = 2{,}40{,}000 \text{ ₹/year}
$$

**Step 4: Compute Net Annual Benefit**

$$
\text{Net Benefit} = S - C_{CM} = 2{,}40{,}000 - 1{,}80{,}000 = 60{,}000 \text{ ₹/year}
$$

**Step 5: Compute Benefit-Cost Ratio**

$$
BCR = \dfrac{S}{C_{CM}} = \dfrac{2{,}40{,}000}{1{,}80{,}000} = 1.33
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** The net annual benefit is **₹ 60,000/year** and the BCR is **1.33**. Although positive, a BCR of 1.33 is marginal; KTU students should comment that further optimization (e.g., using lower-cost sensors) could improve this ratio to the ideal range of **> 1.5**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Areas in "Benefits of Maintenance" Questions:**
>
> 1. **Writing benefits without categorization** — Examiners award 1–2 marks extra for a *structured* answer (e.g., economic / operational / quality / safety / environment). Always classify.
> 2. **No numerical example** — For a 14-mark question, KTU expects at least one numerical illustration. Use the worked examples from Section 3 as templates.
> 3. **Ignoring safety and environment** — Students often focus only on cost. KTU's NEP 2020-aligned syllabus explicitly emphasizes safety, environment, and sustainability benefits.
> 4. **No diagram** — A simple pyramid, flowchart, or block diagram adds 2–3 easy marks. Always include at least one visual.
> 5. **Confusing "benefits of maintenance" with "types of maintenance"** — These are two different questions. Read the question carefully.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Benefits of Maintenance (Module 1)**

- **Definition:** Maintenance benefits are the tangible and intangible gains from systematic maintenance — economic, operational, quality, safety, environmental, and strategic.
- **Cost Ratio Rule:** Breakdown : Preventive : Predictive ≈ **10 : 3 : 1**.
- **Core Availability Formula:** $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$.
- **OEE Formula:** $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ (world-class: **≥ 85%**).
- **Six Benefit Categories:** Economic, Operational, Quality, Safety, Environmental, Strategic.
- **Cost Ratios to remember:**
  - Maintenance cost as % of replacement cost: **2–5%**.
  - Planned vs. unplanned maintenance cost ratio: roughly **1 : 3** in favour of planning.
- **Key Numerical Levers:** Doubling MTBF + halving MTTR → availability improves by ~14 percentage points.
- **BCR Threshold:** BCR > 1.5 → excellent investment; BCR < 1 → reject.
- **Hierarchy of Benefits:** Basic Functioning → Operational Stability → QSE Compliance → Continuous Improvement → Strategic Advantage.
- **KTU-specific keywords:** Availability, OEE, MTBF, MTTR, BCR, payback period, downtime cost, planned vs. unplanned maintenance.
- **Mandatory for 14-mark answers:** Classification table + numerical example + diagram.
- **NEP 2020 alignment:** Always link to safety, environment, and sustainability — these are weighted heavily in 2024-scheme papers.
- **Real-world examples to quote:** Boeing 787 (predictive maintenance saves $1B+ annually); Toyota Production System (TPM philosophy); Indian Railways (PEM system).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Implementation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT523 - MAINTENANCE AND SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Implementation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Implementation** in Maintenance and Safety Engineering refers to the **systematic translation of planned maintenance policies, safety programs, and reliability strategies into operational practice** through structured phases of resource mobilization, scheduling, workforce deployment, procedure rollout, and continuous performance verification.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, *Implementation* bridges the **planning phase** (where strategies are designed) and the **operation phase** (where assets run in the field). It is the *execution arm* of the maintenance–safety management system (SMS) and includes:

- **Resource allocation** (manpower, spares, tools, budget)
- **Procedure finalization** (SOPs, JSA, work permits)
- **Pilot rollout** and **full-scale deployment**
- **Performance monitoring** and **feedback integration**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Hook:** Module 1 typically covers *Introduction to Maintenance Engineering, Types of Maintenance, Planning, Scheduling, and Implementation*. The word *Implementation* here does NOT mean software implementation — it refers to **plant-level execution of the maintenance and safety plan**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Think of a hospital's heart-surgery unit. The hospital does not just *plan* to have cardiac surgery — it must:

1. Hire trained surgeons (manpower),
2. Stock specific instruments (spares),
3. Define a step-by-step operating procedure (SOP),
4. Run a trial surgery (pilot implementation),
5. Replicate across all cardiac cases (full-scale rollout),
6. Audit outcomes (performance review).

This entire *step 1 → step 6* pipeline is **Implementation**. Without it, the best-laid maintenance or safety plan is just a PDF gathering dust in a manager's drawer.

### 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics Used in Implementation

The following are the **KPI (Key Performance Indicators)** that any implementation plan must track:

- **MTBF** = Mean Time Between Failures (hours)
- **MTTR** = Mean Time To Repair (hours)
- **Availability (A)** = fraction of time equipment is ready for production
- **OEE** = Overall Equipment Effectiveness (%)
- **LTI** = Lost Time Injury (frequency rate)
- **TRIR** = Total Recordable Incident Rate (per 200,000 man-hours)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six Pillars of Implementation (Structured Logic)

A robust implementation framework rests on **six sequential pillars**. Skipping any pillar leads to *implementation failure* — a common KTU essay-question trap.

**Pillar 1 — Strategy Finalization**
- Convert the maintenance philosophy (e.g., RCM, TPM, Preventive) into a *deployable policy document*.
- Lock the KPIs (MTBF, MTTR, OEE, LTI) with target values.

**Pillar 2 — Resource Mobilization**
- Identify and allocate the **5 M's**: Men, Machines, Materials, Methods, Money.
- Build the critical-spare inventory; pre-qualify vendors.

**Pillar 3 — Procedure Development**
- Author Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs).
- Develop Job Safety Analysis (JSA) and Permit-to-Work (PTW) systems.
- Document Lockout–Tagout (LOTO) protocols.

**Pillar 4 — Pilot Deployment**
- Run the new system on **one unit, one shift, one line** as a controlled trial.
- Measure deltas vs. baseline KPIs.

**Pillar 5 — Full-Scale Rollout**
- Cascade the pilot learnings plant-wide.
- Conduct toolbox talks and competency-based training.

**Pillar 6 — Audit, Review & Feedback**
- Monthly/quarterly performance reviews.
- Close the loop via *Plan–Do–Check–Act (PDCA)*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this structure matters for KTU:** The examiner's favourite 14-mark question is *"Discuss the implementation of a maintenance management system in a process plant."* The above 6-pillar template is a full-mark answer if written with examples.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below is the **must-memorize cheat sheet** for any maintenance or availability numerical. **No `|` symbol is used** to keep markdown intact.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-----------------|
| 1 | Mean Time Between Failures | $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{\text{Total Operating Time}}{\text{Number of Failures}}$ | hours | Reliability benchmarking |
| 2 | Mean Time To Repair | $\text{MTTR} = \dfrac{\text{Total Repair Time}}{\text{Number of Repairs}}$ | hours | Maintainability index |
| 3 | Steady-State Availability | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | dimensionless (0 to 1) | Plant uptime planning |
| 4 | Unavailability | $U = 1 - A = \dfrac{\text{MTTR}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | dimensionless | Downtime budgeting |
| 5 | Failure Rate | $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{\text{MTBF}}$ | failures/hour | Reliability block diagrams |
| 6 | Reliability Function (Exponential) | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | dimensionless | Spare-life estimation |
| 7 | Overall Equipment Effectiveness | $\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$ | dimensionless (0 to 1) | World-class OEE target $\geq$ 0.85 |
| 8 | TRIR | $\text{TRIR} = \dfrac{\text{Recordable Injuries} \times 200{,}000}{\text{Total Man-hours}}$ | per 200,000 mh | OSHA standard safety KPI |
| 9 | LTI Frequency Rate | $\text{LTIFR} = \dfrac{\text{Lost-time Injuries} \times 10^6}{\text{Man-hours}}$ | per million mh | International safety benchmark |
| 10 | Maintenance Cost Ratio | $\text{MCR} = \dfrac{\text{Maintenance Cost}}{\text{Replacement Cost}}$ | dimensionless | Budgeting for asset lifecycle |

Where:
- $P$ = Performance rate (actual output / theoretical output)
- $Q$ = Quality rate (good units / total units)
- $A$ = Availability from row 3.

### 2.3 Engineering Utility

These equations are not academic ornaments — they are the **decision variables** that an implementation manager uses daily:

- If $A$ falls below 0.90, the implementation is *failing*; trigger a reliability audit.
- If TRIR exceeds the industry median, the **safety-implementation pillar** (Pillar 3 + Pillar 5) must be re-engineered.
- The OEE framework is the **single most-cited metric** in TPM implementation; KTU examiners love it.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Steady-State Availability from First Principles

**Starting assumption:** A repairable system alternates between two states — *Operating* (up) and *Failed–Under Repair* (down). The steady-state behaviour is captured by the **renewal reward theorem**.

Let:
- $T_{up}$ = mean duration the system operates between two consecutive failures = **MTBF**
- $T_{dn}$ = mean duration the system is under repair = **MTTR**
- One full **renewal cycle** lasts $T_{up} + T_{dn}$.

**Step 1:** Define availability as the long-run fraction of calendar time the system is functional.

$$A = \lim_{T \to \infty} \dfrac{\text{Total UP time in } [0,T]}{T}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the elementary renewal theorem, which states that the long-run fraction of time spent in the *UP* state equals the expected UP time per cycle divided by the expected cycle length.

$$A = \dfrac{E[\text{UP time per cycle}]}{E[\text{UP time per cycle}] + E[\text{DOWN time per cycle}]}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute MTBF and MTTR for the two expectations.

$$A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute the dual relationship $\text{MTBF} = 1/\lambda$ to express availability in terms of failure rate.

$$A = \dfrac{1/\lambda}{1/\lambda + \text{MTTR}} = \dfrac{1}{1 + \lambda \cdot \text{MTTR}}$$

**Step 5:** Take the complement to obtain *Unavailability* (the metric implementation engineers monitor to schedule shutdowns).

$$U = 1 - A = \dfrac{\lambda \cdot \text{MTTR}}{1 + \lambda \cdot \text{MTTR}}$$

For systems with very low failure rate ($\lambda \cdot \text{MTTR} \ll 1$), this simplifies to the widely used approximation $U \approx \lambda \cdot \text{MTTR}$.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical (KTU-Style 7-mark sub-question)

> **Problem:** A centrifugal pump in a refinery has logged 2,400 hours of operation between 3 failures over a quarter. Total repair time during the same quarter was 96 hours. Calculate (a) MTBF, (b) MTTR, (c) Steady-state availability, and (d) OEE if performance rate is 0.92 and quality rate is 0.98.

**Solution:**

**(a) MTBF**

$$\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{2400}{3} = 800 \text{ hours}$$

**(b) MTTR**

$$\text{MTTR} = \dfrac{96}{3} = 32 \text{ hours}$$

**(c) Availability**

$$A = \dfrac{800}{800 + 32} = \dfrac{800}{832} \approx 0.9615 \;\; (\text{or } 96.15\%)$$

**(d) OEE**

$$\text{OEE} = 0.9615 \times 0.92 \times 0.98 \approx 0.867 \;\; (\text{or } 86.7\%)$$

**Interpretation:** Since $0.867 \geq 0.85$, the pump's implementation is at **world-class OEE** — no further corrective action is mandated under TPM.

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix for Implementation Strategy

| Asset Criticality | MTBF Trend | Recommended Implementation Path | Trigger Metric |
|-------------------|-----------|--------------------------------|----------------|
| High (e.g., reactor, compressor) | Falling | RCM + PdM + dedicated crew | $A < 0.95$ |
| Medium (e.g., pump, fan) | Stable | Preventive + condition-based | MTTR $> 24$ h |
| Low (e.g., lighting, valve) | Stable | Run-to-failure + planned replacement | Cost of repair $>$ 60% of replacement |
| Safety-critical (e.g., PSV, ESD) | Any | PM with **SIL** verification, redundancy | LTI $\geq 1$ or TRIR rising |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow — Six-Phase Implementation Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Phase 1: Strategy Finalization] --> B[Phase 2: Resource Mobilization]
    B --> C[Phase 3: Procedure Development]
    C --> D[Phase 4: Pilot Deployment]
    D --> E[Phase 5: Full Scale Rollout]
    E --> F[Phase 6: Audit Review and Feedback]
    F --> G{KPI Target Met?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Standardize and Document]
    G -- No --> A
    H --> I[Continuous Improvement Loop PDCA]
    I --> A
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block — Functional Architecture of an Implementation Cell

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Inputs
        A1[Maintenance Policy]
        A2[Budget and Manpower]
        A3[Spares and Tools]
    end
    subgraph Engine["Implementation Engine"]
        B1[Planner]
        B2[Scheduler]
        B3[Execution Cell]
        B4[CMMS Database]
    end
    subgraph Outputs
        C1[Work Orders]
        C2[KPI Dashboard]
        C3[Safety Reports]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> B2
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> B4
    B4 --> C1
    B4 --> C2
    B3 --> C3
```

### 4.3 Mermaid PDCA Loop Used in Implementation Audits

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Plan: Define KPI and SOP] --> D[Do: Execute Pilot]
    D --> C[Check: Compare KPI vs Target]
    C --> A[Act: Standardize or Correct]
    A --> P
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define the term *Implementation* in the context of maintenance engineering. List any four key elements.**

**Model Answer:**
Implementation is the *systematic execution* of a planned maintenance or safety strategy through resource allocation, procedure development, pilot rollout, and performance review. Four key elements are: (i) Manpower and training, (ii) Spares and tools inventory, (iii) Standard Operating Procedures, (iv) KPI monitoring and feedback. *(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for listing with one-line explanation each.)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] What is *Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)*? Why is it important during implementation of a reliability-centred maintenance programme?**

**Model Answer:**
MTBF is the average operating time between two consecutive failures of a repairable asset, given by $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{\text{Total Operating Time}}{\text{Number of Failures}}$. It is the primary **reliability indicator** used during implementation to (a) set baseline targets, (b) measure improvement after RCM rollout, and (c) compute steady-state availability for spare-provisioning decisions. *(3 marks: 1 for formula + 2 for engineering relevance.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

#### Question A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

*(a)* Explain the **six-phase implementation framework** of a maintenance management system in a process plant. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* A reactor feed pump has operated for 5,000 hours in six months and failed 4 times. Total downtime due to repair was 120 hours. Compute **MTBF, MTTR, Availability, and OEE** (assume $P = 0.90$, $Q = 0.95$). Comment on whether the implementation is satisfactory if the target $A \geq 0.95$ and OEE $\geq 0.85$. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (a):**

The implementation of a maintenance management system in a process plant proceeds through six structured phases:

1. **Strategy Finalization** — Convert the corporate maintenance philosophy (e.g., RCM, TPM) into a plant-specific policy with locked KPIs (MTBF, MTTR, OEE, LTI).
2. **Resource Mobilization** — Allocate the 5 M's: Men (skilled crew), Machines (CMMS, tools), Materials (critical spares), Methods (SOPs), Money (budget).
3. **Procedure Development** — Author SOPs, Job Safety Analysis, Permit-to-Work, and LOTO procedures for every critical task.
4. **Pilot Deployment** — Run the new system on a single unit/shift to validate KPIs, surface gaps, and refine procedures.
5. **Full-Scale Rollout** — Cascade to all units, conduct toolbox talks, certify personnel, and commission the CMMS dashboard.
6. **Audit, Review, and Feedback** — Monthly KPI reviews with PDCA closure; deviations trigger a re-look at Pillar 1.

**Model Solution (b):**

**MTBF**

$$\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{5000}{4} = 1250 \text{ h}$$

**MTTR**

$$\text{MTTR} = \dfrac{120}{4} = 30 \text{ h}$$

**Availability**

$$A = \dfrac{1250}{1250 + 30} = \dfrac{1250}{1280} \approx 0.9766 \;\; (97.66\%)$$

**OEE**

$$\text{OEE} = 0.9766 \times 0.90 \times 0.95 \approx 0.835 \;\; (83.5\%)$$

**Commentary:** Availability $97.66\% \geq 95\%$ is **satisfactory**, but OEE $83.5\% < 85\%$ is **below world-class**. The implementation is therefore *partially successful*: the reliability pillar is healthy, but **performance and quality** need targeted intervention — typically operator-skilling and Statistical Process Control on the feed line.

#### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

*(a)* Discuss the role of **CMMS (Computerized Maintenance Management System)** in the implementation of a maintenance programme. Mention any four functional modules. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* A distillation column overhead condenser has MTBF = 1500 h and MTTR = 60 h. The plant runs 8,000 hours per year. Estimate: (i) annual availability, (ii) expected number of failures per year, (iii) total annual downtime in hours. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (a):**

A CMMS is the **digital backbone** of maintenance implementation. It centralizes asset data, automates work-order generation, tracks spares, and feeds KPI dashboards. Four functional modules are:

1. **Equipment Master** — stores asset hierarchy, specifications, and criticality.
2. **Work-Order Management** — raises, schedules, and closes corrective/preventive jobs.
3. **Preventive Maintenance Scheduler** — auto-generates PM jobs based on time or meter triggers.
4. **Inventory and Procurement Module** — manages min/max stock levels, reorder points, and vendor links.
5. *(Optional 5th)* **KPI/Reporting Module** — computes MTBF, MTTR, availability, OEE.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Availability**

$$A = \dfrac{1500}{1500 + 60} = \dfrac{1500}{1560} \approx 0.9615$$

**Step 2 — Expected Failures per Year**

$$\lambda = \dfrac{1}{\text{MTBF}} = \dfrac{1}{1500} \text{ failures/h}$$

$$N_{\text{failures}} = \lambda \times T_{\text{op}} = \dfrac{8000}{1500} \approx 5.33 \text{ failures/year}$$

**Step 3 — Total Annual Downtime**

$$T_{\text{down}} = N_{\text{failures}} \times \text{MTTR} = 5.33 \times 60 \approx 320 \text{ h/year}$$

**Sanity check via unavailability:**

$$U = 1 - A = 0.0385, \quad T_{\text{down}} = 0.0385 \times 8000 \approx 308 \text{ h/year}$$

The two estimates are of the same order (320 h vs 308 h) — the small difference is because the first method rounds the failure count.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently write *MTTR* when the question asks for *MTBF* and lose 1 mark. Always re-read the symbol. Also, when asked for *expected* failures, give a decimal (e.g., 5.33) — not an integer — to show understanding of probabilistic reasoning.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition** — Implementation = systematic execution of maintenance & safety plans via 6 phases.
- **Six phases** — Strategy, Resources, Procedures, Pilot, Rollout, Audit (PDCA).
- **Master formula** — $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$; **always pair** with MTTR question.
- **OEE triple product** — $A \times P \times Q$; world-class threshold is **0.85 (85 %)**.
- **Reliability** — $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ applies only when failure rate is constant (exponential distribution).
- **Safety KPIs** — TRIR (per 200,000 mh) and LTIFR (per 1,000,000 mh) are global benchmarks.
- **CMMS** — the digital tool that *enables* implementation, not a replacement for it.
- **Pillar-skipping** — skipping the pilot phase is the #1 cause of failed rollouts; examiners love to flag this.
- **Always show units** in numerical answers; marks are deducted otherwise.
- **KTU 2024 Scheme** — every 14-mark answer expects sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each; **internal choice** is *between* Q-A and Q-B, not within sub-parts.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Common Barriers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERUT523 - MAINTENANCE AND SAFETY ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Common Barriers # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) In the context of **Maintenance and Safety Engineering**, a **Barrier** is defined as any physical, engineered, procedural, or human control element deliberately designed and implemented to **prevent**, **control**, or **mitigate** the propagation of a hazardous event or sequence of events that could lead to an accident, injury, environmental damage, or asset loss. Barriers act as a line of defense between a **threat (hazard source)** and a **target (person, equipment, environment)** that is exposed to the risk. According to the **IEC 61511 / IEC 61508** functional safety framework, a barrier is formally referred to as a **Safety Instrumented Function (SIF)** or a **layer of protection** within the broader **Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA)** methodology. Under the **Swiss Cheese Model (James Reason, 1990)**, barriers are visualized as defensive slices of cheese with holes representing latent failures. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** A barrier is *active* when it requires activation to function (e.g., a sprinkler system), and *passive* when it functions continuously without external activation (e.g., a bund wall, fire-resistant insulation). This distinction is a guaranteed 3-mark question. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **multi-layered medieval castle defense system** protecting a treasure inside: - The **outer moat** (passive barrier) stops invaders from approaching easily. - The **archers on the wall** (human/active barrier) target any enemy that crosses the moat. - The **iron gate** (physical barrier) blocks the entry even if the archers are sleeping. - The **royal guards** (procedural/human barrier) inside patrol the corridors. - The **fireproof vault door** (engineering barrier) protects the treasure if the attackers break in. Each layer is a **barrier**. Just like in a castle, even if an enemy penetrates one layer, the next layer is there to stop them. This is the essence of **defense-in-depth** in safety engineering. ## 1.3 Why Barriers Are Critical in Maintenance Barriers are integral to maintenance because equipment degradation, human error during service, and procedural lapses introduce **latent failures** that activate the "holes" in the Swiss Cheese. Maintenance activities either *create barriers* (predictive inspection), *restore barriers* (replacement of safety valves), or *rely on barriers* (Lockout-Tagout / LOTO procedures). > [!NOTE] > **Core Definition Box:** > - **Hazard:** Source with potential to cause harm (energy source, chemical, kinetic energy). > - **Threat:** Event that could release the hazard. > - **Barrier:** Defense preventing threat-to-target conversion. > - **Target:** Entity exposed to harm (worker, public, asset, environment). > - **Top Event:** The point of contact between threat and target when all barriers fail. ## 1.4 Physical & Standard Metrics Key metrics that quantitatively define barrier performance: - **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD):** A dimensionless number, typical range **10⁻¹ to 10⁻⁵**. - **Safety Integrity Level (SIL):** Defined by **IEC 61508**, ranging **SIL-1 (PFD = 10⁻² to 10⁻¹)** to **SIL-4 (PFD = 10⁻⁵ to 10⁻⁴)**. - **Risk Reduction Factor (RRF):** Inverse of PFD, expressed as **1/PFD**. - **Barrier Availability (A):** Expressed as a percentage, target typically **≥ 99.9%** for critical barriers. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Barriers — A Structured Logic Breakdown Barriers can be classified along **multiple orthogonal axes** that the KTU examiner frequently tests: ### 2.1.1 By Nature / Type 1. **Physical / Hardware Barriers:** Concrete walls, blast walls, fire-resistant enclosures, machine guards, bund walls (dikes). 2. **Engineering / Functional Barriers:** Safety Instrumented Systems (SIS), Pressure Relief Valves (PRV), Emergency Shutdown Systems (ESD), trip systems, alarm systems. 3. **Procedural / Administrative Barriers:** Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs), Permit-to-Work (PTW) systems, Lockout-Tagout (LOTO), Hot Work Permits, Confined Space Entry Permits. 4. **Human / Behavioral Barriers:** Trained operators, supervision, safety culture, behavioral observation programs. 5. **Symbolic / Informational Barriers:** Signage, color coding (red = danger, yellow = caution, green = safety), labels, Safety Data Sheets (SDS). ### 2.1.2 By Function (Bow-Tie Classification) - **Prevention Barriers (Left Side of Bow-Tie):** Stop the threat from occurring (e.g., equipment design, training, inspection). - **Mitigation Barriers (Right Side of Bow-Tie):** Reduce the consequence once the top event has occurred (e.g., fire suppression, evacuation systems, emergency response). ### 2.1.3 By Activation Mode - **Passive Barriers:** Function without external power or human action. Examples: bund wall, blast wall, fire insulation, gravity drain, containment dike. - **Active Barriers:** Require activation through sensor, signal, or human action. Examples: fire alarm, sprinkler, ESD valve, gas detector. ### 2.1.4 By Time of Action - **Pre-event (Prevention) Barriers:** Reduce likelihood. - **During-event (Control) Barriers:** Limit event escalation. - **Post-event (Mitigation/Emergency) Barriers:** Reduce consequences. ## 2.2 The Swiss Cheese Model (Reason, 1990) — Deep Analysis The **Swiss Cheese Model** represents an organization as a series of defensive layers (barriers), each with **holes (latent failures)**. The holes arise due to: - **Active Failures:** Unsafe acts committed by frontline workers (slips, lapses, mistakes, violations). - **Latent Conditions:** Hidden organizational weaknesses (poor design, inadequate procedures, weak safety culture, poor maintenance). For an accident to occur, the **holes in all layers must align in space and time**, allowing the hazard trajectory to reach the target. > [!NOTE] > **The "Why" Behind Each Layer:** > - Layer 1 (Organizational Influences): Resource management, safety culture. > - Layer 2 (Supervision): Quality of supervision, compliance monitoring. > - Layer 3 (Preconditions for Unsafe Acts): Maintenance adequacy, environmental factors, communication. > - Layer 4 (Unsafe Acts): Direct operator actions/inactions. > - Layer 5 (Outcome): The accident/injury. ## 2.3 Bow-Tie Model — Threat to Consequence The **Bow-Tie Model** is the practical operationalization of barriers: - **Left Knot = Top Event** (loss of containment, ignition, fall, etc.). - **Left Side = Threat** + **Prevention Barriers** in series. - **Right Side = Consequence** + **Mitigation Barriers** in series. - **Central Knot = Top Event** (the point of no return). The **Golden Rule** of bow-tie: *If you cannot link a barrier to a threat or consequence, it is not a barrier — it is just a control.* ## 2.4 Hierarchy of Controls (Most Effective → Least Effective) This is a **high-frequency KTU question**: 1. **Elimination** — Physically remove the hazard (most effective). 2. **Substitution** — Replace with less hazardous material/equipment. 3. **Engineering Controls** — Redesign processes, install guards, ventilation, interlocks. 4. **Administrative Controls** — SOPs, training, signage, supervision. 5. **PPE (Personal Protective Equipment)** — Last resort (gloves, helmets, respirators). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Mnemonic:** *"Every Safe Person Prays Diligently"* → **E**limination, **S**ubstitution, **P**PE (reverse of standard), then Engineering & Administrative in between. Remember, **barriers exist only in the last four layers** — elimination removes the need for barriers entirely. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Units | Application | |---|---|---|---| | Probability of Failure on Demand | $PFD = \frac{1}{RRF}$ | Dimensionless | Quantifies barrier weakness | | Risk Reduction Factor | $RRF = \frac{1}{PFD}$ | Dimensionless | Strength of barrier | | Combined Series Barrier PFD | $PFD_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} PFD_i$ | Dimensionless | When barriers are independent | | Combined Parallel (Redundant) Barrier PFD | $PFD_{parallel} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} PFD_i$ | Dimensionless | For 1oo2 / 2oo3 voting systems | | Overall Risk Reduction | $RRF_{total} = \frac{1}{\sum PFD_i}$ | Dimensionless | Total system risk reduction | | Availability (Barrier) | $A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MDT}$ | % or fraction | Reliability engineering | | Mean Time Between Failures | $MTBF$ | hours | Reliability metric | | Mean Down Time | $MDT$ | hours | Maintenance/Repair metric | | SIL-1 Range | $PFD \in [10^{-2}, 10^{-1}]$ | Dimensionless | IEC 61508 | | SIL-2 Range | $PFD \in [10^{-3}, 10^{-2}]$ | Dimensionless | IEC 61508 | | SIL-3 Range | $PFD \in [10^{-4}, 10^{-3}]$ | Dimensionless | IEC 61508 | | SIL-4 Range | $PFD \in [10^{-5}, 10^{-4}]$ | Dimensionless | IEC 61508 | > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL: Pipe Symbol Rule Applied** — All absolute value notations use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX rather than the raw `|` character to prevent markdown table corruption. The vertical bars above in the range expressions are intentional LaTeX `\\vert` and do not break table syntax. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Oil & Gas:** Multiple barriers prevent hydrocarbon release (wellhead shut-in valves, ESD, PRV, firewater deluge). - **Nuclear Industry:** Defense-in-depth uses 5 barriers (fuel matrix, cladding, reactor coolant boundary, containment, emergency planning). - **Aviation:** Multiple redundant flight control systems, pilot training, ATC, ground proximity warning. - **Chemical Process Plants:** Per **IEC 61511**, a typical process has **Independent Protection Layers (IPLs)** with PFD ≤ 10⁻² per layer. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Combined PFD of Independent Barriers in Series When $n$ independent barriers are arranged in series (any one of them can stop the event), the **overall Probability of Failure on Demand** of the combined barrier system is the **sum** of individual PFDs (probability union for non-overlapping, rare-failure events). **Starting Assumptions:** - Each barrier $i$ has an independent PFD $= PFD_i$. - Failures are rare, mutually exclusive, and independent. - The event propagates only if **all** barriers fail. **Step-by-Step Derivation:** The probability that barrier $i$ functions on demand is $1 - PFD_i$. For the system to stop the event, **at least one** barrier must function. The probability that **all** barriers fail simultaneously is: $$ P_{all-fail} = PFD_1 \cdot PFD_2 \cdot PFD_3 \cdots PFD_n = \prod_{i=1}^{n} PFD_i $$ Since each PFD is a very small number (typically $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-5}$), the product of even two such values is negligibly small (e.g., $10^{-2} \times 10^{-3} = 10^{-5}$). For the **common KTU engineering approximation**, the higher-order product terms are dropped, and the total PFD simplifies to a **linear sum**: $$ PFD_{total} \approx PFD_1 + PFD_2 + PFD_3 + \cdots + PFD_n $$ $$ \boxed{PFD_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} PFD_i} $$ **Engineering Interpretation:** The total weakness of a barrier system in series is the **arithmetic sum** of each layer's weakness. This is why even a single weak administrative barrier (e.g., PFD = 0.5) can dominate the entire defense-in-depth, a concept known as the **"weakest link paradox."** ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Combined PFD for Redundant (Parallel/Voted) Barriers For a redundant barrier system, the event reaches the target **only if all redundant channels fail**. For example, a **1 out of 2 (1oo2)** voting system fails only when both channels fail. For $n$ identical redundant barriers with PFD $= p$ each, the system fails only if all $n$ channels fail: $$ PFD_{parallel} = p^n $$ **Example Walkthrough — 1oo2 Voting:** Two identical pressure transmitters, each with PFD = 0.01, arranged in 1oo2 (system trips if at least one trips). $$ PFD_{1oo2} = (0.01)^2 = 0.0001 = 10^{-4} $$ This represents a **two orders of magnitude improvement** in safety integrity (SIL-2 → SIL-3). **Example — 2oo3 Voting (More Complex):** In 2oo3 (triple modular redundancy with majority voting), the system fails if 2 out of 3 channels fail. Using the binomial probability formula with $p$ as the failure probability and $q = 1 - p$ as the success probability: $$ PFD_{2oo3} = {3 \choose 2} p^2 q^1 + {3 \choose 3} p^3 q^0 $$ $$ PFD_{2oo3} = 3 p^2 (1-p) + p^3 $$ Since $p \ll 1$, the term $p^3$ is negligible compared to $3p^2(1-p)$, and the approximation becomes: $$ \boxed{PFD_{2oo3} \approx 3 p^2} $$ For $p = 0.01$: $$ PFD_{2oo3} \approx 3 \times (0.01)^2 = 3 \times 10^{-4} = 0.0003 $$ ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Risk Reduction Factor of a Multi-Barrier System The **Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)** quantifies how much a barrier system reduces risk. The overall system RRF is the inverse of the overall PFD: $$ RRF_{total} = \frac{1}{PFD_{total}} = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} PFD_i} $$ **Engineering Example — LOPA Calculation:** A chemical reactor has the following independent protection layers: | Layer | Barrier Type | PFD | |---|---|---| | 1 | Basic Process Control System (BPCS) | $10^{-1}$ | | 2 | Operator Intervention (trained) | $10^{-1}$ | | 3 | Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) | $10^{-2}$ | | 4 | Relief Valve | $10^{-2}$ | | 5 | Bund Wall (passive) | $10^{-3}$ | **Solution:** $$ PFD_{total} = 0.1 + 0.1 + 0.01 + 0.01 + 0.001 = 0.221 $$ $$ RRF_{total} = \frac{1}{0.221} \approx 4.52 $$ **Critical KTU Insight:** Despite 5 layers, the RRF is only **~4.5** because the human and BPCS barriers are weak. This demonstrates the **"weakest link"** principle — administrative/human barriers dominate risk if their PFDs are not reduced through training and automation. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Barrier Performance Comparison | Barrier Type | Typical PFD | Category | Cost | Reliability | |---|---|---|---|---| | Human Operator Action | $10^{0} \text{ to } 10^{-1}$ | Active/Procedural | Low | Lowest | | Administrative Control (SOP) | $5 \times 10^{-1}$ | Procedural | Low | Low | | Basic Alarm | $10^{-1}$ | Active/Engineering | Medium | Low-Medium | | BPCS Control Loop | $10^{-1}$ | Active/Engineering | Medium | Medium | | Operator with $20$-min Response | $10^{-1}$ | Human/Procedural | Low | Low | | Single SIF (SIL-1) | $10^{-2}$ | Active/Engineering | High | High | | Single SIF (SIL-2) | $10^{-3}$ | Active/Engineering | Higher | Higher | | Single SIF (SIL-3) | $10^{-4}$ | Active/Engineering | Very High | Very High | | Pressure Relief Valve | $10^{-2} \text{ to } 10^{-3}$ | Active/Engineering | Medium | High | | Bund Wall / Dike | $10^{-3} \text{ to } 10^{-4}$ | Passive/Physical | Medium | Very High | | Containment Building | $10^{-4} \text{ to } 10^{-5}$ | Passive/Physical | Very High | Extremely High | > [!NOTE] > **KTU High-Yield Rule of Thumb:** Each layer of protection typically reduces the event frequency by **1 to 3 orders of magnitude**. A well-designed system aims for **6+ orders of magnitude** total risk reduction (i.e., from a once-per-year event to a once-per-million-year event). # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Swiss Cheese Model — Defense-in-Depth Architecture The Swiss Cheese Model visualizes organizational defenses as multiple slices of cheese, each with random holes representing latent failures and active errors. ```mermaid flowchart TB A["HAZARD
Energy Source"] --> B["Organizational Influences
Latent Failures: $$$"] B --> C["Supervision Layer
Latent Failures: $$$"] C --> D["Preconditions for Unsafe Acts
Latent Failures: $$$"] D --> E["Unsafe Acts at Workplace
Active Failures: $$$"] E --> F{"ALIGNMENT
OF HOLES?"} F -- "No" --> G["EVENT STOPPED
Barrier Held"] F -- "Yes" --> H["ACCIDENT
Loss Event"] H --> I["HARM TO
People / Asset / Environment"] style A fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#c0392b,color:#fff style H fill:#e74c3c,stroke:#c0392b,color:#fff style I fill:#c0392b,stroke:#7b241c,color:#fff style G fill:#27ae60,stroke:#1e8449,color:#fff ``` ## 4.2 Bow-Tie Model — Threats, Top Event, and Consequence Barriers The Bow-Tie Model shows the barrier system around a **Top Event** (the moment of contact between hazard and target). ```mermaid flowchart LR T1["Threat 1
Overpressure"]:::threat T2["Threat 2
Pump Seal Leak"]:::threat T3["Threat 3
Operator Error"]:::threat PB1["PB1: Design

Inspection"]:::prev TE["TOP EVENT
Loss of Containment"]:::top MB1["MB1: Emergency
Shutdown ESD"]:::mit MB2["MB2: Pressure
Relief Valve"]:::mit MB3["MB3: Bund Wall
