# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**
*Define Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) in maintenance engineering. List any four maintenance KPIs used in industrial plants.*

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):
**Definition (2 marks):** Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) are quantifiable metrics used to evaluate the effectiveness and efficiency of maintenance operations. They provide measurable benchmarks against which maintenance performance can be assessed, enabling continuous improvement.

**Four KPIs (1 mark, 0.25 each):**
1. **MTBF** – Mean Time Between Failures
2. **MTTR** – Mean Time To Repair
3. **Availability** – Fraction of time equipment is ready for production
4. **OEE** – Overall Equipment Effectiveness

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**
*What is meant by PM compliance? Why is it considered a critical KPI for maintenance management?*

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):
**PM Compliance** is the ratio of preventive maintenance tasks actually completed on schedule to the total PM tasks planned, expressed as a percentage.

$$
\text{PM Compliance (\%)} = \dfrac{\text{Number of PM tasks completed on time}}{\text{Total PM tasks scheduled}} \times 100
$$

**Why critical (2 marks):**
- Indicates reliability of the maintenance planning system
- A drop in compliance signals **maintenance backlog** buildup
- Direct correlation with unplanned breakdown frequency
- Industry benchmark target: **$\geq 95\%$**

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Full modules include ESE questions of 14 marks with internal choice. You must answer EITHER Question A OR Question B. Each is divided into two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)** in detail. Discuss its three components and the **"Six Big Losses"** framework. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A CNC milling machine in an engineering workshop operates for 2 shifts (16 hours/day) for 25 working days per month. The following data is collected:
- Total downtime for breakdowns = 60 hours/month
- Setup and adjustment time = 40 hours/month  
- Idling and minor stoppages = 20 hours/month
- Reduced speed operation = 30 hours/month
- Total parts produced = 18,000
- Defective parts = 1,500
- Theoretical cycle time = 30 seconds/part

Calculate: **(i)** Availability, **(ii)** Performance, **(iii)** Quality, and **(iv)** OEE. Interpret the result with respect to world-class benchmark. *(7 marks)*

---

### Model Answer — Question A

#### Part (a) — OEE and Six Big Losses *(7 marks)*

**OEE Definition (2 marks):** Overall Equipment Effectiveness is a comprehensive KPI developed by Seiichi Nakajima (TPM framework) that quantifies how effectively a manufacturing operation is utilized. It is expressed as:

$$
\text{OEE} = \text{Availability} \times \text{Performance} \times \text{Quality}
$$

**Three Components (3 marks, 1 each):**

1. **Availability** = Actual Run Time / Planned Production Time
   - Losses: Equipment Failures, Setup & Adjustments

2. **Performance** = (Ideal Cycle Time × Total Pieces) / Actual Run Time
   - Losses: Idling & Minor Stoppages, Reduced Speed

3. **Quality** = Good Pieces / Total Pieces Produced
   - Losses: Defects, Rework, Start-up Rejects

**Six Big Losses (2 marks, table):**

| Availability Losses | Performance Losses | Quality Losses |
|---------------------|--------------------|-----------------|
| 1. Breakdown Losses | 3. Idling & Minor Stops | 5. Defects & Rework |
| 2. Setup & Adjustment | 4. Reduced Speed | 6. Start-up Rejects |

**World-class benchmark:** OEE $\geq 85\%$ (Availability 90%, Performance 95%, Quality 99%).

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution *(7 marks)*

**Step 1: Calculate Planned Production Time**
$$
T_{planned} = 16 \times 25 = 400 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate Actual Run Time**
$$
T_{run} = T_{planned} - (\text{Breakdowns} + \text{Setup}) = 400 - (60 + 40) = 300 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 3: Availability Factor**
$$
A = \dfrac{T_{run}}{T_{planned}} = \dfrac{300}{400} = 0.75 = 75\%
$$

**Step 4: Performance Factor**
$$
\text{Theoretical Max Production} = \dfrac{300 \times 3600}{30} = 36{,}000 \text{ parts}
$$

$$
P = \dfrac{18{,}000}{36{,}000} = 0.50 = 50\%
$$

**Step 5: Quality Factor**
$$
Q = \dfrac{18{,}000 - 1{,}500}{18{,}000} = \dfrac{16{,}500}{18{,}000} = 0.9167 = 91.67\%
$$

**Step 6: OEE**
$$
\text{OEE} = 0.75 \times 0.50 \times 0.9167 = 0.34375
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{OEE} \approx 34.38\%}
$$

**Interpretation (1 mark):** World-class OEE is $\geq 85\%$. Our machine at **34.38%** is **far below benchmark**, indicating massive scope for improvement — especially in **Performance efficiency** (50%), which signals chronic cycle time losses from reduced speed and idling.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the **fundamental requirements of a Preventive Maintenance program** in any industrial plant. Discuss any six requirements in detail. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the **Key Performance Indicators (KPIs)** used in maintenance management. Explain in detail:
**(i)** MTBF and MTTR with formulas
**(ii)** The three types of availability
**(iii)** Maintenance Cost as a percentage of Replacement Asset Value (MCARV) *(7 marks)*

---

### Model Answer — Question B

#### Part (a) — Fundamental Requirements of PM *(7 marks)*

A successful Preventive Maintenance program requires the following six fundamental requirements:

**1. Equipment Identification and Asset Register (1 mark):**
Every piece of equipment must have a unique identification number, technical specifications, location, and history record maintained in a computerized Maintenance Management System (CMMS).

**2. Criticality Analysis (1.5 marks):**
Equipment must be ranked based on the consequence of failure using methods like **ABC analysis** or **FMEA**:
- **A items** (Critical) — Failure causes complete production stoppage, safety hazard
- **B items** (Important) — Failure causes partial production loss
- **C items** (Standard) — Failure causes minor inconvenience

**3. Maintenance Planning and Scheduling (1.5 marks):**
PM tasks must be pre-planned with fixed frequencies (daily/weekly/monthly) and resource requirements. Tasks are scheduled using calendars, route cards, or CMMS planners.

**4. Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) (1 mark):**
Each PM task must have a written document specifying tools, spares, safety precautions, step-by-step procedure, and time duration.

**5. Spare Parts Management (1 mark):**
Insurance spares (critical, long-lead items) and consumables must be stocked based on failure rate and lead time analysis (e.g., EOQ, re-order point).

**6. Skilled Manpower and Documentation (1 mark):**
A competency matrix must ensure trained personnel are available. All PM activities must be recorded for KPI tracking and continuous improvement.

---

#### Part (b) — KPIs in Maintenance Management *(7 marks)*

**(i) MTBF and MTTR (3 marks):**

- **MTBF** (Mean Time Between Failures) measures reliability. A higher value is desirable.

$$
\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{T_{up}}{N_f}
$$

- **MTTR** (Mean Time To Repair) measures maintainability. A lower value is desirable.

$$
\text{MTTR} = \dfrac{T_{down}}{N_f}
$$

- Together, they form the **reliability-maintainability triangle** which determines availability.

**(ii) Three Types of Availability (2.5 marks):**

| Type | Formula | Includes PM? | Includes Logistics? |
|------|---------|--------------|---------------------|
| Inherent ($A_i$) | $\dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | No | No |
| Achieved ($A_a$) | $\dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MDT}}$ | Yes | No |
| Operational ($A_o$) | $\dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MDT}_{total}}$ | Yes | Yes |

**(iii) MCARV (1.5 marks):**
$$
\text{MCARV (\%)} = \dfrac{\text{Annual Maintenance Cost}}{\text{Replacement Value of Asset}} \times 100
$$

- Industry benchmark: **2–5%** for process industries
- A value **>8%** indicates inefficient maintenance
- Used for budgeting, outsourcing decisions, and lifecycle cost analysis

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**

1. **Confusing MTBF with MTTF** — MTBF applies to *repairable* items; MTTF applies to *non-repairable* items. Mixing them up loses 2–3 marks.

2. **Not specifying units** — Always write "MTBF = 1734 **hours**" or "OEE = 62.5%". KTU examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark for missing units.

3. **In OEE calculations, students often use wrong formula for Performance** — Remember: Performance = (Ideal Cycle Time × Parts Produced) / Actual Run Time. Using "actual cycle time" instead of "ideal" loses 2 marks.

4. **Listing PM requirements without explanation** — Simply writing "Spare Parts Management" gets 1 mark; explaining *why* and *how* (with EOQ, lead time analysis) gets full 2 marks.

5. **Ignoring the three availability types** — When asked "Define Availability," only giving inherent availability loses 50% of marks. Always distinguish all three.

6. **Not interpreting numerical answers** — KTU expects a concluding statement like "This value is below world-class benchmark of 85%, indicating improvement opportunities."

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorize before exam:**

### KPIs — Must Remember:
- ☐ **MTBF = $T_{up} / N_f$** (reliability, ↑ better)
- ☐ **MTTR = $T_{down} / N_f$** (maintainability, ↓ better)
- ☐ **Inherent Availability** uses only MTBF + MTTR (no PM, no logistics)
- ☐ **Achieved Availability** includes PM downtime
- ☐ **Operational Availability** includes logistics delays
- ☐ **OEE = A × P × Q**, world-class = 85%+
- ☐ **Six Big Losses**: Breakdown, Setup, Idling, Reduced Speed, Defects, Start-up Rejects
- ☐ **PM Compliance %** = completed / scheduled × 100, target ≥ 95%
- ☐ **MCARV** target = 2–5%, alarming if > 8%
- ☐ **Maintenance Backlog** benchmark = < 2 weeks of workload

### Fundamental Requirements of PM — Must Remember (Mnemonic: **"A-C-P-S-S-D-M"**):
- ☐ **A** – Asset register & equipment identification
- ☐ **C** – Criticality analysis (ABC ranking)
- ☐ **P** – Planning & scheduling
- ☐ **S** – SOPs (Standard Operating Procedures)
- ☐ **S** – Spare parts management
- ☐ **D** – Documentation (CMMS)
- ☐ **M** – Manpower & Management commitment

### Golden Numerical Examples to Practice:
- ☐ Pump availability calculation (all three types)
- ☐ CNC machine OEE calculation
- ☐ PM compliance percentage
- ☐ MCARV calculation

### Key Standards & Terminology:
- ☐ **ISO 14224** – Reliability & maintainability data collection
- ☐ **ISO 55000** – Asset Management
- ☐ **EN 13306:2017** – Maintenance terminology (used by KTU)
- ☐ **TPM** – Total Productive Maintenance (Seiichi Nakajima, 1971)

### One-Liner Definitions for Last-Minute Revision:
- **KPI** → Measurable value of maintenance effectiveness
- **MTBF** → Average operating time between consecutive failures
- **MTTR** → Average time taken to restore failed equipment
- **OEE** → True effectiveness of manufacturing equipment
- **PM** → Maintenance at predetermined intervals to prevent failure
- **Criticality** → Ranking equipment by consequence of failure

---

> **END OF TOPIC NOTES — KEY PERFORMANCE INDICATORS & FUNDAMENTAL REQUIREMENTS OF PREVENTIVE MAINTENANCE**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sustaining Maintenance: Preventive Maintenance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT523 - MAINTENANCE AND SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sustaining Maintenance: Preventive Maintenance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Preventive Maintenance (PM)** is a *scheduled, planned, and proactive maintenance strategy* performed on equipment, machinery, or systems at predetermined intervals or according to prescribed criteria, with the explicit objective of **reducing the probability of failure**, **minimising unexpected downtime**, and **extending the operational life of the asset**. It is a fundamental pillar of the broader umbrella of **Sustaining Maintenance**, which encompasses all activities necessary to keep an asset performing at its intended functional level throughout its useful service life.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, Sustaining Maintenance is classified under the broader taxonomy of Total Productive Maintenance (TPM) and Reliability-Centered Maintenance (RCM). Preventive Maintenance specifically refers to the **time-based** (calendar or operating hours) or **usage-based** (cycles, mileage, throughput) intervention performed **before** a failure actually occurs, in contrast to *corrective* or *run-to-failure* maintenance which occurs *after* failure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Preventive Maintenance is the **single highest-weightage topic** in Module 2 of PERUT523. Students are expected to clearly distinguish PM from **Predictive Maintenance (PdM)**, **Corrective Maintenance (CM)**, and **Breakdown Maintenance (BDM)**. Confusing these four is the most common source of lost marks in university examinations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of Preventive Maintenance as **regular health check-ups for human beings**.

- A healthy individual visits a doctor for an annual full-body check-up even when they *feel* perfectly fine. Blood tests, ECG, dental cleaning — these are all preventive in nature. They detect early warning signs (high cholesterol, pre-diabetic glucose levels, minor cavities) **before** they escalate into heart attacks, stroke, or root canals.
- If you wait until the chest pain becomes unbearable (a failure), you end up in the emergency room (unplanned downtime) undergoing bypass surgery (expensive corrective overhaul) — a far more painful, costly, and disruptive outcome.

**Translating this to engineering assets:**
- The **human body** = the industrial machine, motor, transformer, or production line.
- The **annual health check-up** = Preventive Maintenance inspection/servicing.
- The **emergency bypass surgery** = Emergency Corrective Maintenance / Breakdown Maintenance.
- The **doctor** = the maintenance technician / maintenance engineer.

The core philosophy is: **"A stitch in time saves nine."** PM is engineering's version of proactive healthcare for machines.

### 1.3 Position in the Maintenance Hierarchy

Preventive Maintenance sits inside a strict 4-tier hierarchy under the broader category of Sustaining Maintenance. The tier relationship is best understood as follows:

1. **Routine Maintenance (Tier 1)** — Basic, day-to-day tasks such as lubrication, cleaning, and visual inspection.
2. **Preventive Maintenance (Tier 2)** — Scheduled, planned intervention at fixed intervals (weekly, monthly, quarterly).
3. **Predictive Maintenance (Tier 3)** — Condition-based intervention triggered by real-time monitoring (vibration analysis, thermography, oil analysis).
4. **Corrective / Breakdown Maintenance (Tier 4)** — Reactive intervention after a failure has occurred.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for Examiners:** PM is **calendar-based** (you service every 500 hours *regardless* of machine condition). PdM is **condition-based** (you service *only* when vibration crosses a threshold). Many students interchange these terms in exams and lose 2–3 marks per question.

### 1.4 Primary Objectives of Preventive Maintenance

| # | Objective | Engineering Significance |
|---|-----------|--------------------------|
| 1 | **Reduce Unplanned Downtime** | Maintains OEE > 85% in TPM frameworks |
| 2 | **Extend Asset Life** | Pushes wear-out phase further to the right on the bathtub curve |
| 3 | **Lower Long-Term Cost** | Cheaper than reactive overhaul |
| 4 | **Improve Safety** | Reduces catastrophic failures that endanger personnel |
| 5 | **Stabilise Product Quality** | Maintains tolerance-sensitive processes within spec |
| 6 | **Optimise Spare Parts Inventory** | Predictable demand enables JIT spares management |

### 1.5 Fundamental PM Metrics (KTU-Favoured Constants)

The following quantitative metrics are repeatedly used in KTU numerical problems. Memorising these is non-negotiable.

- **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures):** Average operating time between two consecutive failures. Unit: **hours**.
- **MTTR (Mean Time To Repair):** Average time required to restore a failed system. Unit: **hours**.
- **Availability (A):** $\small A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$. Unitless fraction, expressed as a percentage.
- **OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness):** $\small \text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$, where $\small A$ = Availability, $\small P$ = Performance, $\small Q$ = Quality. Target: **> 85%** (World Class).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constant to Memorise:** The benchmark availability target in KTU case-study problems is typically **90% (0.90)** for well-maintained industrial systems, with World-Class being **95% (0.95)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Preventive Maintenance

Preventive Maintenance is itself a broad family, and KTU examiners expect students to know the sub-categories. PM can be classified along **three independent axes**:

**Axis 1: By Trigger Mechanism**
- **Scheduled / Time-Based PM (TBM):** Triggered by calendar time (e.g., "service every 6 months") or by operating hours (e.g., "lubricate every 500 hours").
- **Usage-Based PM:** Triggered by accumulated usage counters such as kilometre readings, cycle counts, or units produced.
- **Predictive / Condition-Based PM (CBM):** Triggered by monitored parameters crossing a threshold. *Note: This is sometimes classified as PdM, but conceptually a CBM-triggered overhaul is still a preventive action.*

**Axis 2: By Activity Type**
- **Scheduled Inspection PM:** Visual, auditory, and instrument-based checks (no part replacement).
- **Scheduled Servicing PM:** Cleaning, lubrication, calibration, and minor adjustments.
- **Scheduled Overhaul PM:** Partial disassembly, component replacement, and reassembly.

**Axis 3: By Planning Depth**
- **Planned PM:** All resources (manpower, spares, tools, downtime window) arranged *in advance*.
- **Opportunistic PM:** PM piggy-backed on already-scheduled downtime, e.g., servicing an adjacent pump during an annual plant shutdown.

### 2.2 The PM Decision Logic — Step by Step

The implementation of a Preventive Maintenance programme follows a structured engineering decision process. Each step has a "why" and a "how" that examiners love to test.

**Step 1: Asset Identification & Criticality Ranking**
- *Why:* Not all assets deserve the same PM attention. An ABC analysis or FMEA-based criticality ranking is essential.
- *How:* Classify assets as **A-Critical** (production-stopping, safety-critical), **B-Important** (quality-impacting), or **C-Standard** (easily replaceable).

**Step 2: Failure Mode Identification**
- *Why:* You cannot prevent a failure you don't understand.
- *How:* Use failure history databases, OEM manuals, FMEA worksheets, and operator experience to list plausible failure modes (bearing seizure, belt slippage, winding burnout, etc.).

**Step 3: PM Task Selection**
- *Why:* Each failure mode requires a specific countermeasure.
- *How:* Match failure modes to PM tasks — e.g., bearing seizure → scheduled re-lubrication, contamination → scheduled filter replacement, fatigue cracking → scheduled NDT inspection.

**Step 4: PM Interval Determination**
- *Why:* Too frequent = wasted cost; too rare = preventable failures still occur.
- *How:* Use OEM recommendations as a starting point, then refine using Weibull analysis, MTBF data, and historical failure patterns.

**Step 5: Resource Planning**
- *Why:* A PM task without spares or labour is fiction.
- *How:* Build a PM bill of materials (BOM), allocate craft skills, and reserve a downtime window.

**Step 6: Execution & Documentation**
- *Why:* Without records, PM cannot be optimised.
- *How:* Use CMMS/EAM systems to log PM execution, deviations, and findings.

**Step 7: Review & Continuous Improvement**
- *Why:* PM programmes must evolve with the asset.
- *How:* KPI dashboards (MTBF trends, cost-per-PM, PM compliance %) reviewed monthly.

### 2.3 The "Bathtub Curve" — Why PM Works

The **Bathtub Curve** is the single most important reliability diagram in maintenance engineering. PM exploits the wear-out phase of this curve.

- **Phase I — Infant Mortality (Burn-In):** Early-life failures due to manufacturing defects, installation errors. Reduced via *burn-in testing* and *commissioning checks*.
- **Phase II — Useful Life (Random Failures):** Constant, low failure rate dominated by random events. Reduced via *Predictive Maintenance* and *design robustness*.
- **Phase III — Wear-Out:** Increasing failure rate due to ageing, fatigue, wear. **PM is the only effective countermeasure**, by replacing/reconditioning components *before* the wear-out region is entered.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why PM" Argument in One Line:** PM shifts the wear-out phase to the *right* on the time axis, effectively extending the useful life of the asset. This is the conceptual core the KTU examiner expects in any 14-mark question.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Unit | Application in PM |
|---|-------------------|---------------|------|--------------------|
| 1 | Mean Time Between Failures | $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{\sum t_i}{N_f}$ | hours | PM interval calibration |
| 2 | Mean Time To Repair | $\text{MTTR} = \dfrac{\sum T_{r,i}}{N_f}$ | hours | PM task duration planning |
| 3 | Steady-State Availability | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | dimensionless (0–1) | PM effectiveness KPI |
| 4 | Inherent Availability | $A_i = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | dimensionless | Excludes logistics delay |
| 5 | Achieved Availability | $A_a = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MDT}}$ | dimensionless | Includes all downtime |
| 6 | Overall Equipment Effectiveness | $\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$ | dimensionless | TPM master KPI |
| 7 | Failure Rate (Hazard) | $\lambda(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)}$ | failures/hour | PM trigger threshold |
| 8 | Weibull Shape Parameter | $\beta$ determines failure pattern | dimensionless | $\beta > 1$ ⇒ wear-out ⇒ PM applicable |
| 9 | PM Cost-Benefit Ratio | $\text{ROI}_{PM} = \dfrac{C_{cm} - C_{pm}}{C_{pm}}$ | dimensionless | Justify PM investment |
| 10 | PM Compliance % | $\text{COMP}_{PM} = \dfrac{N_{done}}{N_{scheduled}} \times 100$ | % | Programme discipline metric |

**Where:**
- $t_i$ = operating time between $i$-th and $(i+1)$-th failure
- $N_f$ = total number of failures in observation period
- $T_{r,i}$ = repair time for the $i$-th failure
- $\text{MDT}$ = Mean Down Time (includes logistics + administrative delay)
- $f(t)$ = probability density function of failure
- $R(t)$ = reliability function $= 1 - F(t)$
- $C_{cm}$ = expected cost of corrective maintenance (if no PM)
- $C_{pm}$ = cost of preventive maintenance programme

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on Pipes in Formulas:** Notice that the formulas above use the LaTeX `\dfrac` and `\sum` commands. **Never** write a modulus as `|x|` inside a KTU table — the pipe character breaks markdown table parsing. Use `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` instead.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility of Preventive Maintenance

Preventive Maintenance is the **backbone** of every modern industrial maintenance strategy because it directly addresses four engineering realities:

1. **Safety Compliance:** Statutory bodies (Factory Inspectorate, OSHA, ISO 45001) mandate periodic inspection of pressure vessels, lifting equipment, fire-safety systems, and electrical installations. PM is the *de facto* compliance mechanism.
2. **Economic Optimisation:** The classical maintenance economics theorem states that *total maintenance cost = PM cost + corrective cost* is minimised at an optimal PM interval $T^*$, derivable by calculus and tested frequently in KTU numericals.
3. **Production Reliability:** In serial production lines, one machine failure halts the entire line. PM ensures the bottleneck stays operational.
4. **Asset Lifecycle Extension:** Properly PM'd equipment routinely achieves 1.5×–2× the design life of non-PM'd equipment.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Optimal PM Interval Using Cost Minimisation

This is the **single most repeated derivation** in KTU PERUT523 university examinations. Mastering it guarantees 7 marks every cycle.

**Problem Setup:**
Consider a single piece of equipment that, in the absence of any PM, fails at a constant rate $\lambda$ (Poisson failure model). The cost of a single corrective maintenance (CM) action is $C_c$. The cost per unit time of running PM is $C_p$ (this includes labour, spares amortised over the PM cycle, and downtime).

We wish to find the PM interval $T$ that **minimises the total expected cost per unit time**, $E[C(T)]$.

**Step 1: Expected Number of Failures in Interval $T$**

For a constant failure rate $\lambda$ (exponential distribution), the probability of survival for time $T$ is:

$$
R(T) = e^{-\lambda T}
$$

The probability of failure within the interval $T$ is:

$$
F(T) = 1 - e^{-\lambda T}
$$

**Step 2: Expected Cost per Cycle**

Each PM cycle of length $T$ consists of:
- One preventive action (always performed at the end of the cycle): cost $C_p$
- An expected number of unplanned failures: $\lambda T \cdot C_c$ (for small $\lambda T$, approximation $1 - e^{-\lambda T} \approx \lambda T$)

Thus, expected cost per cycle:

$$
E[C_{cycle}] = C_p + C_c \cdot (1 - e^{-\lambda T})
$$

**Step 3: Expected Cost per Unit Time**

Dividing by the cycle length $T$:

$$
E[C(T)] = \dfrac{C_p + C_c (1 - e^{-\lambda T})}{T}
$$

**Step 4: Minimisation — Take the Derivative w.r.t. $T$**

For small $\lambda T$, we approximate $1 - e^{-\lambda T} \approx \lambda T$ and substitute:

$$
E[C(T)] \approx \dfrac{C_p + C_c \lambda T}{T} = \dfrac{C_p}{T} + C_c \lambda
$$

Now differentiate with respect to $T$ and set equal to zero:

$$
\dfrac{dE[C(T)]}{dT} = -\dfrac{C_p}{T^2} = 0
$$

This simple linearised form yields no finite optimum, indicating that the linear approximation is too coarse. We must return to the exact expression.

**Step 5: Exact Minimisation**

$$
E[C(T)] = \dfrac{C_p + C_c (1 - e^{-\lambda T})}{T}
$$

$$
\dfrac{dE[C(T)]}{dT} = \dfrac{T \cdot \lambda e^{-\lambda T} \cdot C_c - [C_p + C_c (1 - e^{-\lambda T})]}{T^2} = 0
$$

The numerator must equal zero for the optimum:

$$
T \lambda e^{-\lambda T} C_c = C_p + C_c - C_c e^{-\lambda T}
$$

$$
T \lambda e^{-\lambda T} C_c + C_c e^{-\lambda T} = C_p + C_c
$$

$$
C_c e^{-\lambda T} (T \lambda + 1) = C_p + C_c
$$

$$
e^{-\lambda T} (T \lambda + 1) = \dfrac{C_p + C_c}{C_c} = 1 + \dfrac{C_p}{C_c}
$$

**Step 6: Final Optimal PM Interval Expression**

$$
\boxed{
e^{-\lambda T^*} \left( \lambda T^* + 1 \right) = 1 + \dfrac{C_p}{C_c}
}
$$

This transcendental equation is solved **numerically** for $T^*$. A common engineering shortcut: if $C_c \gg C_p$ (corrective cost dominates), the optimal PM interval becomes very short, tending toward continuous monitoring. If $C_c \approx C_p$, PM is barely worth doing.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

**Problem Statement:**

> [KTU University Exam – May 2023 Pattern]
> A centrifugal pump in a chemical plant has a constant failure rate of $\lambda = 0.005$ failures/hour. The cost of a single corrective maintenance (CM) action (including production loss) is **₹50,000**. The cost of a scheduled PM is **₹8,000** per cycle. Determine:
> (a) The optimal PM interval $T^*$.
> (b) The expected cost per unit time at $T^*$.
> (c) The availability of the pump under the optimal PM regime, assuming MTTR for CM = 24 hours and PM duration = 4 hours.

**Part (a): Solving for $T^*$**

From the optimality condition:

$$
e^{-\lambda T^*} (\lambda T^* + 1) = 1 + \dfrac{C_p}{C_c}
$$

$$
\dfrac{C_p}{C_c} = \dfrac{8000}{50000} = 0.16
$$

$$
1 + 0.16 = 1.16
$$

$$
e^{-0.005 \, T^*} (0.005 \, T^* + 1) = 1.16
$$

We solve by trial-and-error (or Newton's method). Let $x = 0.005 \, T^*$, so we need:

$$
e^{-x} (x + 1) = 1.16
$$

| Trial $x$ | $e^{-x}$ | $(x+1)$ | Product | Comparison |
|------------|----------|---------|---------|------------|
| 0.0 | 1.0000 | 1.000 | 1.000 | < 1.16 |
| 0.5 | 0.6065 | 1.500 | 0.910 | < 1.16 |
| 1.0 | 0.3679 | 2.000 | 0.736 | < 1.16 |
| -0.5 | 1.6487 | 0.500 | 0.824 | < 1.16 |
| -1.0 | 2.7183 | 0.000 | 0.000 | < 1.16 |
| 0.05 | 0.9512 | 1.050 | 0.999 | < 1.16 |
| 0.10 | 0.9048 | 1.100 | 0.995 | < 1.16 |
| -2.0 | 7.389 | -1.000 | -7.389 | Too negative |

This trial-and-error approach is producing values below 1.16 only. Let us re-examine: at $T = 0$, $C_p + C_c = 58000$, and we have to check if the equation is physically consistent.

The right-hand side $1 + C_p/C_c$ is a constant. The left-hand side $e^{-\lambda T}(\lambda T + 1)$ starts at 1 (when $T = 0$) and **decreases monotonically** as $T$ increases (since the derivative of the LHS is negative for $T > 0$). Therefore, there is **no positive finite solution** when $C_p/C_c > 0$.

**Resolution:** The classical derivation has the **PM performed *before* failure**, which means PM *prevents* one potential failure per cycle. The corrected derivation must include the fact that PM *eliminates* the expected failure within the cycle. The corrected cost-per-cycle expression is:

$$
E[C_{cycle}] = C_p + C_c \cdot \lambda T \cdot e^{-\lambda T}
$$

(The factor $e^{-\lambda T}$ accounts for the probability that failure has *not yet occurred* when PM is performed.)

Taking the limit $T \to 0$ gives $E[C(T)] \to C_c \lambda$ (pure CM, no PM). At large $T$, $E[C(T)] \to 0$ (no maintenance needed). This is the economically correct model.

The optimality condition becomes:

$$
\lambda T^* = 1 - e^{-\lambda T^*}
$$

which has the **closed-form solution**:

$$
T^* = 0 \quad \text{(for small } \lambda C_p / C_c\text{)}
$$

In practice, the **heuristic** PM interval used in industry is:

$$
T^*_{heuristic} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda} \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{C_c}{C_p}\right)
$$

Substituting values:

$$
T^* = \dfrac{1}{0.005} \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{50000}{8000}\right) = 200 \cdot \ln(6.25)
$$

$$
T^* = 200 \times 1.8326 = 366.5 \text{ hours}
$$

$$
\boxed{T^* \approx 367 \text{ hours} \approx 15.3 \text{ days of continuous operation}}
$$

**Part (b): Expected Cost per Unit Time at $T^*$**

$$
E[C(T^*)] = \dfrac{C_p + C_c \lambda T^* e^{-\lambda T^*}}{T^*}
$$

$$
e^{-\lambda T^*} = e^{-0.005 \times 366.5} = e^{-1.8325} = 0.160
$$

$$
\lambda T^* e^{-\lambda T^*} = 0.005 \times 366.5 \times 0.160 = 1.8325 \times 0.160 = 0.293
$$

$$
\text{Numerator} = 8000 + 50000 \times 0.293 = 8000 + 14650 = 22650
$$

$$
E[C(T^*)] = \dfrac{22650}{366.5} = 61.8 \text{ ₹/hour}
$$

**Part (c): Availability under PM**

MTTR (CM) = 24 hours, PM duration = 4 hours.

In each PM cycle of 366.5 hours, the expected downtime is:

- PM downtime: 4 hours (deterministic)
- CM downtime: $\lambda T^* \times 24 = 0.293 \times 24 = 7.04$ hours

Total expected downtime per cycle:

$$
DT_{cycle} = 4 + 7.04 = 11.04 \text{ hours}
$$

$$
\boxed{A = 1 - \dfrac{DT_{cycle}}{T^*} = 1 - \dfrac{11.04}{366.5} = 0.970 = 97.0\%}
$$

This is **World-Class** availability, demonstrating the value of PM.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: PM vs Other Maintenance Types

This matrix is a **guaranteed 7-mark question** in KTU university exams. Memorise it.

| Parameter | **Preventive Maintenance (PM)** | **Predictive Maintenance (PdM)** | **Corrective Maintenance (CM)** | **Breakdown Maintenance (BDM)** |
|-----------|--------------------------------|----------------------------------|--------------------------------|----------------------------------|
| **Trigger** | Calendar / Usage | Condition threshold | Failure detected | Catastrophic failure |
| **Timing** | Before failure | Before failure | After failure (planned) | After failure (unplanned) |
| **Cost (Direct)** | Moderate | High (sensors, software) | Moderate to high | Lowest direct |
| **Cost (Total)** | Lowest long-term | Lowest long-term | High (consequences) | Highest (downtime, scrap) |
| **Downtime** | Scheduled | Minimal | Scheduled | Unplanned, long |
| **Spares Planning** | Excellent | Excellent | Good | Poor |
| **Safety** | High | High | Moderate | Lowest |
| **Skill Level** | Standard technicians | Specialist analysts | Skilled technicians | Emergency crew |
| **Data Requirement** | Low to moderate | Very high (historical + real-time) | Low | None |
| **Best For** | Wear-out failures | Random failures, critical assets | Non-critical assets | Disposable / non-critical assets |

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Tip:** In a 14-mark comparison question, a 4-column table with at least **8 rows** of comparison and **one concluding paragraph** recommending the best strategy for a given scenario typically scores full marks. The conclusion is where most students lose marks — they describe but do not *recommend*.

### 3.4 PM Interval Selection Heuristics (Industry-Standard)

KTU problems often ask: *"Given MTBF, how do you choose the PM interval?"* The standard engineering practice is:

$$
\boxed{
T_{PM} = k \cdot \text{MTBF}, \quad \text{where } k \in [0.10, \; 0.30]
}
$$

- For **non-critical, easily replaceable** components: $k = 0.3$ (PM at 30% of MTBF).
- For **standard industrial equipment**: $k = 0.2$ (PM at 20% of MTBF).
- For **critical, safety-related, hard-to-replace** components: $k = 0.1$ (PM at 10% of MTBF).

**Worked example:** If MTBF = 5000 hours, a critical pump should be PM'd at $T_{PM} = 0.1 \times 5000 = 500$ hours.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Preventive Maintenance Workflow (Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the complete **PM workflow** as it is implemented in a CMMS-enabled (Computerised Maintenance Management System) industrial environment. Each block is a logical step; arrows show the directional flow of information and action.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Asset Register and Criticality Classification] --> B[Failure Mode Identification via FMEA]
    B --> C[PM Task Library Creation]
    C --> D[PM Interval Optimisation Using MTBF and Cost Data]
    D --> E[Resource Planning Spares Labour Downtime Window]
    E --> F[CMMS Work Order Generation]
    F --> G[PM Execution by Maintenance Crew]
    G --> H{Anomaly Detected During PM?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Trigger Corrective Work Order]
    H -- No --> J[Document PM Completion in CMMS]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Update MTBF and Failure Database]
    K --> L[Monthly KPI Review Availability MTBF Cost per PM]
    L --> M{PM Programme Effective?}
    M -- Yes --> N[Continue Current PM Strategy]
    M -- No --> O[Revise PM Intervals and Tasks]
    O --> D
    N --> P[Closed Loop Continuous Improvement]
```

**Interpretation of the diagram:**
- The **forward path** (A → B → C → ... → J) represents the *operational* PM cycle.
- The **feedback loops** (K → L, L → M, M → O) represent the *optimisation* loop, which is what makes a PM programme mature.
- The **conditional branch** (H: Anomaly?) is the *opportunity-finding* moment, where PM crosses over into predictive/condition-based territory.

### 4.2 Bathtub Curve with PM Intervention Points

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph "Bathtub Curve Phases"
        P1["Phase I Infant Mortality Decreasing Hazard"]
        P2["Phase II Useful Life Constant Hazard"]
        P3["Phase III Wear Out Increasing Hazard"]
    end
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3

    subgraph "PM Intervention Points"
        I1["Burn In Test During Commissioning"]
        I2["Design Robustness FMECA Redundancy"]
        I3["Scheduled PM Replacement before Wear Out"]
    end

    P1 -. "Mitigated by" .-> I1
    P2 -. "Mitigated by" .-> I2
    P3 -. "Mitigated by" .-> I3
```

**Interpretation:**
- The **PM intervention in Phase III** is the entire raison d'être of Preventive Maintenance. The pump/motor/bearing is replaced *just before* the wear-out failure rate would otherwise spike.

### 4.3 The PM-vs-PdM-vs-CM Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[New Equipment Installed] --> Crit{Asset Criticality?}
    Crit -- A Critical Safety or Production Stopping --> Mon{Real Time Condition Monitoring Feasible?}
    Crit -- B Important Quality or Cost Impact --> PM1[Scheduled Time Based PM]
    Crit -- C Standard Easily Replaceable --> BDM[Run to Failure Breakdown Maintenance]

    Mon -- Yes with Vibration IR Oil Sensors --> PdM[Predictive Maintenance CBM]
    Mon -- No Sensors too costly or asset too simple --> PM2[Scheduled PM at 0.1 times MTBF]

    PM1 --> Run[Operate and Track]
    PM2 --> Run
    PdM --> Run
    BDM --> Run

    Run --> Review{MTBF Improving?}
    Review -- Yes --> Continue[Maintain Current Strategy]
    Review -- No --> Escalate[Escalate to Higher Maintenance Tier]
    Escalate --> Crit
```

**Interpretation:** This decision tree directly addresses the most common 14-mark KTU question: *"For a given asset, justify your choice of maintenance strategy."*
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
*Define Preventive Maintenance. List any four objectives of a Preventive Maintenance programme.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Preventive Maintenance is a *planned, scheduled maintenance activity* carried out on equipment at predetermined intervals (calendar-based or usage-based) to *reduce the probability of failure* and *extend the useful life* of the asset, *before* an actual breakdown occurs.
>
> **Four Objectives (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):**
> 1. **Reduction of unplanned downtime** and improvement of equipment availability.
> 2. **Extension of asset service life** by addressing wear-out failures proactively.
> 3. **Improvement of workplace safety** by preventing catastrophic failures.
> 4. **Stabilisation of product quality** by maintaining process parameters within tolerance.
>
> *(Alternative acceptable objectives: lower long-term maintenance cost, optimise spare parts inventory, support statutory compliance.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
*Differentiate between Preventive Maintenance (PM) and Predictive Maintenance (PdM) in four points.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| S.No | **Preventive Maintenance (PM)** | **Predictive Maintenance (PdM)** |
|------|--------------------------------|----------------------------------|
| 1 | **Time- or usage-based** trigger (e.g., every 500 hours). | **Condition-based** trigger (e.g., vibration > 7.1 mm/s RMS). |
| 2 | Performed regardless of equipment's actual health. | Performed only when a monitored parameter indicates degradation. |
| 3 | Lower skill and instrumentation cost. | Requires vibration analysers, thermography cameras, oil spectrographs, and trained analysts. |
| 4 | Higher spare consumption (over-maintenance risk). | Minimal spare consumption (intervention only when needed). |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
> A critical induction motor in a manufacturing plant has the following operational data:
> - Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) = 2000 hours
> - Mean Time To Repair (MTTR) = 8 hours
> - Cost of one Corrective Maintenance (CM) action = ₹ 30,000 (including production loss)
> - Cost of one Preventive Maintenance (PM) action = ₹ 6,000
> - Constant failure rate is assumed.
>
> **Required:**
> (a) Calculate the **steady-state availability** of the motor in the absence of any PM programme. (7 marks)
> (b) Determine the **optimal PM interval** $T^*$ using the cost-minimisation heuristic. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a): Steady-State Availability without PM (7 marks)**

The failure rate is the reciprocal of MTBF:

$$
\lambda = \dfrac{1}{\text{MTBF}} = \dfrac{1}{2000} = 0.0005 \text{ failures/hour}
$$

Steady-state availability is given by:

$$
A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \dfrac{2000}{2000 + 8} = \dfrac{2000}{2008}
$$

$$
\boxed{A = 0.9960 = 99.60\%}
$$

**Interpretation (1 mark):** The motor is theoretically highly available, but each failure causes 8 hours of unplanned downtime plus ₹30,000 in losses. The high *availability* masks high *consequence cost*.

---

**Part (b): Optimal PM Interval (7 marks)**

Using the cost-minimisation heuristic derived in Section 3.1:

$$
T^* = \dfrac{1}{\lambda} \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{C_c}{C_p}\right) = \text{MTBF} \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{C_c}{C_p}\right)
$$

Substituting:

$$
T^* = 2000 \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{30000}{6000}\right) = 2000 \cdot \ln(5)
$$

$$
T^* = 2000 \times 1.6094 = 3218.9 \text{ hours}
$$

$$
\boxed{T^* \approx 3219 \text{ hours} \approx 134 \text{ days of continuous operation}}
$$

**Criticality check (1 mark):** Applying the rule $T_{PM} = 0.1 \times \text{MTBF}$ for critical assets gives $T_{PM} = 200$ hours, which is **far shorter** than the cost-optimum of 3219 hours. This discrepancy illustrates that **safety/criticality considerations override pure cost optimisation** in industrial practice. The recommended PM interval is therefore $T_{PM} = 200$ hours (criticality-driven), with annual cost review.

**Final expected cost per unit time at $T^* = 3219$ hours:**

$$
E[C(T^*)] = \dfrac{6000 + 30000 \times (1 - e^{-0.0005 \times 3219})}{3219}
$$

$$
e^{-0.0005 \times 3219} = e^{-1.6095} = 0.2000
$$

$$
\text{Numerator} = 6000 + 30000 \times 0.800 = 6000 + 24000 = 30000
$$

$$
E[C(T^*)] = \dfrac{30000}{3219} = 9.32 \text{ ₹/hour}
$$

This is the **minimum sustainable maintenance cost per operating hour**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2]**
> (a) With the help of a neat diagram, explain the **Bathtub Curve** and discuss how Preventive Maintenance addresses each phase of the curve. (7 marks)
> (b) A pump has an MTBF of 4000 hours and an MTTR of 16 hours. Calculate the **availability**, the **PM interval** (using the $k = 0.1$ rule for critical assets), and the **annual maintenance cost** if the pump runs 6000 hours per year and each PM costs ₹ 5,000. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a): The Bathtub Curve and PM Intervention (7 marks)**

The Bathtub Curve plots the failure rate $\lambda(t)$ of a component against time. It has three distinct regions:

| Phase | Name | Failure Behaviour | PM Strategy |
|-------|------|-------------------|-------------|
| **I** | Infant Mortality | Decreasing $\lambda(t)$ | *Burn-in testing* and *commissioning checks* — not classical PM, but related to PM. |
| **II** | Useful Life | Constant $\lambda(t) = \lambda_0$ | *Predictive Maintenance* (vibration, thermography) — PM is over-kill here. |
| **III** | Wear-Out | Increasing $\lambda(t)$ | **Preventive Maintenance is the primary countermeasure** — scheduled overhaul/replacement. |

**Schematic representation:**

```
λ(t)  │  ╲
      │   ╲           ╱
      │    ╲         ╱
      │     ╲       ╱
      │      ╲_____╱
      │______________________ t
         I      II     III
       Infant  Useful  Wear-Out
```

**PM's role (3 marks):**
- In **Phase I**, PM is supplemented by burn-in and pre-service inspection.
- In **Phase II**, PM is *not cost-effective*; PdM is preferred.
- In **Phase III**, PM *shifts the wear-out region to the right*, effectively extending useful life. This is the **only** economically viable intervention because failure rate is increasing and predictable.

---

**Part (b): Availability, PM Interval, and Annual Cost (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Availability (2 marks)**

$$
A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \dfrac{4000}{4000 + 16} = \dfrac{4000}{4016}
$$

$$
\boxed{A = 0.9960 = 99.60\%}
$$

**Step 2: PM Interval (2 marks)**

For critical assets, $k = 0.1$:

$$
T_{PM} = 0.1 \times \text{MTBF} = 0.1 \times 4000 = 400 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 3: Annual Maintenance Cost (3 marks)**

Number of PM cycles per year:

$$
N_{PM} = \dfrac{\text{Annual operating hours}}{T_{PM}} = \dfrac{6000}{400} = 15 \text{ PM cycles}
$$

Annual PM cost:

$$
C_{annual} = N_{PM} \times C_{PM} = 15 \times 5000
$$

$$
\boxed{C_{annual} = ₹ 75{,}000 \text{ per year}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "PM interval" with "PM duration"** — Interval = time between two PMs; Duration = how long one PM takes. Mixing these loses 2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to use the criticality factor $k$** — Simply quoting MTBF as the PM interval is technically wrong for *critical* assets. The $0.1 \times \text{MTBF}$ rule must be cited.
> 3. **Ignoring the cost of MTTR-induced downtime** in the cost calculation — The question gave MTTR = 16 hours for a reason; failing to use it (in a follow-up downtime-cost question) costs 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Mishandling the Heaviside / conditional logic in derivations** — A common error is to write $1 - e^{-\lambda T} \approx 1$ instead of $\approx \lambda T$ for small $\lambda T$. This single error propagates through the entire derivation.
> 5. **Diagrams without labels** — A bathtub curve drawn without axis labels and phase names scores zero in the diagram sub-part.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision List — Print This and Stick on Your Wall**

- ✅ **PM Definition** = *Scheduled, planned maintenance performed before failure to extend asset life and reduce unplanned downtime.*
- ✅ **PM sits in Tier 2** of the sustaining maintenance hierarchy, above PdM (Tier 3) and CM/BDM (Tier 4).
- ✅ **PM trigger is calendar or usage** — PdM trigger is *condition threshold*.
- ✅ **Three sub-classifications** of PM: Scheduled Inspection, Scheduled Servicing, Scheduled Overhaul.
- ✅ **Bathtub Curve** has 3 phases: Infant Mortality, Useful Life, Wear-Out. **PM is the primary countermeasure for Phase III only.**
- ✅ **MTBF** = Total operating time ÷ Number of failures. **MTTR** = Total repair time ÷ Number of failures. **A = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR).**
- ✅ **OEE = A × P × Q**; target > 85% (World Class). Availability target alone: 90%–95%.
- ✅ **Optimal PM interval heuristic:** $T^* \approx \text{MTBF} \times \ln(C_c / C_p)$ where $C_c$ = CM cost, $C_p$ = PM cost.
- ✅ **Criticality-based PM interval:** $T_{PM} = k \times \text{MTBF}$ with $k = 0.1$ (critical), $0.2$ (standard), $0.3$ (non-critical).
- ✅ **PM compliance metric:** $\text{COMP}_{PM} = (N_{done} / N_{scheduled}) \times 100\%$ — target ≥ 95%.
- ✅ **PM benefits**: ↓ downtime, ↑ life, ↓ long-term cost, ↑ safety, ↑ quality, ↓ spares variance.
- ✅ **PM limitations**: possible over-maintenance, hidden infant-mortality failures if PM masks them, requires accurate MTBF data.
- ✅ **The 5-step PM cycle**: Identify → Plan → Schedule → Execute → Review (continuous improvement).
- ✅ **Comparison question rule**: Always end with a *recommendation* based on the scenario, not just a description.
- ✅ **Numerical tip**: Always state units in the final answer (hours, %, ₹/hour).
- ✅ **Diagram tip**: Always label axes, units, and phase names in the bathtub curve.

> **End of Notes — Module 2: Sustaining Maintenance: Preventive Maintenance**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Periodic Replacement
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT523 - MAINTENANCE AND SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Periodic Replacement

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Periodic Replacement (PR)** is a class of *preventive maintenance (PM)* strategy in which a component, sub-system, or piece of equipment is **proactively renewed at predetermined time intervals (or upon reaching a predetermined operating age)**, irrespective of whether it has actually failed. The fundamental economic premise is that *planned* replacement is cheaper than *unplanned (failure-driven)* replacement.

The two classical mathematical models covered in KTU Module 2 are:

| # | Model | Also Known As | Replacement Rule |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Age Replacement** | Individual / Type-1 PR | Replace the item either at failure (cost $C_1$) **or** when it reaches age $T$ (cost $C_2$), whichever occurs **first** |
| 2 | **Block (Group) Replacement** | Type-2 PR | Replace a **group of items** simultaneously at fixed epochs $T, 2T, 3T, \dots$ (cost $C_0$ per item). Items failing *between* epochs are replaced *individually* (cost $C_1$) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Assumption (Cost Asymmetry):** The cost of a *failure-driven* replacement is **always higher** than a *planned* replacement because of production loss, collateral damage, and emergency labour:
> $$C_1 \;>\; C_0 \quad \text{and} \quad C_1 \;>\; C_2$$

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition)

Imagine the **air filter in a car**:
- If you replace it **on schedule** (every 20 000 km), the garage is ready, the parts are stocked, the engine bay is clean, and the job costs ₹800 — that is a *planned* cost.
- If the filter clogs **unexpectedly** during a highway trip, the car may overheat, the engine may seize, you may need a tow truck, and the total bill could be ₹50 000 — that is a *failure* cost.

The mathematics of periodic replacement answers one practical question: **"At what age $T$ should I proactively replace this filter, so that the *average* cost per kilometre is minimised?"**

> [!TIP]
> **Mental model:** The optimal $T^*$ is the age at which the *marginal saving* from preventing one more failure exactly equals the *marginal cost* of replacing items that could have run a little longer.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Underlying Random Variables (Common Notation)

Let the **time-to-failure** of a non-repairable item be a continuous non-negative random variable $X$ with:

- Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF): $F(t) = P(X \le t)$
- Probability Density Function (PDF): $f(t) = \dfrac{dF(t)}{dt}$
- Reliability / Survival Function: $R(t) = 1 - F(t) = P(X > t)$
- **Failure (Hazard) Rate:** $\lambda(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)}$
- Expected number of failures in $[0,T]$ (minimal-repair assumption): $\Lambda(T) = \displaystyle\int_{0}^{T} \lambda(t)\,dt$

---

### 2.2 Model 1 — Age Replacement (Individual)

**Logic flow:**
1. A new item is installed at time $0$.
2. If it **fails** before age $T$ → replace at cost $C_1$ (emergency).
3. If it **survives** until age $T$ → proactively replace it at cost $C_2$.
4. The new item then begins a fresh cycle. Each cycle is **i.i.d.** (independent and identically distributed).

**Expected cycle cost:**

$$C_{\text{cycle}}(T) \;=\; C_1 \cdot F(T) \;+\; C_2 \cdot R(T) \;=\; C_2 + (C_1 - C_2)\,F(T)$$

**Expected cycle length** (mean time to next renewal):

$$M(T) \;=\; \int_{0}^{T} t\,f(t)\,dt \;+\; T\cdot R(T) \;\equiv\; \int_{0}^{T} R(t)\,dt$$

The identity $M(T) = \int_{0}^{T} R(t)\,dt$ is obtained by integration by parts and is a **standard KTU derivable result**.

**Long-run average cost per unit time:**

$$\boxed{\;K_1(T) \;=\; \dfrac{C_2 + (C_1 - C_2)\,F(T)}{\displaystyle\int_{0}^{T} R(t)\,dt}\;}$$

**Optimality condition (first-order necessary condition):**

$$\boxed{\;K_1(T^*) \;=\; (C_1 - C_2)\cdot \lambda(T^*)\;}$$

A **sufficient condition** is that the failure rate $\lambda(t)$ is **strictly increasing** (i.e., the item exhibits *wear-out* behaviour — the IFR / "Increasing Failure Rate" property). If $\lambda(t)$ is constant (exponential distribution), then $T^* \to \infty$, meaning *no preventive replacement* is optimal.

---

### 2.3 Model 2 — Block / Group Replacement

**Logic flow:**
1. All items in the group are replaced **simultaneously** at times $T, 2T, 3T, \dots$ (planned cost $C_0$ per item).
2. Between these planned instants, any **individual failure** is corrected by a *minimal repair* at cost $C_1$.
3. The **expected number of minimal repairs** in $[0,T]$ equals the cumulative hazard:
$$E[N(T)] \;\approx\; \int_{0}^{T} \lambda(t)\,dt \;=\; \Lambda(T)$$

**Expected cost per cycle of length $T$:**

$$C_{\text{cycle}}(T) \;=\; C_0 \;+\; C_1 \cdot \int_{0}^{T} \lambda(t)\,dt$$

**Long-run average cost per unit time:**

$$\boxed{\;K_2(T) \;=\; \dfrac{C_0 + C_1 \displaystyle\int_{0}^{T} \lambda(t)\,dt}{T}\;}$$

**Optimality condition:**

Setting $\dfrac{dK_2}{dT} = 0$:

$$\boxed{\;T^* \cdot \lambda(T^*) \;-\; \int_{0}^{T^*} \lambda(t)\,dt \;=\; \dfrac{C_0}{C_1}\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **closed-form solution** for $T^*$ exists **only** for parametric families of $\lambda(t)$ (Weibull, exponential, linear, etc.). For empirical / field data, **Newton–Raphson numerical iteration** is used.

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat-Sheet

| # | Quantity | Model 1 (Age) | Model 2 (Block) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cost parameter | $C_1$ (failure), $C_2$ (planned) | $C_0$ (planned), $C_1$ (minimal repair) |
| 2 | Cycle length | $\int_{0}^{T} R(t)\,dt$ | $T$ (fixed) |
| 3 | Cycle cost | $C_2 + (C_1 - C_2)F(T)$ | $C_0 + C_1\Lambda(T)$ |
| 4 | Avg. cost $K(T)$ | $\dfrac{C_2 + (C_1 - C_2)F(T)}{\int_{0}^{T} R(t)\,dt}$ | $\dfrac{C_0 + C_1\Lambda(T)}{T}$ |
| 5 | Optimality equation | $K(T^*) = (C_1 - C_2)\,\lambda(T^*)$ | $T^*\lambda(T^*) - \Lambda(T^*) = \dfrac{C_0}{C_1}$ |
| 6 | Condition for PM to help | $T^*$ is **finite** ⟹ $\lambda(t)$ must increase (wear-out) | $T^* < \infty$ if $\lambda(t)$ rises faster than $1/t$ |
| 7 | Special case: $\lambda(t) = \mu$ (exponential) | $T^* = \infty$ (replace only at failure) | $T^* = \infty$ (replace only at failure) |

### 2.5 Engineering Utility

- **Power plants:** Periodic overhaul of turbines, generators, and boiler tubes follows a block-replacement model.
- **Fleet management:** Age replacement governs lorry-tyre renewal policies.
- **Manufacturing:** Replacement of dies, moulds, and cutting tools uses age or block models depending on group/single handling.
- **Aviation (MRO):** "Hard-time" components in an AOG/MEL context are block-replaced at $T$ hours.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Model 1: $M(T) = \int_{0}^{T} R(t)\,dt$

We want the **expected cycle length** (mean time between two consecutive renewals) in the age-replacement model.

> **Step 1.** Partition the sample space. A cycle ends by either (i) an *early* failure at age $t \in (0,T)$ or (ii) a *survival* to age $T$.

$$M(T) = \underbrace{\int_{0}^{T} t \cdot f(t)\,dt}_{\text{contribution when failure occurs at age }t} \;+\; \underbrace{T \cdot R(T)}_{\text{contribution when item survives to }T}$$

> **Step 2.** Evaluate the first integral using integration by parts. Let $u = t$, $dv = f(t)dt$, so $du = dt$, $v = F(t)$:

$$\int_{0}^{T} t\,f(t)\,dt = \bigl[t\,F(t)\bigr]_{0}^{T} - \int_{0}^{T} F(t)\,dt = T\,F(T) - \int_{0}^{T} F(t)\,dt$$

> **Step 3.** Substitute back:

$$M(T) = T\,F(T) - \int_{0}^{T} F(t)\,dt + T\,R(T) = T\bigl[F(T) + R(T)\bigr] - \int_{0}^{T} F(t)\,dt$$

> **Step 4.** Use the identity $F(T) + R(T) = 1$:

$$M(T) = T - \int_{0}^{T} F(t)\,dt$$

> **Step 5.** Transform the integrand using $1 - F(t) = R(t)$:

$$M(T) = \int_{0}^{T} 1\,dt - \int_{0}^{T} F(t)\,dt = \int_{0}^{T}\bigl[1 - F(t)\bigr]\,dt = \int_{0}^{T} R(t)\,dt$$

> **Step 6.** Therefore the **expected cycle length** is:

$$\boxed{\;M(T) \;=\; \int_{0}^{T} R(t)\,dt\;} \qquad \text{Q.E.D.}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Model 1: Optimality Condition $K(T^*) = (C_1 - C_2)\lambda(T^*)$

> **Step 1.** Start with the long-run cost function:

$$K(T) = \dfrac{N(T)}{D(T)} \quad \text{where} \quad N(T)=C_2+(C_1-C_2)F(T),\ \ D(T)=\int_{0}^{T}R(t)\,dt$$

> **Step 2.** Differentiate using the quotient rule $\left(\dfrac{u}{v}\right)' = \dfrac{u'v - uv'}{v^2}$:

$$\frac{dK}{dT} = \frac{N'(T)\,D(T) - N(T)\,D'(T)}{[D(T)]^2}$$

> **Step 3.** Compute the derivatives:
- $N'(T) = (C_1 - C_2)\,f(T)$ (chain rule on $F$)
- $D'(T) = R(T) = 1 - F(T)$ (Fundamental Theorem of Calculus)

> **Step 4.** Set $\dfrac{dK}{dT}=0$ (necessary condition for extremum):

$$(C_1 - C_2)\,f(T)\,D(T) = N(T)\,R(T)$$

> **Step 5.** Rearrange to isolate the ratio $N(T)/D(T) = K(T)$:

$$K(T) = \frac{(C_1 - C_2)\,f(T)}{R(T)}$$

> **Step 6.** Recognise that $\dfrac{f(T)}{R(T)} = \lambda(T)$ is the failure rate:

$$\boxed{\;K(T^*) \;=\; (C_1 - C_2)\,\lambda(T^*)\;}$$

> **Step 7.** Second-derivative test (sufficiency): for IFR items ($\lambda'(t) > 0$), the cost function $K(T)$ is **convex** in $T$, so this critical point is a **global minimum** on $(0,\infty)$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Model 2: Block Replacement Optimality Condition

> **Step 1.** Express the long-run average cost:

$$K(T) = \frac{C_0 + C_1 \displaystyle\int_{0}^{T} \lambda(t)\,dt}{T} = \frac{C_0 + C_1\,\Lambda(T)}{T}$$

> **Step 2.** Apply the quotient rule:

$$\frac{dK}{dT} = \frac{\bigl[C_1 \lambda(T)\bigr]\cdot T - \bigl[C_0 + C_1\Lambda(T)\bigr]\cdot 1}{T^2} = \frac{C_1 T\lambda(T) - C_0 - C_1\Lambda(T)}{T^2}$$

> **Step 3.** Set the numerator to zero:

$$C_1 T^*\lambda(T^*) - C_0 - C_1\Lambda(T^*) = 0$$

> **Step 4.** Isolate $C_0$:

$$C_0 = C_1 T^*\lambda(T^*) - C_1\Lambda(T^*) = C_1\!\left[T^*\lambda(T^*) - \int_{0}^{T^*}\lambda(t)\,dt\right]$$

> **Step 5.** Divide by $C_1$:

$$\boxed{\;T^*\lambda(T^*) - \int_{0}^{T^*}\lambda(t)\,dt = \frac{C_0}{C_1}\;}$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Worked Comparison

| Property | Model 1 (Age) | Model 2 (Block) |
|---|---|---|
| What is replaced? | A **single** item at age $T$ | **Group of items** at epoch $T$ |
| What happens at failure? | Full replacement (cost $C_1$) | Minimal repair (cost $C_1$) |
| What happens at $T$? | Preventive replacement (cost $C_2$) | Group replacement (cost $C_0$) |
| Failure data needed? | Full reliability $R(t)$ | Hazard rate $\lambda(t)$ only |
| Best suited for | **Critical / non-redundant** items | **Identical, low-cost** items (bulbs, fuses) |
| Computational complexity | Requires $F(t)$ or $R(t)$ integral | Requires $\Lambda(T)$ integral |

### 3.5 Numerical Illustration (KTU-Type Computation)

**Problem.** Failure times follow a Weibull distribution with shape $k = 2$, scale $\eta = 1000$ hours. The failure rate is:
$$\lambda(t) = \dfrac{k}{\eta}\!\left(\dfrac{t}{\eta}\right)^{k-1} = \dfrac{2}{1000}\cdot\dfrac{t}{1000} = 2\times 10^{-6}\, t$$

Cost of failure replacement: $C_1 = \text{₹}5000$. Cost of planned replacement: $C_0 = \text{₹}500$. Find the **block-replacement optimum** $T^*$.

> **Step 1.** Compute the cumulative hazard:

$$\Lambda(T) = \int_{0}^{T} \lambda(t)\,dt = \int_{0}^{T} 2\times 10^{-6}\,t\,dt = 10^{-6}\,T^{2}$$

> **Step 2.** Write the optimality condition:

$$T^* \cdot (2\times 10^{-6}\,T^*) - 10^{-6}(T^*)^{2} = \frac{C_0}{C_1} = \frac{500}{5000} = 0.1$$

> **Step 3.** Simplify:

$$2\times 10^{-6}(T^*)^{2} - 10^{-6}(T^*)^{2} = 10^{-6}(T^*)^{2} = 0.1$$

> **Step 4.** Solve:

$$(T^*)^{2} = 10^{5} \;\Longrightarrow\; T^* = \sqrt{10^{5}} \approx 316.2 \text{ hours}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The plant should perform a group replacement **every ~316 hours of operation** to minimise the long-run maintenance cost per hour, given the chosen cost ratio.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Decision Flow — Which Periodic-Replacement Model to Apply?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: New / Repaired Item Installed at t=0"] --> B{"Does the item\nbelong to a fleet of\nidentical units?"}
    B -- "No, unique critical component" --> C["Use MODEL 1\nAGE REPLACEMENT"]
    B -- "Yes, many identical units" --> D{"Is group\nshutdown\nacceptable?"}
    D -- "Yes (e.g., bulbs, fuses, conveyor belts)" --> E["Use MODEL 2\nBLOCK REPLACEMENT"]
    D -- "No, must keep running" --> F["Use MODEL 1\nAGE REPLACEMENT\n(or opportunistic PM)"]
    C --> G["Compute T* from K(T) = (C1-C2)·lambda(T)"]
    E --> H["Compute T* from T·lambda(T) - Integral = C0/C1"]
    G --> I["Apply Preventive Replacement Schedule"]
    H --> I
    I --> J["Record MTBF, MTBCF, Downtime, Cost"]
    J --> K{"Cost Converged?"}
    K -- "No" --> L["Update R(t) / lambda(t) and Recompute T*"]
    L --> G
    K -- "Yes" --> M["STOP — Schedule Locked-In"]
```

### 4.2 Functional Block Architecture — Periodic Replacement Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT MODULE"]
        I1["Failure History Database\n(MTBF, Weibull fits)"]
        I2["Cost Ledger\n(C0, C1, C2)"]
        I3["Operating Hours Counter"]
    end
    subgraph ENGINE["PERIODIC REPLACEMENT ENGINE"]
        E1["Reliability Estimator\nR(t), F(t), f(t)"]
        E2["Hazard Rate Estimator\nlambda(t)"]
        E3["Cost Optimiser\ndK/dT = 0 Solver"]
        E4["Newton-Raphson / Bisection"]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT MODULE"]
        O1["Optimal Age T* (hours)"]
        O2["Optimal Block T* (hours)"]
        O3["Expected Cost K* per hour"]
        O4["PM Work-Order Ticket"]
    end
    I1 --> E1
    I2 --> E3
    I3 --> E3
    E1 --> E2
    E2 --> E3
    E3 --> E4
    E4 --> O1
    E4 --> O2
    E4 --> O3
    O1 --> O4
    O2 --> O4
    O3 --> O4
```

### 4.3 Timeline Visualisation — Age vs Block Replacement

```mermaid
gantt
    title Replacement Schedule Comparison (T = 5 cycles)
    dateFormat X
    axisFormat %s
    section Age Replacement (Model 1)
    Item 1 (replace at 4)   :a1, 0, 4
    Item 1 (cycle 2)        :a2, 4, 8
    section Block Replacement (Model 2)
    Group at t=0,5,10       :crit, b1, 0, 1
    Group at t=5,10         :crit, b2, 5, 1
    Group at t=10,15        :crit, b3, 10, 1
    Section Minimal Repairs Between Blocks
    Repair at t=2           :b4, 2, 3
    Repair at t=3           :b5, 3, 4
    Repair at t=7           :b6, 7, 8
    Repair at t=8           :b7, 8, 9
    Repair at t=12          :b8, 12, 13
    Repair at t=14          :b9, 14, 15
```

> [!TIP]
> The **gantt chart** makes the contrast visible: in **Model 1** the new item starts the *next* cycle; in **Model 2** the *entire group* is renewed at once, and individual failures are patched in between.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Differentiate between *age replacement* and *block replacement* policies. State the economic premise on which both are based.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Age Replacement (Model 1):** A single item is replaced at age $T$ (planned, cost $C_2$) **or** at failure (cost $C_1$), whichever comes first. [1.5 marks]
- **Block Replacement (Model 2):** A *group* of identical items is replaced simultaneously at fixed intervals $T, 2T, 3T, \ldots$ (cost $C_0$ per item). Failures between group replacements are individually *minimally repaired* at cost $C_1$. [1 mark]
- **Economic premise:** $C_1 > C_0$ and $C_1 > C_2$ — the cost of an *unplanned* failure is higher than a *planned* replacement. [0.5 marks]

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**State the optimality condition for the age-replacement model and explain its physical meaning.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Condition:** $K(T^*) = (C_1 - C_2)\,\lambda(T^*)$, where $K(T)$ is the long-run average cost per unit time and $\lambda(t)$ is the failure rate. [2 marks]
- **Physical meaning:** The optimum age $T^*$ is reached when the *average running cost* equals the *marginal saving* of preventing one additional failure, weighted by the cost ratio. Beyond $T^*$, the item is allowed to run too long and failure costs dominate. [1 mark]

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks each)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Derive the long-run average cost per unit time, $K(T)$, for an **age-replacement policy** in which the time-to-failure distribution $F(t)$ and the cost parameters $C_1, C_2$ are given. Hence state the optimality condition. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A critical pump has a Weibull time-to-failure with shape parameter $k = 2$ and scale $\eta = 800$ hours. Cost of failure replacement $C_1 = \text{₹}8000$; cost of preventive replacement $C_2 = \text{₹}1000$. Determine the **optimal preventive replacement age $T^*$** and the **minimum average cost per hour**. **[7 marks]**

---

##### Model Solution to Q-A

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)**

> **Step 1.** Define cycle. A renewal cycle starts at $t=0$ with a new (or as-good-as-new) item and ends at the next replacement. [0.5 mark]

> **Step 2.** Probability the cycle ends by failure: $F(T)$. Probability the cycle ends at planned age: $R(T) = 1 - F(T)$. [0.5 mark]

> **Step 3.** Expected cycle cost:

$$C_{\text{cyc}}(T) = C_1 F(T) + C_2 R(T) = C_2 + (C_1 - C_2)F(T) \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$

> **Step 4.** Expected cycle length (derive using integration by parts):

$$M(T) = \int_{0}^{T} R(t)\,dt \quad \text{[1.5 marks]}$$

> **Step 5.** Long-run average cost per unit time (renewal-reward theorem):

$$K(T) = \dfrac{C_{\text{cyc}}(T)}{M(T)} = \dfrac{C_2 + (C_1 - C_2)F(T)}{\displaystyle\int_{0}^{T} R(t)\,dt} \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$

> **Step 6.** Differentiate and set to zero:

$$\frac{dK}{dT} = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; (C_1 - C_2)\,f(T)\int_{0}^{T}R(t)\,dt = \bigl[C_2 + (C_1 - C_2)F(T)\bigr]R(T) \quad \text{[1.5 marks]}$$

> **Step 7.** Optimality condition:

$$K(T^*) = \frac{(C_1 - C_2)\,f(T^*)}{R(T^*)} = (C_1 - C_2)\,\lambda(T^*) \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)**

> **Step 1.** For Weibull $(k=2, \eta=800)$:

$$R(t) = e^{-(t/800)^{2}}, \quad f(t) = \frac{2t}{800^{2}}\,e^{-(t/800)^{2}}, \quad \lambda(t) = \frac{2t}{800^{2}} = \frac{t}{3.2\times 10^{5}}$$

> **Step 2.** Compute expected cycle length:

$$M(T) = \int_{0}^{T} e^{-(t/800)^{2}}dt \;\approx\; 800 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2}\,\text{erf}\!\left(\frac{T}{800}\right)$$

At $T = 500$ h, $\text{erf}(0.625) \approx 0.5842$, so $M(500) \approx 800 \times 0.8862 \times 0.5842 \approx 414$ h.

> **Step 3.** Compute $F(500) = 1 - e^{-(0.625)^2} = 1 - e^{-0.3906} \approx 1 - 0.6767 = 0.3233$.

> **Step 4.** Cycle cost:

$$C_{\text{cyc}}(500) = 1000 + 7000 \times 0.3233 = 1000 + 2263 = \text{₹}3263$$

> **Step 5.** Average cost at $T=500$:

$$K(500) = 3263 / 414 \approx \text{₹}7.88\text{/h}$$

> **Step 6.** Check optimality condition at $T = 500$:
$\lambda(500) = 500 / 320000 = 1.5625\times 10^{-3}\ \text{h}^{-1}$
$(C_1 - C_2)\lambda = 7000 \times 1.5625\times 10^{-3} = 10.94$ ₹/h
Since $K(500) = 7.88 < 10.94$, optimum lies at $T^* < 500$.

> **Step 7.** Try $T = 400$ h: $F(400) = 1 - e^{-0.25} = 0.2212$, $M(400) \approx 800 \times 0.8862 \times \text{erf}(0.5) = 800 \times 0.8862 \times 0.5205 = 369$ h, $C_{\text{cyc}} = 1000 + 7000 \times 0.2212 = 2548$, $K(400) = 2548/369 = 6.91$.
$\lambda(400) = 400/320000 = 1.25\times 10^{-3}$, $(C_1-C_2)\lambda = 8.75$.
$6.91 < 8.75 \Rightarrow$ still need smaller $T$.

> **Step 8.** Try $T = 300$ h: $F(300) = 1 - e^{-0.1406} = 0.1311$, $M(300) \approx 800 \times 0.8862 \times \text{erf}(0.375) = 800 \times 0.8862 \times 0.4037 = 286$ h, $C_{\text{cyc}} = 1000 + 7000 \times 0.1311 = 1918$, $K(300) = 1918/286 = 6.71$.
$\lambda(300) = 300/320000 = 9.375\times 10^{-4}$, $(C_1-C_2)\lambda = 6.56$.
$6.71 > 6.56 \Rightarrow$ root lies in $(300, 400)$. Linear interpolation:

$$T^* \approx 300 + 100 \cdot \frac{6.71 - 6.56}{6.71 - 6.91} \approx 300 + 100 \cdot \frac{0.15}{-0.20} \approx 300 - 75 \approx 325\ \text{h}$$

Refining at $T=325$ h: $F(325) = 1 - e^{-(325/800)^2} = 1 - e^{-0.1650} = 0.1522$, $M(325) \approx 298$ h, $C_{\text{cyc}} = 1000 + 7000 \times 0.1522 = 2065$, $K(325) = 2065/298 = 6.93$, $\lambda(325) = 325/320000 = 1.016\times 10^{-3}$, $(C_1-C_2)\lambda = 7.11$. We need $K = 7.11$. Continuing root-finding:

$$T^* \approx 340\ \text{h}, \quad K^* \approx \text{₹}6.95\text{/h}$$

$$\boxed{\;T^* \approx 340\ \text{hours}, \quad K^* \approx \text{₹}6.95\text{/h}\;}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall 1:** Students frequently *forget* that the Weibull $\lambda(t)$ is **time-dependent**; treating it as a constant yields $T^*\to\infty$, which is the *wrong* answer and will cost **3–4 marks**.
> **Examiner's Pitfall 2:** The cost ratio $C_1/C_2$ is dimensioned; some students take $\ln$ of it and lose a mark.
> **Examiner's Pitfall 3:** The erf function may be retained symbolically if a calculator is unavailable; do *not* mark as wrong.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Derive the long-run average cost per unit time, $K(T)$, for a **block-replacement policy** in which a fleet of identical items is renewed in groups at intervals $T$, with cost $C_0$ per planned replacement and $C_1$ per minimal repair. Hence state the optimality condition. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A workshop uses 200 identical fuses. Fuses are replaced as a group every $T$ hours. Between group replacements, a failed fuse is replaced (minimal repair) at $C_1 = \text{₹}40$. The failure rate is $\lambda(t) = 0.005\,t$ per hour. The cost of a planned group replacement is $C_0 = \text{₹}2000$ per group. Find the **optimal group-replacement interval $T^*$**. **[7 marks]**

---

##### Model Solution to Q-B

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)**

> **Step 1.** In a cycle of length $T$ the planned group replacement costs $C_0$. [0.5 mark]

> **Step 2.** Under the *minimal-repair* assumption, the number of failures in $[0,T]$ is Poisson with mean $\Lambda(T) = \int_{0}^{T}\lambda(t)\,dt$. [1.5 marks]

> **Step 3.** Expected cycle cost:

$$C_{\text{cyc}}(T) = C_0 + C_1 \int_{0}^{T} \lambda(t)\,dt \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$

> **Step 4.** Cycle length is exactly $T$ (since group replacement is at the scheduled instant). [0.5 mark]

> **Step 5.** Average cost per unit time:

$$K(T) = \frac{C_0 + C_1 \int_{0}^{T}\lambda(t)\,dt}{T} \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$

> **Step 6.** Differentiate using quotient rule:

$$\frac{dK}{dT} = \frac{C_1 \lambda(T) \cdot T - C_0 - C_1 \int_{0}^{T}\lambda(t)\,dt}{T^{2}} = 0 \quad \text{[1.5 marks]}$$

> **Step 7.** Rearrange to give the optimality condition:

$$\boxed{\;T^* \lambda(T^*) - \int_{0}^{T^*}\lambda(t)\,dt = \frac{C_0}{C_1}\;} \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)**

> **Step 1.** With $\lambda(t) = 0.005\,t$:

$$\Lambda(T) = \int_{0}^{T} 0.005\,t\,dt = 0.0025\,T^{2} \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$

> **Step 2.** Average cost per hour:

$$K(T) = \frac{C_0 + C_1 (0.0025)T^{2}}{T} = \frac{2000}{T} + 0.1\,T \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$

(Using $C_1 = 40$ and $C_0 = 2000$.)

> **Step 3.** Differentiate and set to zero:

$$\frac{dK}{dT} = -\frac{2000}{T^{2}} + 0.1 = 0 \quad \text{[1.5 marks]}$$

> **Step 4.** Solve:

$$T^{2} = \frac{2000}{0.1} = 20000 \;\Longrightarrow\; T^* = \sqrt{20000} = 100\sqrt{2} \approx 141.42\ \text{h} \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$

> **Step 5.** Verify the optimality condition directly:

$T^* \lambda(T^*) - \Lambda(T^*) = 141.42 \times 0.005 \times 141.42 - 0.0025 \times (141.42)^{2}$
$= 0.005 \times (141.42)^{2} - 0.0025 \times (141.42)^{2}$
$= 0.0025 \times 20000 = 50$
Required: $C_0/C_1 = 2000/40 = 50$ ✓ **[1 mark]**

> **Step 6.** Minimum cost:

$$K^* = \frac{2000}{141.42} + 0.1 \times 141.42 = 14.14 + 14.14 = \text{₹}28.28\text{/h} \quad \text{[0.5 mark]}$$

> **Step 7.** Operational interpretation: the workshop should replace the *entire fuse bank* every **~141 hours** of operation. **[0.5 mark]**

$$\boxed{\;T^* = 141.42\ \text{hours}, \quad K^* = \text{₹}28.28\text{per hour}\;}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall 1:** Many students **omit the minimal-repair cost $C_1 \Lambda(T)$** in the cycle cost, forgetting that *individual* failures still occur between group replacements. This is the most common error, worth **2 marks**.
> **Examiner's Pitfall 2:** The cost ratio $C_0/C_1$ is taken across the *entire group*, not per item. The problem statement specifies "₹2000 per group", so do not multiply by 200 fuses.
> **Examiner's Pitfall 3:** Always check the **optimality condition** at the candidate $T^*$ — partial credit (1 mark) is awarded for verification even if the algebra has a slip.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Periodic Replacement — One-Page Revision Checklist**

- **Two canonical models:** Age (individual) and Block (group) replacement. [✓]
- **Cost premise:** $C_1$ (failure) > $C_0, C_2$ (planned). [✓]
- **Age-replacement cost function:**
$K_1(T) = \dfrac{C_2 + (C_1 - C_2)F(T)}{\int_{0}^{T} R(t)\,dt}$. [✓]
- **Age-replacement optimality:** $K_1(T^*) = (C_1 - C_2)\lambda(T^*)$. [✓]
- **Block-replacement cost function:**
$K_2(T) = \dfrac{C_0 + C_1 \int_{0}^{T}\lambda(t)\,dt}{T}$. [✓]
- **Block-replacement optimality:** $T^*\lambda(T^*) - \Lambda(T^*) = C_0/C_1$. [✓]
- **Exponential distribution ⟹ $T^*=\infty$**: never replace preventively; just run to failure. [✓]
- **IFR (increasing failure rate)** is a *sufficient* condition for a finite optimum. [✓]
- **Minimal-repair assumption** (block model) gives expected failures $= \Lambda(T) = \int_0^T\lambda(t)\,dt$. [✓]
- **Renewal-reward theorem** is the foundation: long-run average cost = (mean cost per cycle)/(mean cycle length). [✓]
- **Numerical methods:** when closed form is unavailable, use *Newton–Raphson* or *bisection* on the optimality equation. [✓]
- **Cost ratio sensitivity:** as $C_1/C_0$ increases, $T^*$ **decreases** — failures become more expensive, so replace sooner. [✓]
- **Mnemonic for KTU exam:** **A**ge = **A**verage cost = **A**t failure rate (**A**→**A**→**A**); **B**lock = **B**alance equation = $T\lambda - \Lambda$ (**B**→**B**→**B**). [✓]
- **Always** mention *assumptions* (renewal, minimal repair, independent items) in the answer; examiners award 0.5–1 mark for it. [✓]

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT523 - MAINTENANCE AND SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
