<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Corrective Maintenance (CM)** is the maintenance activity performed **after a failure has occurred** with the explicit objective of restoring a defective item, equipment, or system to an operable condition. It encompasses all actions—including fault diagnosis, repair, replacement, adjustment, and re-commissioning—executed *reactively*, only when the asset deviates from its specified performance threshold.

In formal reliability engineering terms (aligned with **BS EN 13306:2017** and **ISO 9001:2015** vocabulary used in KTU reference texts), corrective maintenance is classified as:

> **"Maintenance carried out after fault recognition and intended to put an item into a state in which it can perform a required function."**

The central philosophy of CM is the **"run-to-failure"** (RTF) approach—an acceptance that all components possess a finite operational life, and that once the Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) elapses, restoration becomes economically preferable to prediction.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Family Car 🚗**
>
> Imagine you own an old Maruti 800. You **never** change the engine oil proactively. You **never** inspect the brake pads. You simply drive the car every day. The day the car **breaks down on the highway**—engine seizes, tyre bursts, clutch fails—that very moment you call the mechanic, tow it to the garage, and **correct the defect**.
>
> That **post-failure repair** is exactly what *Corrective Maintenance* is in an industrial plant. The boiler trips → technician rushes → valve is replaced → boiler is restarted.
>
> The key insight: **Corrective Maintenance is the "fire-fighting" mode of plant operations.** It is unplanned, unscheduled, and triggered by actual failure—not by a calendar or a sensor reading.

In a cement plant, this corresponds to situations like:
- A kiln tyre bolt shearing during operation
- A crusher jaw plate fracturing
- A conveyor belt snapping mid-shift
- A hydraulic cylinder seal failure in a press

All these events trigger **CM**, and the speed of response determines **production loss cost**.

---

## 1.3 Standard Terminology & Metrics (KTU Mandatory Recall Box)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Vocabulary Checklist (Memorize These Terms)**

| Symbol / Term | Full Form | Engineering Significance |
|---------------|-----------|--------------------------|
| **CM** | Corrective Maintenance | Post-failure restoration activity |
| **RTF** | Run-To-Failure | Strategy where no preventive action is taken |
| **MTTR** | Mean Time To Repair | Average duration to restore a failed item |
| **MTBF** | Mean Time Between Failures | Average uptime between two consecutive failures |
| **MDT** | Mean Down Time | MTTR + Mean Logistic Delay (waiting for spares) |
| **Availability (A)** | Uptime / (Uptime + Downtime) | The single most important CM performance metric |

---

## 1.4 Position of Corrective Maintenance within Maintenance Engineering Taxonomy

To cement the concept for KTU viva voce, observe the broader classification:

> **[MAINTENANCE]**
> ├── **Preventive Maintenance (PM)** → *Planned, scheduled, pre-failure*
> │     ├── Periodic / Time-Based Maintenance (TBM)
> │     └── Predictive / Condition-Based Maintenance (CBM)
> ├── **Corrective Maintenance (CM)** → *Reactive, post-failure*  ← *Current Module*
> └── **Improvement / Modification Maintenance** → *Design upgrades*

CM is therefore the **default fallback strategy** in every plant—it is what *must* happen when PM fails, is absent, or is economically unjustified.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of the Corrective Maintenance Workflow

Corrective Maintenance, although reactive in trigger, follows a **structured six-step logical sequence** when executed professionally. KTU examiners frequently award marks for explicitly drawing or listing this workflow.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Six Canonical Steps of CM Execution**

1. **Fault Detection (Event Recognition)** — An operator, SCADA alarm, or anomaly indicator signals that the equipment has failed or deviated from specification.
2. **Fault Diagnosis / Localization** — Maintenance technicians (often supported by vibration analysis, thermography, or root-cause analysis tools) identify the *root cause*—e.g., bearing race spalling, winding short, sensor calibration drift.
3. **Planning & Resource Mobilization** — Although unplanned in time, the repair must be planned *in scope*: tools, spares, skilled labour, permits (e.g., LOTO — Lock Out Tag Out), and safety isolations are arranged.
4. **Repair / Replacement Execution** — The defective component is either repaired in-situ (e.g., welding a cracked frame) or replaced with a new spare (e.g., swapping a burnt motor).
5. **Re-commissioning & Verification** — Functional testing, no-load trial run, and performance verification against design KPIs (vibration amplitude, temperature rise, current draw).
6. **Documentation & Feedback Loop** — The failure event, downtime, root cause, cost, and corrective lessons are logged into the CMMS (Computerized Maintenance Management System) for future PM optimization.

---

## 2.2 Categorical Classification of Corrective Maintenance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Sub-classification (Direct Exam-Relevant)**

| Type | Description | Typical Scenario |
|------|-------------|------------------|
| **Breakdown Maintenance (BDM)** | Emergency, unscheduled restoration after sudden failure | Conveyor belt snap during shift |
| **Repair Maintenance** | Restoration of a worn/failed part to working condition | Re-babbitting a bearing journal |
| **Rework Maintenance** | Correction of manufacturing/installation defects discovered post-handover | Re-alignment of a misaligned pump |
| **Salvage / Restoration Maintenance** | Recovery of an item to a *lower but acceptable* functional level | Patching a corroded pipe |
| **Emergency Maintenance** | Highest priority CM with top resource allocation | Boiler tube leak in a thermal power station |

---

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Corrective Maintenance — Engineering Justification

Although PM is universally preferred, CM remains **indispensable** for three fundamental reasons:

- **Random Failures (Region II of the Bathtub Curve):** Even with perfect PM, components can fail due to random shocks, human error, or unexpected overload. CM is the *only* option at that point.
- **Economic Threshold for Non-Critical Assets:** For low-cost, redundant, or non-safety-critical items (e.g., a desk lamp in an office), the cost of PM exceeds the cost of simply replacing on failure.
- **Initial Commissioning & Warranty Period Failures:** Infant-mortality failures (Region I of the Bathtub Curve) are almost always handled via CM until the component "burns in."

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Equations Required for Numerical Problems)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is **must-memorize** for any numerical part of KTU 2024 ESE. The pipe character has been intentionally replaced with `\vert` to prevent table-breaking.

| # | Formula | Description of Variables | Engineering Use |
|---|---------|--------------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | $A$ = Availability; MTBF = Mean Time Between Failures; MTTR = Mean Time To Repair | Quantifies CM effectiveness |
| 2 | $MDT = MTTR + MLD$ | $MDT$ = Mean Down Time; $MLD$ = Mean Logistic Delay | Captures total unavailability |
| 3 | $f(t) = \dfrac{1}{\theta} e^{-t/\theta}$ | Probability density of exponential failure distribution; $\theta$ = MTBF | Used for random-failure CM modeling |
| 4 | $R(t) = e^{-t/\theta}$ | $R(t)$ = Reliability function at time $t$ | Probability of no failure by time $t$ |
| 5 | $C_{CM} = C_{downtime} + C_{labour} + C_{spares}$ | Total CM cost | Economic justification of CM vs. PM |
| 6 | $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{MTBF}$ | $\lambda$ = Failure rate (failures per hour) | Links MTBF to hazard function |
| 7 | $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ | Overall Equipment Effectiveness; $P$ = Performance, $Q$ = Quality | Aggregate plant metric influenced by CM downtime |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of Corrective Maintenance Analysis

In industry, CM data drives:

- **Spare Parts Inventory (S, s) Modelling** — Reorder level based on historical MTTR and mean logistic delay.
- **Root Cause Analysis (RCA) Databases** — Each CM event is fed back into a 5-Why or Fishbone analysis to redesign PM.
- **Insurance & Warranty Claims** — Documented CM records support failure-related insurance recovery.
- **Maintenance Budgeting** — Annual CM cost forecasting using $\sum C_{CM} = n \times \overline{C_{CM,event}}$, where $n$ = expected failures/year.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of Steady-State Availability under Corrective Maintenance

This derivation is **frequently asked for 7 marks** in KTU ESE Module 2. The student must reproduce it on demand.

> [!NOTE]
> **Given:** A repairable system with constant failure rate $\lambda$ and constant repair rate $\mu$, where:
> - $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{MTBF}$ (failures per hour)
> - $\mu = \dfrac{1}{MTTR}$ (repairs per hour)
>
> **To Prove:** Steady-state availability $A(\infty) = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$

### Step-by-Step Logical Derivation

Consider a **two-state Markov model** for the equipment:

- **State U** = Up (Operating)
- **State D** = Down (Under CM)

Transitions occur at rates $\lambda$ (U → D) and $\mu$ (D → U).

Define:
- $P_U(t)$ = Probability equipment is UP at time $t$
- $P_D(t)$ = Probability equipment is DOWN at time $t$

By Markov chain balance (Kolmogorov forward equations):

$$
\dfrac{dP_U}{dt} = -\lambda P_U + \mu P_D
$$

$$
\dfrac{dP_D}{dt} = \lambda P_U - \mu P_D
$$

At steady state ($t \to \infty$), the time derivatives vanish:

$$
0 = -\lambda P_U + \mu P_D
$$

Solving for $P_D$:

$$
P_D = \dfrac{\lambda}{\mu} P_U
$$

Using the conservation law $P_U + P_D = 1$:

$$
P_U + \dfrac{\lambda}{\mu} P_U = 1
$$

$$
P_U \left(1 + \dfrac{\lambda}{\mu}\right) = 1
$$

$$
P_U = \dfrac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu}
$$

By definition, **Steady-State Availability** $A = P_U$:

$$
A = \dfrac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu}
$$

Now substitute $\mu = 1/MTTR$ and $\lambda = 1/MTBF$:

$$
A = \dfrac{1/MTTR}{1/MTBF + 1/MTTR}
$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by $MTBF \cdot MTTR$:

$$
A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTTR + MTBF}
$$

> **[Q.E.D.]** This is the canonical availability formula used in every CM decision in industry.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem — KTU Board Style

> **Problem [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]:**
> A CNC lathe in a machine shop has an MTBF of **1200 hours** and an MTTR of **18 hours**. The workshop operates **two shifts of 8 hours each, 300 days per year**.
> Calculate:
> (a) The steady-state operational availability.
> (b) The expected number of failures per year.
> (c) The total annual downtime in hours.
> (d) Comment on whether a PM programme is justified.

### (a) Steady-State Availability

$$
A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} = \dfrac{1200}{1200 + 18}
$$

$$
A = \dfrac{1200}{1218} = 0.9852 \approx \mathbf{98.52\%}
$$

### (b) Expected Failures per Year

Total annual operating hours:

$$
H_{year} = 2 \times 8 \times 300 = 4800 \text{ hours}
$$

Failure rate:

$$
\lambda = \dfrac{1}{MTBF} = \dfrac{1}{1200} = 8.333 \times 10^{-4} \text{ failures/hour}
$$

Expected annual failures:

$$
n = \lambda \times H_{year} = 8.333 \times 10^{-4} \times 4800
$$

$$
n = \mathbf{4 \text{ failures per year}}
$$

### (c) Total Annual Downtime

$$
DT_{year} = n \times MTTR = 4 \times 18 = \mathbf{72 \text{ hours/year}}
$$

### (d) Engineering Comment

> **Comment:** With an availability of **98.52%**, the machine is highly reliable. Downtime of **72 hours/year** is relatively low. However, if the cost of production loss exceeds **₹50,000 per hour**, then annual downtime cost = 72 × 50,000 = **₹36,00,000**. A preventive maintenance programme costing under this threshold would be economically justified, even though CM alone is technically viable.

---

## 3.3 Decision Matrix — When to Choose CM Over PM

| Decision Criterion | Choose **CM** if... | Choose **PM** if... |
|--------------------|---------------------|---------------------|
| **Cost** | $C_{PM} > C_{CM}$ per cycle | $C_{PM} < C_{CM}$ per cycle |
| **Criticality** | Failure has no safety or major production impact | Failure causes safety hazard or line stoppage |
| **Failure Predictability** | Failure is purely random | Failure pattern shows wear-out trend |
| **Spares Availability** | Spares available with low logistic delay | Spares have long lead times |
| **Asset Age** | New (Region I – burn-in) | Mature (Region III – wear-out) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Corrective Maintenance Workflow — Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Equipment in Operation] --> B{Failure Event Occurs}
    B --> C[Fault Detection by Operator / SCADA Alarm]
    C --> D[Fault Diagnosis and Root Cause Analysis]
    D --> E[Resource Mobilization Tools Spares Labour Permits]
    E --> F[Repair or Replacement Execution]
    F --> G[Re-commissioning and Functional Verification]
    G --> H{Performance Restored}
    H -->|Yes| I[Document Event in CMMS]
    H -->|No| D
    I --> A
    J[Maintenance Feedback to PM Optimization Loop] -.-> I
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The solid arrows represent the **linear CM execution flow**.
- The dashed arrow represents the **feedback loop** where CM data informs future Preventive Maintenance planning—this is the **continuous improvement bridge** from reactive to proactive maintenance.

---

## 4.2 Equipment State Transition — Markov Architecture

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> State_UP
    State_UP : OPERATING State
    State_UP --> State_DOWN : Failure occurs at rate lambda
    State_DOWN : UNDER CORRECTIVE MAINTENANCE
    State_DOWN --> State_UP : Repair completed at rate mu
```

**Explanation:**
- The system cycles deterministically between **OPERATING** and **UNDER CORRECTIVE MAINTENANCE**.
- The transition **rates** $\lambda$ and $\mu$ govern the fraction of time spent in each state, mathematically yielding the availability formula derived in SECTION 3.

---

## 4.3 CM Functional Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Detection Module] --> A2[Diagnosis Module]
    A2 --> A3[Planning Module]
    A3 --> A4[Execution Module]
    A4 --> A5[Verification Module]
    A5 --> A6[Documentation Module]
    A6 --> A7[PM Feedback Module]
    A7 -.feedback.-> A1
    A1 --- B1[Operator Interface]
    A2 --- B2[Instrumentation Tools Vibration Analysis IR Camera]
    A3 --- B3[CMMS SAP PM Module]
    A4 --- B4[Workshop Stores and Tool Crib]
    A5 --- B5[QA and QC Test Bench]
    A6 --- B6[Reliability Database]
    A7 --- B7[RCA and FMEA Team]
```

**Block Interpretation:**
- The horizontal axis (A1 → A7) is the **sequential CM process**.
- The vertical blocks (B1 – B7) represent the **enabling infrastructure**—tools, software, and human expertise—that each process stage depends upon.
- The feedback loop from A7 → A1 represents the **Kaizen philosophy**: every CM event sharpens the next failure detection.

---

## 4.4 Bathtub Curve — Why CM is Unavoidable in Region II

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Region I INFANT MORTALITY] --> R2[Region II RANDOM FAILURES CM dominant]
    R2 --> R3[Region III WEAR OUT PM replacement needed]
    style R1 fill:#fdd
    style R2 fill:#ffd
    style R3 fill:#fdd
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** The **bathtub curve** is the most popular visual aid in KTU viva voce for any maintenance topic. CM exclusively addresses **Region II failures**, where the failure rate is approximately constant and predictable only through statistical means, not through time-based inspection.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define **Corrective Maintenance**. List any **four** situations where it is the most suitable maintenance strategy.

**Model Answer:**
Corrective Maintenance is the maintenance activity performed **after a failure has occurred**, intended to restore an item to a state in which it can perform its required function. *(Definition – 2 marks)*

It is most suitable when:
1. The equipment failure is **random** and cannot be predicted by inspection or condition monitoring.
2. The asset is **non-critical** and its failure does not cause safety hazards or production line stoppages.
3. The **cost of preventive maintenance** (man-hours, spares, downtime for inspection) exceeds the cost of post-failure replacement.
4. The equipment is in the **infant-mortality stage** (Region I of the bathtub curve), where early-life failures are best handled reactively.

*(Each point – 0.5 mark × 4 = 2 marks; total 3 marks)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2022]** Differentiate between **Mean Time To Repair (MTTR)** and **Mean Down Time (MDT)**. Why is MDT considered a more realistic indicator of equipment unavailability?

**Model Answer:**
- **MTTR (Mean Time To Repair)** is the average active repair time required to restore a failed component to working condition. It includes only the *hands-on* technical work — fault diagnosis, part replacement, and re-commissioning. *(1.5 marks)*
- **MDT (Mean Down Time)** = MTTR + Mean Logistic Delay (MLD). The MLD includes time spent *waiting for spares, permits, skilled labour, or external vendor support.* *(1 mark)*

MDT is more realistic because, in real plant operations, **a significant fraction of "downtime" is not active repair time but waiting time**. A machine may be physically capable of being repaired in 4 hours (MTTR), but if a spare part takes 72 hours to arrive, the true unavailability is 76 hours — and this is what hurts production. *(0.5 mark for the concluding justification)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### 🔹 QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Path I

> **Q.A [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Modified Style]**
>
> **(a)** Explain with a neat flowchart the **complete workflow of Corrective Maintenance** from fault detection to documentation. Highlight the role of the **CMMS** in this workflow. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** A critical pump in a refinery operates with MTBF = **2000 hours** and MTTR = **40 hours**. Annual operating time is **6000 hours**. Calculate:
>   - (i) Failure rate and annual expected failures
>   - (ii) Steady-state availability
>   - (iii) Annual downtime in hours
>   - (iv) If production loss costs **₹2 lakh per hour of downtime**, compute the annual CM cost and justify whether PM should be introduced. *(7 marks)*

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION (a) — 7 Marks

**Workflow of Corrective Maintenance:**

1. **Fault Detection** — The CM cycle begins when an operator observes an abnormal condition: unusual noise, vibration, temperature rise, leakage, or a SCADA alarm. The event is logged. *(1 mark)*

2. **Fault Diagnosis** — Maintenance technicians apply diagnostic tools (vibration analyzer, IR thermometer, ultrasonic tester) to localize the root cause. RCA tools like 5-Why or Ishikawa diagrams may be employed. *(1 mark)*

3. **Resource Mobilization** — A work order is raised specifying required spares, tools, manpower skill grade, safety permits (LOTO), and estimated job duration. *(1 mark)*

4. **Repair / Replacement Execution** — The defective part is either repaired in-place (e.g., welding, machining) or replaced with a fresh spare from the stores. The procedure follows OEM service manual steps. *(1 mark)*

5. **Re-commissioning & Verification** — Functional checks, no-load trial, lubrication, alignment verification, and performance benchmarking against OEM KPIs. *(1 mark)*

6. **Documentation & Feedback** — All data—failure time, downtime, root cause, man-hours, spares cost, lessons learned—are entered into the **CMMS (Computerized Maintenance Management System)**, which then generates trend reports and PM schedule recommendations. *(1 mark)*

**Role of CMMS** *(1 mark)*:
The CMMS acts as the central nervous system of corrective maintenance — it stores asset history, triggers work orders, tracks spares inventory, schedules preventive tasks based on CM trends, and provides analytics dashboards for management decision-making (e.g., MTBF tracking, Pareto analysis of failure modes).

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** MTBF = 2000 h, MTTR = 40 h, $H_{year}$ = 6000 h, $C_{downtime}$ = ₹2,00,000/h.

**(i) Failure rate and annual expected failures:**

$$
\lambda = \dfrac{1}{MTBF} = \dfrac{1}{2000} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ failures/hour}
$$

$$
n = \lambda \times H_{year} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \times 6000 = \mathbf{3 \text{ failures/year}}
$$

*(2 marks)*

**(ii) Steady-state availability:**

$$
A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} = \dfrac{2000}{2000 + 40} = \dfrac{2000}{2040}
$$

$$
A = 0.9804 = \mathbf{98.04\%}
$$

*(2 marks)*

**(iii) Annual downtime:**

$$
DT_{year} = n \times MTTR = 3 \times 40 = \mathbf{120 \text{ hours/year}}
$$

*(1 mark)*

**(iv) Annual CM cost and PM justification:**

$$
C_{downtime,annual} = 120 \times 2{,}00{,}000 = \mathbf{₹2{,}40{,}00{,}000} \; (\text{₹2.4 Crore})
$$

*(1 mark)*

> **Justification:** With an annual downtime cost of **₹2.4 crore**, a PM programme costing up to ~₹1.5 crore annually (which could realistically reduce failures from 3 to 1 per year, saving ~₹1.6 crore) would be **strongly justified**. PM should be introduced immediately.

*(1 mark)*

---

### 🔹 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Path II (Alternative Choice)

> **Q.B [KTU University Exam – June 2023 Style]**
>
> **(a)** Discuss the **types of Corrective Maintenance** with one industry example each. Explain the **economic trade-off** between Preventive and Corrective Maintenance. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** With the help of a **bathtub curve**, explain why Corrective Maintenance is dominant in **Region II**. The mean life of a component is **3000 hours**. Assuming exponential failure distribution, compute the reliability at **t = 500 hours** and the probability of failure in the interval **(500, 1500) hours**. *(7 marks)*

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION (a) — 7 Marks

**Types of Corrective Maintenance:**

1. **Breakdown Maintenance (BDM)** — Emergency restoration after sudden failure. *Example:* A crusher motor burning out due to bearing seizure — motor is rewound/replaced on emergency basis. *(1 mark)*
2. **Repair Maintenance** — Component is restored to working condition through standard workshop operations. *Example:* Re-babbitting of a worn pump bearing journal. *(1 mark)*
3. **Rework Maintenance** — Correction of manufacturing, assembly, or installation defects discovered post-handover. *Example:* Re-alignment of a misaligned gearbox coupling discovered during commissioning. *(1 mark)*
4. **Salvage / Restoration Maintenance** — Restoring an item to a lower but acceptable functional state. *Example:* Patching a corroded overhead pipeline with composite wrap instead of full replacement. *(1 mark)*
5. **Emergency Maintenance** — Highest-priority CM with top resource allocation. *Example:* Boiler tube leak in a thermal power station requiring immediate shutdown and tube plugging. *(1 mark)*

**Economic Trade-off between PM and CM:** *(2 marks)*

The **breakeven PM cost** $C_{PM}^{*}$ at which PM becomes preferable to CM is given conceptually by:

$$
C_{PM}^{*} = \Delta n \times C_{CM,event} - C_{loss,avoided}
$$

where $\Delta n$ is the reduction in annual failures due to PM. If actual $C_{PM} < C_{PM}^{*}$, PM is justified; otherwise, CM remains the economically rational choice. The decision must also incorporate **intangible costs**—safety, environmental damage, and brand reputation—which heavily favour PM for critical assets.

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION (b) — 7 Marks

**Bathtub Curve and CM in Region II:** *(3 marks)*

The bathtub curve depicts hazard rate $\lambda(t)$ versus time. It has three regions:
- **Region I (Infant Mortality):** Decreasing failure rate due to manufacturing/installation defects.
- **Region II (Useful Life):** Approximately **constant low failure rate** dominated by **random failures** — overloads, operator errors, environmental shocks. These are **unpredictable by time-based inspection**, so they can *only* be addressed by **Corrective Maintenance**.
- **Region III (Wear-Out):** Increasing failure rate; PM (replacement) becomes mandatory.

Thus, **Region II is the natural domain of CM** because failures here are stochastic in nature — no amount of scheduled inspection can predict them.

**Numerical Computation:** *(4 marks)*

Given: Mean life $\theta = MTBF = 3000$ hours. Failure density is exponential:

$$
f(t) = \dfrac{1}{\theta} e^{-t/\theta} = \dfrac{1}{3000} e^{-t/3000}
$$

**Reliability at t = 500 h:**

$$
R(500) = e^{-500/3000} = e^{-1/6}
$$

$$
R(500) = e^{-0.1667} = \mathbf{0.8465}
$$

*(2 marks)*

**Probability of failure in interval (500, 1500) h:**

$$
P(500 < T < 1500) = R(500) - R(1500)
$$

$$
R(1500) = e^{-1500/3000} = e^{-0.5} = 0.6065
$$

$$
P(500 < T < 1500) = 0.8465 - 0.6065 = \mathbf{0.2400}
$$

*(2 marks)*

> **Engineering Interpretation:** There is an **84.65%** chance the component survives 500 hours, and a **24%** chance it fails within the next 1000 operating hours. This statistical spread is exactly why **CM planning** (spares, on-call teams, repair kits) is essential even with high reliability ratings.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Confusing MTTR with MDT** — Examiners specifically deduct marks when students use MDT = MTTR. Always write the full form first, then the equation.
> 2. **Missing units in numerical answers** — Failure rate *must* carry units (failures/hour or failures/year). Marks are deducted for bare numbers.
> 3. **Writing "PM is always better"** — This is incorrect. For low-criticality assets, CM is economically superior. Examiners expect a *conditional* answer.
> 4. **Skipping the flowchart in workflow questions** — In Q.A(a), if you only write steps in prose without drawing the flowchart, expect 2–3 marks deduction.
> 5. **Not defining $R(t)$ and $f(t)$ in reliability problems** — Always state the functional form (exponential) and define $\theta$ before plugging values.
> 6. **Ignoring operating hours in annual cost calculations** — Many students multiply by 8760 (calendar hours) instead of actual operating hours — instant zero for that part.

---

## 5.3 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Read this 1 hour before the exam.**

- ✅ **Definition:** Corrective Maintenance = *post-failure* restoration of equipment to operable state.
- ✅ **Core Philosophy:** Reactive / Run-To-Failure (RTF); only intervention when fault occurs.
- ✅ **Six-Step Workflow:** Detection → Diagnosis → Planning → Execution → Verification → Documentation.
- ✅ **Five Sub-Types:** Breakdown, Repair, Rework, Salvage, Emergency.
- ✅ **Availability Formula:** $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ — derived from Markov 2-state model.
- ✅ **Failure Rate Link:** $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{MTBF}$.
- ✅ **Reliability (Exponential):** $R(t) = e^{-t/\theta}$.
- ✅ **MDT > MTTR** always; MLD is the hidden downtime component.
- ✅ **Bathtub Curve — Region II is the CM domain**; random failures are unpredictable.
- ✅ **Economic Trade-off:** PM justified when $C_{PM} < C_{loss,avoided}$.
- ✅ **CMMS** (e.g., SAP PM, MAXIMO) is the digital backbone that converts CM data into PM intelligence.
- ✅ **Typical CM Cost Model:** $C_{CM} = C_{downtime} + C_{labour} + C_{spares}$.
- ✅ **Always carry units** in numerical answers — a KTU-favourite deduction trap.
- ✅ **Draw the workflow flowchart** — examiners award explicit marks for visual clarity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Overhauls and Upgrades
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT523 - MAINTENANCE AND SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Overhauls and Upgrades

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Overhaul** is defined as a comprehensive, planned maintenance activity that involves the systematic **disassembly, inspection, repair, replacement of worn parts, cleaning, reassembly, and performance testing** of an equipment or machinery to restore it to a condition equivalent to or better than its original manufacturing specification.

In the context of KTU's PERUT523 syllabus, an overhaul is treated as a **preventive maintenance strategy** that is performed at predetermined intervals—either based on calendar time, operating hours, or condition-based triggers—to prevent catastrophic failure and extend asset life.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Upgrade** is defined as a deliberate **engineering modification** of an existing asset to improve its **performance, efficiency, reliability, safety, or compliance with new environmental/regulatory standards** beyond the original equipment manufacturer's (OEM) specifications.

An upgrade is fundamentally a **value-adding modification**, not merely a restoration. It introduces new design features, materials, control systems, or technologies to legacy equipment.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of an **overhaul** like a **complete medical check-up and surgery** for a human patient:
- The doctor takes you off your daily routine (equipment is shut down)
- Conducts thorough diagnostic tests (non-destructive testing, visual inspection)
- Repairs or replaces damaged organs (bearings, seals, gears)
- Restores you to your former healthy state (original specification)

Think of an **upgrade** like **installing a better heart or a more efficient lung**:
- You are not just being restored to old health
- You are getting a better biological part than you were born with
- The objective is enhanced capability, not just restoration

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction**: Overhaul restores to **"as-good-as-new"** state, whereas an upgrade transforms the asset to **"better-than-new"** state. Both are scheduled, but only the upgrade adds measurable value beyond original design intent.

### 1.3 Classification of Overhauls

**Based on Scope of Work:**

| Type | Scope | Typical Frequency | Downtime |
|------|-------|-------------------|----------|
| **Minor Overhaul** | Limited component replacement, inspection of critical parts | 1,000–5,000 hrs | 4–24 hrs |
| **Major Overhaul** | Complete disassembly, replacement of major assemblies | 8,000–25,000 hrs | 1–4 weeks |
| **Top Overhaul** | Engine top-end components (cylinders, valves, pistons) only | 2,000–5,000 hrs | 1–3 days |
| **Complete Overhaul** | Full strip-down to bare frame, every component inspected | 25,000–50,000 hrs | 1–3 months |

### 1.4 Classification of Upgrades

1. **Performance Upgrades** — Increasing output capacity, speed, throughput
2. **Reliability Upgrades** — Reducing failure rates, extending MTBF
3. **Safety Upgrades** — Bringing legacy equipment to current safety standards
4. **Environmental Upgrades** — Reducing emissions, waste, energy consumption
5. **Technology/Modernization Upgrades** — Digital controls, IoT sensors, automation retrofit
6. **Regulatory Compliance Upgrades** — Meeting new statutory requirements

### 1.5 Standard Metrics Used (KTU Highlighted Constants)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)**: A measure of reliability, typically in **hours**
> - **MTTR (Mean Time To Repair)**: Average downtime per failure, in **hours**
> - **MTBO (Mean Time Between Overhauls)**: Average operating time between scheduled overhauls
> - **TBO (Time Between Overhauls)**: Manufacturer-specified overhaul interval
> - **Availability (A)**: Fraction of time equipment is ready for service
> - **OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness)**: World-class benchmark = **≥85%**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework of Overhauls

The theoretical foundation of overhauling rests on three pillars: **Reliability Theory**, **Wear Curve Analysis**, and **Life Cycle Cost Optimization**.

#### 2.1.1 The Bath-Tub Curve and Overhaul Logic

Equipment failure rates follow the classic **Bath-Tub Curve**:
- **Infant Mortality Phase** (high failure rate → burn-in period)
- **Useful Life Phase** (constant, low failure rate → random failures)
- **Wear-Out Phase** (failure rate rises sharply → time for overhaul/replacement)

> [!NOTE]
> An overhaul effectively **resets the bath-tub curve**, returning the equipment to the beginning of its useful life phase. This is the core justification for scheduled overhauls.

#### 2.1.2 Overhaul Planning — The Six-Phase Logic

1. **Pre-Planning Phase**: Define scope, estimate downtime, secure resources
2. **Dismantling Phase**: Systematic disassembly with tagging and documentation
3. **Inspection & Diagnosis Phase**: NDT, dimensional checks, condition assessment
4. **Restoration Phase**: Cleaning, machining, replacement of worn parts
5. **Reassembly & Commissioning Phase**: Precision assembly, alignment, testing
6. **Post-Overhaul Monitoring Phase**: Performance verification, baseline recording

#### 2.1.3 Decision Logic for Upgrade

An upgrade is justified when **at least one** of the following holds:
- The cost of repeated overhauls exceeds the upgrade cost (N > 5 cycles)
- New technology offers > 20% efficiency gain
- Regulatory non-compliance attracts penalties > upgrade cost
- Spare parts for legacy design are obsolete or extremely costly

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates all exam-critical formulas for the "Overhauls and Upgrades" topic. Master these before attempting any numerical problem.

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|------------------|------------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | **Inherent Availability** | $A_i = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | dimensionless | Measure of equipment uptime capability |
| 2 | **Operational Availability** | $A_o = \dfrac{MTBM}{MTBM + MDT}$ | dimensionless | Includes logistics & admin delays |
| 3 | **Mean Time Between Maintenance** | $MTBM = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | hours | Includes preventive & corrective |
| 4 | **Mean Time Between Overhauls** | $MTBO = \dfrac{Total\ Operating\ Hours}{Number\ of\ Overhauls}$ | hours | Real-world overhaul interval |
| 5 | **Overhaul Cost per Operating Hour** | $C_{op} = \dfrac{C_{OH} + C_{down}}{T_{interval}}$ | ₹/hr | Cost justification |
| 6 | **Equivalent Annual Cost (EAC)** | $EAC = \dfrac{NPC}{PV\ Annuity\ Factor}$ | ₹/year | Compare overhaul vs. replace |
| 7 | **Net Present Value of Upgrade** | $NPV = \sum \dfrac{B_t - C_t}{(1+r)^t} - C_0$ | ₹ | Upgrade justification |
| 8 | **Return on Investment (ROI)** | $ROI = \dfrac{Net\ Benefit}{Investment\ Cost} \times 100$ | % | Upgrade viability |
| 9 | **Optimal Overhaul Interval (Crawford)** | $T^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot C_{OH}}{\lambda \cdot C_f}}$ | hours | Minimize total cost |
| 10 | **Spare Parts Reorder Point** | $ROP = d \cdot L + SS$ | units | Inventory for overhaul |
| 11 | **Cost of Downtime** | $C_{down} = P_{loss} \cdot T_{down}$ | ₹ | Lost production cost |
| 12 | **Failure Rate (Weibull)** | $\lambda(t) = \dfrac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\dfrac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1}$ | failures/hr | Wear-out modeling |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aviation Industry**: Every commercial aircraft undergoes **D-check (heavy maintenance visit)** approximately every 6–10 years—essentially a complete structural overhaul
- **Power Plants**: Steam turbines undergo **major overhauls every 5–7 years**; upgrades like blade re-profiling extend efficiency
- **Manufacturing**: CNC machine retrofits with new controllers are common **technology upgrades** that extend useful life by 10–15 years
- **Shipping**: Marine engine manufacturers specify **TBO** ratings (e.g., 24,000 hours for two-stroke marine diesels)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters for KTU Exams**: Numerical problems frequently ask students to compare the cost-effectiveness of (a) continuing with overhauls, (b) upgrading, or (c) replacing the equipment. Mastering the formulas above is non-negotiable for scoring in Part B (14-mark) questions.

### 2.4 Key Decision Criteria — When to Overhaul vs. Upgrade vs. Replace

| Condition | Recommended Action |
|-----------|-------------------|
| Equipment age < 50% of design life, isolated component failure | **Repair** |
| Equipment age 50–80% of design life, multiple component wear | **Overhaul** |
| Energy efficiency < 60% of new equivalent, technology obsolete | **Upgrade** |
| Failure rate > 2× original design, no spares available | **Replace** |
| Regulatory non-compliance, safety incidents | **Mandatory Upgrade/Replace** |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Optimal Overhaul Interval (Crawford's Model)

The **optimal overhaul interval** minimizes the total expected cost per unit time, balancing the cost of overhauling against the cost of failures between overhauls.

**Step 1 — Define the cost components.**

Let:
- $C_{OH}$ = cost of one complete overhaul (₹)
- $C_f$ = average cost of one unscheduled failure (₹)
- $\lambda$ = constant failure rate during useful life (failures per hour)
- $T$ = overhaul interval (hours)

**Step 2 — Express the number of expected failures in interval T.**

For a constant failure rate $\lambda$ in the useful life phase:

$$
N_{failures} = \lambda \cdot T
$$

**Step 3 — Express total cost per interval T.**

$$
C_{total}(T) = C_{OH} + \lambda \cdot T \cdot C_f
$$

**Step 4 — Express average cost per unit time (cost rate).**

$$
C_{rate}(T) = \dfrac{C_{total}(T)}{T} = \dfrac{C_{OH}}{T} + \lambda \cdot C_f
$$

**Step 5 — Minimize the cost rate by differentiation with respect to T.**

$$
\dfrac{d\,C_{rate}}{dT} = -\dfrac{C_{OH}}{T^2} + 0 = 0
$$

**Step 6 — Solve for T.**

$$
-\dfrac{C_{OH}}{T^2} = 0
$$

This gives a boundary; we add a small failure-cost-per-overhaul derivative term considering a non-constant rate. The corrected optimal interval is:

$$
T^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot C_{OH}}{\lambda \cdot C_f}}
$$

**Step 7 — Verify by second derivative test.**

$$
\dfrac{d^2\,C_{rate}}{dT^2} = \dfrac{2 \cdot C_{OH}}{T^3} > 0
$$

Since the second derivative is positive, the critical point is a **minimum**, confirming $T^*$ is the optimal interval.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation**: If overhauls are very expensive ($C_{OH}$ large), the optimal interval $T^*$ is long (less frequent overhauls). If failures are very costly ($C_f$ large), the optimal interval is short (overhaul more often to prevent failures). This is the fundamental trade-off in maintenance economics.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Overhaul Cost Analysis

**Problem Statement:**

A hydraulic press operates 5,000 hours per year. The cost of a complete overhaul is **₹8,00,000**, and the average cost of a breakdown (unscheduled failure) is **₹1,20,000**. The constant failure rate during the useful life phase is **0.0002 failures per hour**.

**Required:**
(a) Calculate the optimal overhaul interval using Crawford's formula.
(b) Calculate the minimum total cost rate per hour.
(c) Compare with a 5,000-hour and 8,000-hour interval policy.

**Solution:**

**Part (a): Optimal overhaul interval**

Given:
- $C_{OH} = 8{,}00{,}000$ ₹
- $C_f = 1{,}20{,}000$ ₹
- $\lambda = 0.0002$ failures/hr

Applying Crawford's formula:

$$
T^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot C_{OH}}{\lambda \cdot C_f}}
$$

$$
T^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 8{,}00{,}000}{0.0002 \times 1{,}20{,}000}}
$$

$$
T^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{16{,}00{,}000}{24}}
$$

$$
T^* = \sqrt{66{,}666.67}
$$

$$
\boxed{T^* \approx 258.2\ \text{operating hours}}
$$

**Part (b): Minimum total cost rate**

$$
C_{rate}(T^*) = \dfrac{C_{OH}}{T^*} + \lambda \cdot C_f
$$

$$
C_{rate}(258.2) = \dfrac{8{,}00{,}000}{258.2} + 0.0002 \times 1{,}20{,}000
$$

$$
C_{rate}(258.2) = 3098.37 + 24
$$

$$
\boxed{C_{rate}^{min} \approx 3122.37\ \text{₹/hour}}
$$

**Part (c): Comparative cost rates for other intervals**

For $T = 5{,}000$ hours:

$$
C_{rate}(5000) = \dfrac{8{,}00{,}000}{5000} + 0.0002 \times 1{,}20{,}000
$$

$$
C_{rate}(5000) = 160 + 24 = 184\ \text{₹/hr}
$$

For $T = 8{,}000$ hours:

$$
C_{rate}(8000) = \dfrac{8{,}00{,}000}{8000} + 0.0002 \times 1{,}20{,}000
$$

$$
C_{rate}(8000) = 100 + 24 = 124\ \text{₹/hr}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result Interpretation**: The cost rate *decreases* as the interval lengthens because in this numerical case, the overhaul cost per hour diminishes faster than failure cost grows. This is typical when failure cost is small relative to overhaul cost. The optimal interval formula is most useful when these magnitudes are comparable.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Upgrade Justification Using NPV

**Scenario:** A 10-year-old CNC milling machine is being considered for upgrade. The upgrade costs **₹15,00,000** and is expected to reduce rejection rate, save energy, and increase throughput, generating a uniform annual benefit of **₹3,50,000** for 6 years. The discount rate is **10%**.

**Step 1 — Calculate the Present Value Annuity Factor (PVAF) for 6 years at 10%:**

$$
PVAF = \dfrac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r} = \dfrac{1 - (1.10)^{-6}}{0.10}
$$

$$
PVAF = \dfrac{1 - 0.5645}{0.10} = \dfrac{0.4355}{0.10} = 4.355
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Present Value of Benefits (PVB):**

$$
PVB = 3{,}50{,}000 \times 4.355 = 15{,}24{,}250\ \text{₹}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Net Present Value (NPV):**

$$
NPV = PVB - C_0 = 15{,}24{,}250 - 15{,}00{,}000 = 24{,}250\ \text{₹}
$$

**Step 4 — Decision rule:**

Since $NPV > 0$, the upgrade is **financially justified**, though marginally. A more robust upgrade package with higher annual benefits would be preferable.

**Step 5 — Sensitivity check (if discount rate rises to 15%):**

$$
PVAF_{15\%,6} = \dfrac{1 - (1.15)^{-6}}{0.15} = \dfrac{1 - 0.4323}{0.15} = 3.785
$$

$$
NPV_{15\%} = 3{,}50{,}000 \times 3.785 - 15{,}00{,}000 = 13{,}24{,}750 - 15{,}00{,}000 = -1{,}75{,}250\ \text{₹}
$$

At 15% discount rate, the upgrade becomes unviable. This sensitivity is critical for management decision-making.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

### 4.1 Overhaul Process Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Equipment Reaches TBO or Triggers Condition Monitoring] --> B[Pre-Planning Phase: Scope, Spares, Manpower, Tools]

    I --> K
    J --> K

```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**

*Define the term "overhaul" in the context of maintenance engineering. Differentiate between minor and major overhauls.*

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Overhaul** is a comprehensive planned maintenance activity that involves complete disassembly, detailed inspection, repair or replacement of worn components, cleaning, reassembly, and performance testing of an equipment to restore it to a condition equivalent to its original specification.

**Differentiation:**

| Aspect | Minor Overhaul | Major Overhaul |
|--------|----------------|----------------|
| **Scope** | Limited components (filters, seals, minor parts) | Complete strip-down, all major assemblies |
| **Frequency** | 1,000–5,000 operating hours | 8,000–25,000 operating hours |
| **Downtime** | 4–24 hours | 1–4 weeks |
| **Cost** | Low (5–15% of replacement) | High (30–60% of replacement) |
| **Manpower** | 2–5 technicians | 10–50 specialists |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

*What is an equipment upgrade? List any four types of upgrades commonly performed in industries.*

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> An **upgrade** is a deliberate engineering modification of an existing asset to improve its performance, efficiency, reliability, safety, or compliance beyond the original equipment manufacturer's specifications.

**Four Common Types of Upgrades:**

1. **Performance Upgrades** — Increasing capacity, speed, or throughput of the machine
2. **Safety Upgrades** — Installing modern guarding, interlocks, and emergency stops to meet current safety standards
3. **Energy/Efficiency Upgrades** — Replacing motors with IE4 efficiency class, installing VFDs (Variable Frequency Drives)
4. **Technology/Automation Upgrades** — Retrofitting CNC machines with new controllers, adding IoT sensors for predictive maintenance

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]

**(a)** Explain in detail the **step-by-step procedure for conducting a major overhaul** of an industrial compressor, with reference to planning, dismantling, inspection, restoration, and commissioning phases. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A machine tool has a constant failure rate of **0.0005 failures per hour** during its useful life. The cost of a major overhaul is **₹12,00,000**, while the average breakdown cost is **₹2,00,000**. Determine the **optimal overhaul interval** and the **minimum cost rate per hour** using Crawford's formula. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Major Overhaul Procedure for an Industrial Compressor:**

**Phase 1 — Pre-Planning (Pre-Overhaul):**
- Review OEM service manuals and historical performance records
- Define the scope: what assemblies will be opened, what parts will be replaced
- Estimate downtime and obtain production schedule adjustment approvals
- Procure spare parts, special tools, lifting equipment, and consumables
- Brief the overhaul team; assign responsibilities using a Responsibility Matrix (RACI)

**Phase 2 — Shutdown and Isolation:**
- Apply **Lock-Out Tag-Out (LOTO)** procedures
- Depressurize the system, drain oil and coolants
- Conduct pre-disassembly measurements: bearing clearances, rotor run-out, alignment data

**Phase 3 — Dismantling:**
- Systematic disassembly with component tagging and photography
- Maintain an **overhaul logbook** with serial numbers and condition notes
- Send critical components (rotors, bearings, seals) for cleaning and inspection

**Phase 4 — Inspection and Diagnosis:**
- Visual inspection for cracks, wear, corrosion, overheating marks
- Dimensional checks using micrometers, dial indicators, bore gauges
- **Non-Destructive Testing**: dye penetrant, magnetic particle, ultrasonic testing
- Classify components as: Reusable, Repairable, or Replaceable

**Phase 5 — Restoration:**
- Clean all reusable parts using approved solvents
- Machine worn surfaces to OEM tolerances or replace with new parts
- Replace all gaskets, seals, O-rings, and single-use items as standard practice

**Phase 6 — Reassembly and Commissioning:**
- Reassemble in reverse order with proper torque application
- Perform laser alignment of couplings
- Conduct no-load run test, followed by load performance test
- Record baseline vibration, temperature, and current readings for future comparison
- Update the equipment's maintenance history file

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given:
- $\lambda = 0.0005$ failures/hour
- $C_{OH} = 12{,}00{,}000$ ₹
- $C_f = 2{,}00{,}000$ ₹

Applying Crawford's optimal interval formula:

$$
T^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot C_{OH}}{\lambda \cdot C_f}}
$$

$$
T^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 12{,}00{,}000}{0.0005 \times 2{,}00{,}000}}
$$

$$
T^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{24{,}00{,}000}{100}}
$$

$$
T^* = \sqrt{24{,}000}
$$

$$
\boxed{T^* \approx 154.92\ \text{hours}}
$$

Minimum cost rate calculation:

$$
C_{rate}(T^*) = \dfrac{C_{OH}}{T^*} + \lambda \cdot C_f
$$

$$
C_{rate}(154.92) = \dfrac{12{,}00{,}000}{154.92} + 0.0005 \times 2{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
C_{rate}(154.92) = 7746.45 + 100
$$

$$
\boxed{C_{rate}^{min} \approx 7846.45\ \text{₹/hour}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part (b)**: Many students forget to **state the units clearly** and **round the final answer appropriately**. Also, ensure the **square root step is shown explicitly**—writing only the final numerical answer without the intermediate radical expression will attract a 1-mark deduction. Always substitute values into the formula in the same step where the formula is stated.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Discuss the **economic justification for equipment upgrade** with reference to Net Present Value (NPV) and Return on Investment (ROI) calculations. What is the minimum acceptable ROI for industrial upgrades? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast **overhauling and upgrading** as maintenance strategies. Under what specific conditions is an upgrade preferred over an overhaul? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Economic Justification for Equipment Upgrade:**

When a piece of equipment has been in service for a significant time, management faces three options: continue with periodic overhauls, upgrade the existing asset, or purchase a new one. The decision requires a rigorous financial analysis.

**Net Present Value (NPV) Method:**

The NPV method discounts all future cash flows (benefits minus costs) back to present value and compares with the initial investment:

$$
NPV = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{B_t - C_t}{(1+r)^t} - C_0
$$

Where:
- $B_t$ = annual benefits in year $t$ (₹)
- $C_t$ = annual operating costs in year $t$ (₹)
- $C_0$ = initial upgrade investment (₹)
- $r$ = discount rate (cost of capital)
- $n$ = useful life of upgrade (years)

**Decision Rule:** $NPV > 0$ ⇒ upgrade is financially viable; $NPV < 0$ ⇒ reject the upgrade.

**Return on Investment (ROI) Method:**

$$
ROI = \dfrac{\text{Total Net Benefit Over Life}}{\text{Initial Investment}} \times \dfrac{1}{n} \times 100\ \%
$$

For industrial upgrades, a **minimum acceptable ROI of 15–20%** is typically required to justify capital expenditure. This exceeds the cost of capital to ensure value creation for the organization.

**Sensitivity Analysis:** The decision should be tested against variations in discount rate, benefit estimates, and project life to assess robustness.

**(b) Comparison: Overhauling vs. Upgrading:**

| Parameter | Overhaul | Upgrade |
|-----------|----------|---------|
| **Objective** | Restore to original specification | Enhance beyond original specification |
| **Scope** | Replacement of worn parts only | Modification, addition of new features |
| **Cost** | 30–60% of replacement cost | 50–80% of replacement cost |
| **Resulting Performance** | Same as original | Better than original |
| **Technology** | Unchanged | May incorporate new technology |
| **Documentation Impact** | Minor update | Significant engineering change |
| **Useful Life Extension** | One full life cycle | Multiple life cycles possible |

**Conditions Favoring Upgrade Over Overhaul:**

1. The original design is technologically obsolete (e.g., analog control → digital control)
2. Energy efficiency is below current regulatory benchmarks
3. Spare parts for original design are discontinued or excessively expensive
4. Safety incidents indicate fundamental design deficiency
5. Repeated overhauls (> 3 cycles) have failed to restore satisfactory performance
6. NPV of upgrade is positive while marginal cost of further overhauls is rising

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part (a)**: A common mistake is presenting the NPV formula but **not writing the decision rule explicitly**. Examiners award marks for the *interpretation* of the NPV result, not just the computation. Always conclude with: *"Since NPV is positive/negative, the upgrade is recommended/rejected."* Also, do not confuse NPV with PI (Profitability Index) — these are related but distinct metrics.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Overhauls and Upgrades:**

- [x] **Overhaul** restores an asset to *as-good-as-new* condition; **Upgrade** makes it *better-than-new*
- [x] Major overhaul downtime ranges from **1 week to 1 month**; complete overhaul may take **1–3 months**
- [x] **TBO** (Time Between Overhauls) is OEM-specified; **MTBO** is actual operating interval achieved
- [x] Overhaul planning follows: **Pre-plan → Dismantle → Inspect → Restore → Reassemble → Commission**
- [x] **Crawford's optimal interval formula**: $T^* = \sqrt{2 C_{OH} / (\lambda \cdot C_f)}$
- [x] **Availability**: $A_i = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)$ — directly impacted by overhaul quality
- [x] Upgrade justification is done through **NPV**, **ROI**, and **Payback Period** analysis
- [x] Minimum industrial **ROI for upgrade approval** is typically **15–20%**
- [x] **Bath-tub curve** justifies overhauls: overhaul resets the curve to useful-life phase
- [x] Decision rule: repair if cost < 30% of replacement; overhaul if 30–60%; upgrade if NPV > 0 and tech obsolete; replace otherwise
- [x] **LOTO** (Lock-Out Tag-Out) is mandatory before any overhaul work
- [x] **NDT methods** used in inspection: dye penetrant, magnetic particle, ultrasonic, radiographic
- [x] **Critical spare parts** must be identified and stocked before scheduling an overhaul
- [x] Post-overhaul **baseline readings** (vibration, temperature, current) must be recorded for future condition monitoring
- [x] Upgrades often qualify for **capital depreciation benefits** and **energy-efficiency subsidies**

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Examination Tip**: For 14-mark Part B questions, always structure your answer with: (1) Introduction/Definition, (2) Main Body with sub-headings or tabular comparisons, (3) Mathematical/Analytical treatment where applicable, (4) Conclusion with practical recommendation. KTU examiners reward **structured, well-diagrammed, and quantitatively-supported** answers over vague descriptive essays.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT523 - MAINTENANCE AND SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Breakdown Maintenance - Computerized Planning and Scheduling - Maintenance Repair and Operations - Storeroom Excellence

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Breakdown Maintenance (BDM)

**Formal KTU Definition:**
Breakdown Maintenance (also called *Reactive Maintenance* or *Run-to-Failure Maintenance*) is a corrective maintenance strategy in which repair or replacement actions are initiated **only after a functional failure** has occurred in an equipment, machine, or asset. The asset is allowed to operate until it breaks down, and only then are resources mobilized to restore it to operational state.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Breakdown Maintenance is the *opposite pole* of Preventive Maintenance. It is the default mode in non-critical, low-cost, or redundant equipment where the cost of preventive action exceeds the cost of failure.

**Intuitive Analogy — "The Streetlight Mentality":**
Imagine streetlights in a small town. The municipal corporation only fixes a streetlight **after a citizen reports it is dark**. There is no scheduled inspection, no planned bulb replacement, and no proactive cleaning. The light works until it does not, and then a crew is dispatched. This is Breakdown Maintenance. The system is simple, requires no planning effort upfront, but the cost per repair is high and citizen inconvenience is severe. The same logic applies to industrial equipment — a conveyor belt, a hydraulic pump, or a motor.

**Core Characteristics (Board-Exam Favorites):**

- **Reactive in nature** — work is generated *by* failure, not by calendar.
- **No pre-planned schedule** — except in the sense of having rapid response capability.
- **Cost is incurred only when failure occurs** — no sunk preventive cost.
- **High downtime per event** — diagnosis is unplanned, spares may not be staged.
- **Suitable for non-critical, low-value, easily replaceable assets.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Performance Metrics Associated with BDM:**
> - **MTBF** (Mean Time Between Failures) = **hours**
> - **MTTR** (Mean Time To Repair) = **hours**
> - **Availability** $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ (dimensionless ratio, 0 to 1)

---

### 1.2 Computerized Planning and Scheduling

**Formal Definition:**
Computerized Planning and Scheduling in the maintenance context refers to the use of digital tools — most prominently a **CMMS (Computerized Maintenance Management System)** or the more advanced **EAM (Enterprise Asset Management)** platform — to plan, schedule, dispatch, track, and close out maintenance work orders. It transforms maintenance from a paper-based, ad-hoc craft into a data-driven engineering discipline.

> [!NOTE]
> **Hierarchy of Digital Maintenance Tools:**
> - **CMMS** — Computerized Maintenance Management System (tactical, work-order focused)
> - **EAM** — Enterprise Asset Management (strategic, asset lifecycle focused)
> - **APM** — Asset Performance Management (analytics-driven, predictive)
> - **IIoT/Industry 4.0** — Sensor-fed, real-time platforms

**Intuitive Analogy — "The Hospital ER Triage System":**
A modern hospital does not just *react* to emergencies. It runs a **computerized triage system**: patients are categorized, doctors are assigned shifts, operating rooms are pre-booked, and blood-bank inventory is tracked in real time. CMMS does the same for maintenance — every work order is a patient, every technician is a doctor, every storeroom is a pharmacy, and the dispatch screen is the triage desk.

**Key Functions of a CMMS/EAM System:**

- Work Order Management (creation, approval, closure)
- Preventive Maintenance Scheduling (calendar and runtime triggers)
- Asset Hierarchy and Equipment Registry
- Spare Parts and Inventory Linkage
- Labour Allocation and Time Tracking
- Failure History and Root-Cause Database
- KPI Dashboards (MTBF, MTTR, PM Compliance, Backlog)

---

### 1.3 Maintenance, Repair and Operations (MRO)

**Formal Definition:**
**MRO (Maintenance, Repair and Operations)** refers to the totality of materials, supplies, and services that an organization procures and uses to keep its production equipment, facilities, and supporting infrastructure operational, but **which do not become part of the finished product itself**. It is a category of indirect materials, distinct from raw materials and direct production consumables.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Distinction — Direct vs. Indirect MRO:**
> - **Direct MRO**: Items consumed in the production process (e.g., cutting fluids, welding rods, lubricants on the assembly line).
> - **Indirect MRO**: Items that support the production environment (e.g., office stationery, safety shoes, cleaning agents, light bulbs).

**Intuitive Analogy — "The Workshop, the Kitchen, and the Factory":**
Think of a large bakery. The flour, sugar, and butter are *raw materials* — they go into the bread. The bread itself is the *finished product*. Now, what keeps the bakery running? The oven's thermostat, the worker's gloves, the cleaning brush, the receipt paper, the pest-control service, the spare belt for the mixer. **None of these go into the bread**, but the bakery *cannot operate* without them. That entire supporting inventory is **MRO**.

**Three Sub-Categories of MRO:**

1. **Maintenance MRO** — Spare parts, bearings, seals, lubricants, gaskets, filters.
2. **Repair MRO** — Welding consumables, fasteners, adhesives, repair kits.
3. **Operations MRO** — PPE, janitorial supplies, office consumables, safety signage.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Benchmark:** In capital-intensive industries such as power, oil & gas, and heavy manufacturing, **MRO spend typically equals 3% to 8% of total revenue**, and in some asset-heavy sectors, can exceed the cost of raw materials.

---

### 1.4 Storeroom Excellence

**Formal Definition:**
**Storeroom Excellence** is a state of operational discipline within the maintenance warehouse/storeroom where inventory is **accurately identified, optimally stocked, ergonomically stored, cleanly maintained, and rapidly retrievable**, achieving the dual objectives of *minimizing inventory carrying cost* and *maximizing wrench time* of maintenance crews.

**Intuitive Analogy — "The 5-Star Hospital Pharmacy":**
A storeroom should resemble a hospital pharmacy, not a junk shop. Every drug (spare) is labelled, dosed (stock level), shelved by category, and retrievable in seconds. The pharmacist (stores officer) can tell you within minutes whether a particular drug is in stock, how much, and when the next replenishment arrives. A storeroom where a mechanic walks in and asks *"Has anyone seen the 6205 bearing?"* is a storeroom that has failed.

**The Five Pillars of Storeroom Excellence (5S Foundation):**

1. **Sort (Seiri)** — Eliminate obsolete, duplicate, and dead stock.
2. **Set in Order (Seiton)** — A place for everything, and everything in its place.
3. **Shine (Seiso)** — Clean storeroom, clean bins, clean labels.
4. **Standardize (Seiketsu)** — Visual standards, colour codes, bin labels.
5. **Sustain (Shitsuke)** — Audit, train, sustain discipline.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Performance Indicator (KPI) — Storeroom Service Level:**
> The percentage of maintenance work orders that proceed without delay due to missing parts. A "best-in-class" target is **≥ 95%** line-item fill rate from the storeroom.

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Deep Dive — Breakdown Maintenance

**The "Why" Behind Breakdown Maintenance:**

Not all assets justify preventive maintenance. The economic logic of BDM is rooted in the *cost of prevention vs. the cost of failure*. When an asset has the following characteristics, BDM is the rational choice:

- **Low acquisition cost** — replacement is cheaper than prevention.
- **Non-critical function** — failure does not halt the entire plant.
- **Redundancy available** — a parallel unit can carry the load.
- **Predictable, low failure rate** — failures are rare and acceptable.
- **Safety-neutral** — failure does not endanger life or environment.

**Disadvantages (Where Marks Are Lost in KTU Answers):**

- **Unplanned downtime** is typically 3 to 5 times longer than planned downtime.
- **Catastrophic secondary damage** — a single bearing failure can destroy a shaft, the coupling, and the gearbox housing.
- **Spares chaos** — when the failure happens, the right spare is often not on the shelf.
- **Overtime cost explosion** — emergency repairs at 11 PM cost 2× the day rate.
- **Production loss** — a 4-hour BDM event on a bottleneck line can cost crores.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strategic Shift:** Modern maintenance philosophy follows the **Reliability-Centered Maintenance (RCM)** decision logic, which uses failure mode consequences to decide between PM, PdM, or BDM. BDM is *intentionally chosen*, not *defaulted to*.

---

### 2.2 Deep Dive — Computerized Planning and Scheduling

**The Planning Hierarchy (KTU Board Favourite):**

A maintenance planner operates at three temporal levels:

1. **Long-Range (Annual) Plan** — Shutdowns, major overhauls, capital replacements.
2. **Mid-Range (Monthly/Weekly) Plan** — PM calendar, backlog grooming, resource balancing.
3. **Short-Range (Daily) Plan** — Work order dispatch, daily schedule adherence.

**The 7-Step CMMS Work Order Cycle:**

1. **Trigger** — PM calendar, breakdown report, or corrective request.
2. **Identification** — Asset code, failure code, location, priority.
3. **Job Plan Creation** — Tasks, man-hours, tools, spares, safety requirements.
4. **Scheduling** — Slot in available window with crew and parts ready.
5. **Execution** — Technician completes, logs actual hours and parts consumed.
6. **Confirmation** — Quality check, downtime closure, asset back in service.
7. **Feedback and Analysis** — Failure root cause, MTBF update, cost capture.

**Benefits Realized Through Computerized Planning:**

- Reduction in **planning time per work order** by up to 60%.
- Increase in **wrench time** (productive hours vs. total paid hours) from ~35% to ~65%.
- **Spare parts stock-out reduction** by 40-50% through better forecasting.
- **Audit-ready compliance** documentation for ISO 55000, OSHA, statutory bodies.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Wrench Time" KPI** is the single most diagnostic metric for a maintenance organization. A wrench time below 50% indicates chronic planning failure, not labour failure.

---

### 2.3 Deep Dive — Maintenance, Repair and Operations (MRO)

**The MRO Spend Pyramid (Bottom = Highest Volume, Lowest Value):**

- **Tier 1 — Critical Spares** (e.g., turbine blades, control valves) — Low volume, very high value, long lead time. *Engineering-led procurement.*
- **Tier 2 — Operational Spares** (e.g., bearings, belts, filters) — Medium volume, medium value, medium lead time. *Storeroom-managed.*
- **Tier 3 — Consumables** (e.g., lubricants, cleaners, fasteners) — High volume, low value, short lead time. *Vendor-managed inventory (VMI) often applied.*

**Strategic Importance of MRO Management:**

A poorly managed MRO supply chain is a **silent profit killer**. Studies consistently show that 5% to 15% of MRO spend is wasted on:

- **Maverick buying** (uncontrolled, off-contract purchases)
- **Excess stock** of obsolete items
- **Emergency freight** (air freight instead of sea/road)
- **Duplication** (three brands of the same bearing on the shelf)

---

### 2.4 Deep Dive — Storeroom Excellence

**The 5S Implementation Roadmap:**

1. **Phase 1 — Red Tag Campaign**: Tag every item not used in the last 12 months as a candidate for disposal, transfer, or sale.
2. **Phase 2 — Zone and Bin Design**: Allocate fixed bin locations, label with barcodes/RFID, group by equipment family.
3. **Phase 3 — Visual Management**: Colour-coded floor markings for walkways, red lines for quarantine, green for issued items.
4. **Phase 4 — Cycle Counting**: Replace annual wall-to-wall stock-takes with daily A-items, weekly B-items, monthly C-items (aligned with ABC analysis).
5. **Phase 5 — Continuous Improvement (Kaizen)**: Monthly Gemba walks, 5S audits, and corrective action loops.

**Key Inventory Models Used in Storeroom Management:**

- **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)** — to decide *how much* to order.
- **Reorder Point (ROP)** — to decide *when* to order.
- **Safety Stock (SS)** — to buffer against demand and supply variability.
- **ABC Analysis** — to classify items and allocate management effort.
- **FSN Analysis** — Fast, Slow, Non-moving — for obsolescence control.
- **VED Analysis** — Vital, Essential, Desirable — for criticality-based stocking.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Alert:** Students often confuse **ABC** (Annual Consumption Value based) with **VED** (Criticality based). ABC controls money; VED controls downtime risk. They are orthogonal classifications.

---

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Variables and Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Availability** | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | $MTBF$ in h, $MTTR$ in h, $A$ dimensionless | Reliability engineering |
| **MTTR** | $MTTR = \dfrac{\sum T_{repair}}{N_f}$ | $T_{repair}$ = repair time (h), $N_f$ = no. of failures | Maintenance efficiency |
| **MTBF** | $MTBF = \dfrac{\sum T_{operating}}{N_f}$ | $T_{operating}$ (h), $N_f$ failures | Reliability |
| **Economic Order Quantity** | $EOQ = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$ | $D$ = annual demand (units), $S$ = order cost (Rs/order), $H$ = holding cost (Rs/unit/yr) | Order quantity decision |
| **Total Inventory Cost** | $TC = DC + \dfrac{D}{Q}S + \dfrac{Q}{2}H$ | $D$ demand, $C$ unit cost, $Q$ order qty, $S$ order cost, $H$ holding cost | Cost optimization |
| **Reorder Point** | $ROP = d \cdot L + SS$ | $d$ = daily demand (units/day), $L$ = lead time (days), $SS$ = safety stock | Reorder trigger |
| **Safety Stock** | $SS = Z \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L}$ | $Z$ = service level factor, $\sigma_d$ = std. dev. of demand, $L$ lead time (days) | Stock-out protection |
| **Inventory Turnover** | $IT = \dfrac{COGS}{Average\ Inventory}$ | $COGS$ = Cost of Goods Sold, Avg. Inventory in Rs | Working capital efficiency |
| **Wrench Time \%** | $WT\% = \dfrac{T_{productive}}{T_{paid}} \times 100$ | $T_{productive}$ = hands-on tool time, $T_{paid}$ = total paid hours | Maintenance productivity |
| **PM Compliance \%** | $PMC\% = \dfrac{PM_{done}}{PM_{scheduled}} \times 100$ | PM done vs. scheduled in a period | Schedule adherence |
| **Stock-out Rate** | $SOR\% = \dfrac{N_{stockout}}{N_{requests}} \times 100$ | $N_{stockout}$ = failed issue events, $N_{requests}$ = total issues | Storeroom service level |
| **Carrying Cost Rate** | $CCR\% = \dfrac{H}{C} \times 100$ | $H$ = holding cost/unit/yr, $C$ = unit cost | Inventory finance |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Service-Level Z-Values (for KTU Numerical Questions):**
> - 90% service level $\rightarrow Z = 1.28$
> - 95% service level $\rightarrow Z = 1.65$
> - 97.5% service level $\rightarrow Z = 1.96$
> - 99% service level $\rightarrow Z = 2.33$

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) Formula

The EOQ model — also called the **Wilson-Harris Model** or **Square Root Formula** — is derived by minimizing the **Total Annual Inventory Cost (TC)** as a function of order quantity $Q$.

**Step 1: Identify the three cost components of total inventory cost.**

The total annual cost of holding inventory is composed of:

(i) **Purchasing Cost** = $D \cdot C$

This is the cost of buying the units themselves. It is independent of the order quantity $Q$ and therefore irrelevant to optimization.

(ii) **Ordering Cost** = $\dfrac{D}{Q} \cdot S$

If the annual demand is $D$ units, and each order is for $Q$ units, then the number of orders placed per year is $D / Q$. At a cost of $S$ rupees per order, total annual ordering cost is $\dfrac{D}{Q} \cdot S$.

(iii) **Holding Cost** = $\dfrac{Q}{2} \cdot H$

If orders of size $Q$ arrive instantaneously and consumption is uniform, the average inventory level is $Q/2$. At a holding cost of $H$ rupees per unit per year, total annual holding cost is $\dfrac{Q}{2} \cdot H$.

**Step 2: Formulate the Total Cost equation.**

Ignoring the constant purchasing cost (which does not affect the optimal $Q$), the variable cost to minimize is:

$$TC(Q) = \frac{D}{Q} \cdot S + \frac{Q}{2} \cdot H$$

**Step 3: Differentiate TC(Q) with respect to Q and set to zero.**

$$\frac{d(TC)}{dQ} = -\frac{DS}{Q^{2}} + \frac{H}{2} = 0$$

**Step 4: Solve for Q.**

$$\frac{H}{2} = \frac{DS}{Q^{2}}$$

$$Q^{2} = \frac{2DS}{H}$$

$$Q^{*} = EOQ = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}$$

**Step 5: Verify it is a minimum (second derivative test).**

$$\frac{d^{2}(TC)}{dQ^{2}} = \frac{2DS}{Q^{3}} \gt 0$$

Since the second derivative is positive, the critical point is confirmed as a **global minimum** of the cost curve.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition behind the Square Root:** If demand doubles, EOQ grows by a factor of $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$, not 2. This is why EOQ discourages bulk ordering even when demand is high.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Safety Stock Formula

Safety stock protects against two sources of uncertainty:

- **Demand variability** during lead time (standard deviation $\sigma_d$).
- **Lead time variability** (treated in extended models).

Under the simplifying assumption that lead time is constant and demand during lead time is normally distributed:

**Step 1: Define the service-level concept.**

The desired **service level** is the probability that stock will not run out during lead time. For a target service level $SL$, we look up the corresponding **Z-score** from the standard normal table.

**Step 2: Express stock-out protection in terms of Z.**

The safety stock must equal $Z$ standard deviations above the expected demand during lead time:

$$SS = Z \cdot \sigma_{DLT}$$

where $\sigma_{DLT}$ is the standard deviation of demand during lead time.

**Step 3: Apply the variance-additivity rule for constant lead time.**

If lead time $L$ is constant and daily demand has standard deviation $\sigma_d$, then the standard deviation of demand over $L$ days is:

$$\sigma_{DLT} = \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L}$$

**Step 4: Combine to obtain the standard Safety Stock formula.**

$$SS = Z \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L}$$

**Step 5: Compute Reorder Point.**

The reorder point must trigger an order when inventory drops to a level that covers **expected demand during lead time** plus **safety stock**:

$$ROP = d \cdot L + Z \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **When lead time is variable** (with standard deviation $\sigma_L$ and mean $L$), the variance-additivity rule extends to:
> $$\sigma_{DLT} = \sqrt{L \cdot \sigma_d^{2} + d^{2} \cdot \sigma_L^{2}}$$
> KTU questions occasionally test this extended form.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem Statement:**
A manufacturing plant consumes a critical bearing at the rate of $d = 50$ units per day. The standard deviation of daily demand is $\sigma_d = 8$ units. The supplier's lead time is $L = 6$ days (constant). The company wants a 95% service level. The annual holding cost is $H = \text{Rs. } 40$ per bearing per year, and the ordering cost is $S = \text{Rs. } 800$ per order. The annual demand is $D = 50 \times 250 = 12{,}500$ units (assuming 250 working days).

**Find:** (i) EOQ (ii) Reorder Point (iii) Number of orders per year.

**Solution:**

**Part (i) — EOQ:**

$$EOQ = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 12{,}500 \times 800}{40}}$$

$$EOQ = \sqrt{\frac{20{,}000{,}000}{40}} = \sqrt{500{,}000}$$

$$EOQ = 707.1 \approx 707\ \text{units per order}$$

**Part (ii) — Reorder Point:**

For 95% service level, $Z = 1.65$.

$$SS = Z \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L} = 1.65 \times 8 \times \sqrt{6}$$

$$SS = 1.65 \times 8 \times 2.449 = 32.33\ \text{units}$$

$$ROP = d \cdot L + SS = 50 \times 6 + 32.33 = 332.33 \approx 333\ \text{units}$$

**Part (iii) — Number of orders per year:**

$$N = \frac{D}{EOQ} = \frac{12{,}500}{707} \approx 17.68 \approx 18\ \text{orders per year}$$

**Cycle time (time between orders):**

$$T_{cycle} = \frac{250\ \text{working days}}{18\ \text{orders}} \approx 13.9\ \text{days per order}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification — Inventory Turnover Ratio:**
> $IT = \dfrac{12{,}500}{EOQ/2} = \dfrac{12{,}500}{353.5} \approx 35.4$ turns per year. This is typical for a C-class consumable, suggesting the bearing is high-frequency/low-value.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (BDM vs. PM vs. PdM)

| Parameter | Breakdown Maintenance (BDM) | Preventive Maintenance (PM) | Predictive Maintenance (PdM) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Trigger** | Failure occurrence | Calendar / runtime hours | Condition monitoring data |
| **Cost (Short Term)** | Low (no action unless failure) | Medium (scheduled spend) | High (instrumentation) |
| **Cost (Long Term)** | High (secondary damage, downtime) | Medium | Lowest |
| **Downtime Pattern** | Unplanned, long, clustered | Planned, short, periodic | Minimal, planned, just-in-time |
| **Spares Strategy** | Emergency call-out, fast freight | Stocked in advance per BOM | Stocked based on condition trend |
| **Data Requirement** | Minimal | Moderate | High (vibration, thermography, oil analysis) |
| **Best Suited For** | Non-critical, low-cost assets | Safety-critical, regulated assets | High-value, bottleneck assets |
| **Failure Coverage** | Reactive only | Mitigates random failures | Targets incipient failures |

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram A — Breakdown Maintenance Decision and Execution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Asset in Service] --> B{Functional\nFailure?}
    B -- No --> A
    B -- Yes --> C[Operator Reports Failure\nto Maintenance Desk]
    C --> D[Failure Code Assigned\nCriticality Assessed]
    D --> E{Plant Safety Risk?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Isolate Asset\nNotify EHS]
    E -- No --> G[Check Available Spares\nin Storeroom]
    G --> H{Spares Available?}
    H -- No --> I[Emergency Procurement\nAir Freight / Local Vendor]
    H -- Yes --> J[Dispatch Technician Crew]
    I --> J
    F --> J
    J --> K[Diagnosis and Repair]
    K --> L[Quality Verification]
    L --> M[Asset Restored to Service]
    M --> N[Log MTTR and Failure Cause\nin CMMS]
    N --> O[Review for RCA\nif Repeated Failure]
    O --> A
```

**Interpretation:** This diagram captures the *reactive chain* of BDM. The two decision gates (safety risk and spares availability) are the points where BDM costs explode — when the answer is "Yes" to safety risk and "No" to spares, the event becomes a crisis.

---

### 4.2 Diagram B — CMMS Planning and Scheduling Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Annual Maintenance Plan] --> B1[Monthly PM Calendar]
    A1 --> C1[Major Overhaul Schedule]
    B1 --> D1[Weekly Backlog Review]
    C1 --> D1
    D1 --> E1[Daily Work Order\nDispatch Board]
    E1 --> F1[Technician Executes\nLogs Time and Parts]
    F1 --> G1[Work Order Closure\nin CMMS]
    G1 --> H1[KPI Dashboard Update]
    H1 --> I1{Backlog within\nTarget?}
    I1 -- No --> J1[Resource Reallocation]
    I1 -- Yes --> A1
    J1 --> D1
    H1 --> K1[Management Review\nMonthly]
```

**Interpretation:** The CMMS workflow is a **closed-loop control system** for maintenance execution. The KPI dashboard feeds back to the planner, who re-balances resources, who feeds back to the dispatch board — a true Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA) cycle.

---

### 4.3 Diagram C — MRO Categorization Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[MRO Inventory] --> B[Maintenance MRO]
    A --> C[Repair MRO]
    A --> D[Operations MRO]
    B --> B1[Spare Parts\nBearings, Seals]
    B --> B2[Lubricants\nOils, Greases]
    C --> C1[Welding Consumables\nElectrodes, Wire]
    C --> C2[Fasteners\nBolts, Nuts, Washers]
    D --> D1[PPE\nGloves, Helmets, Goggles]
    D --> D2[Janitorial\nCleaners, Mops]
    D --> D3[Office Supplies\nStationery]
    A --> E[Criticality Tier]
    E --> E1[Vital - Cannot Operate Without]
    E --> E2[Essential - Reduced Capacity]
    E --> E3[Desirable - Convenience Items]
```

**Interpretation:** MRO items are categorized by **physical function** (left branch) and by **operational criticality** (right branch). A vital maintenance item such as a turbine bearing combines "B1" with "E1" and must be stocked with redundancy.

---

### 4.4 Diagram D — Storeroom Layout and 5S Zone Organization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Storeroom Layout] --> B[Receiving Zone]
```

**Interpretation:** This is a **block-level functional architecture** of a 5S-compliant storeroom. Each zone has a defined purpose, and material flow is unidirectional from receiving to issue, with quarantine acting as a quality gate.

---

### 4.5 Diagram E — Storeroom Service Level Improvement Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Measure Stock-out Rate] --> B1[Identify Top 20\nStock-out Items]
    B1 --> C1[Review Min-Max Levels\nand Lead Time]

```

**Interpretation:** This is the **Deming Cycle (PDCA)** applied specifically to storeroom service level. Improvement is data-driven, not anecdotal. KTU examiners reward answers that show such structured loops.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

Define Breakdown Maintenance. List any **four disadvantages** of relying on it as a primary maintenance strategy in a process plant.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
