Breakdown Maintenance (BDM) is a reactive maintenance strategy in which corrective action is taken **only after an equipment failure has occurred**, with no pre-planned preventive intervention. The asset is run until it fails, and repair is then executed on an emergency or priority basis.

**Four Disadvantages (1.5 marks):**

1. **Unplanned and prolonged downtime** — failure events typically cause 3 to 5 times longer downtime than planned interventions, often halting the entire production line.
2. **High cost of emergency repair** — overtime wages, expedited freight, and rushed procurement inflate the per-event cost by 200% to 400% versus a planned repair.
3. **Cascading secondary damage** — a single component failure (e.g., a seized bearing) can destroy adjacent components (shaft, coupling, gearbox housing), multiplying the repair cost.
4. **Loss of spare parts readiness** — the right spare is often not on the shelf, leading to extended waits and operational chaos.
5. *Any other valid point such as poor safety record, loss of customer commitments, and lack of failure data for improvement.*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

What is **MRO** in the context of industrial maintenance? Differentiate clearly between **Direct MRO** and **Indirect MRO** with one example each.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition of MRO (1 mark):**
MRO stands for **Maintenance, Repair and Operations**. It refers to the broad category of materials, supplies, and services that an organization uses to keep its production equipment, facilities, and operations running, but which **do not form part of the finished product**.

**Direct vs. Indirect MRO (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Direct MRO | Indirect MRO |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Items consumed during the production process itself, in direct contact with the product or production line. | Items that support the production environment and the workforce, without touching the product. |
| **Example** | Cutting fluid on a CNC machine; welding electrode in a fabrication shop. | PPE gloves used by a maintenance technician; office stationery for the maintenance planner. |

---

### PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of a **Computerized Maintenance Management System (CMMS)**. Describe its **seven key functional modules** and explain how each contributes to maintenance planning and control. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of **Storeroom Excellence**. Discuss the **5S methodology** as applied to a maintenance storeroom, and list **six Key Performance Indicators (KPIs)** used to measure storeroom performance. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — CMMS Architecture and Modules (7 marks):**

A CMMS is an integrated software platform that captures, processes, and reports on all maintenance-related data. Its architecture typically consists of a **central database** linked to multiple functional modules accessed by different users (planner, technician, supervisor, finance).

**Seven Key Modules:**

1. **Equipment/Asset Register Module** — Maintains a hierarchical database of all assets (Plant → Area → System → Equipment → Component), with technical specifications, serial numbers, and parent-child relationships. *Foundation for all other modules.*

2. **Work Order Management Module** — Creates, routes, and closes work orders (corrective, preventive, emergency). Captures actual labour hours, actual cost, failure codes, and root-cause notes.

3. **Preventive Maintenance Scheduling Module** — Generates PM work orders based on calendar triggers (e.g., monthly) or runtime triggers (e.g., every 500 hours of operation). Sends automatic notifications to the planner.

4. **Inventory and Procurement Module** — Manages spare-parts stock levels, min-max parameters, reorder points, and integrates with purchase orders and goods-received notes.

5. **Labour Management Module** — Tracks technician skills, certifications, shift availability, and assignment of work to qualified personnel.

6. **Failure History and RCA Module** — Stores a chronological record of all failures, enables Pareto analysis of failure modes, and supports Root-Cause Analysis (5-Why, Fishbone).

7. **Reporting and KPI Dashboard Module** — Computes and displays metrics such as MTBF, MTTR, PM compliance, backlog hours, wrench time, and maintenance cost per unit of production.

**Contribution to Planning and Control:**
The CMMS converts maintenance from a *reactive paper trail* into a *proactive, data-driven control system*. It enables the maintenance manager to **plan weeks ahead, balance resources, prevent stockouts, and continuously improve** based on hard data rather than anecdote.

**Part (b) — Storeroom Excellence, 5S and KPIs (7 marks):**

**Concept of Storeroom Excellence:**
Storeroom Excellence refers to the systematic, disciplined management of the maintenance warehouse such that the right part, in the right quantity, is available to the technician at the right time, while minimizing inventory carrying cost. It is the *operational backbone* of effective maintenance.

**5S Methodology Applied to Storeroom:**

1. **Sort (Seiri):** A "Red-Tag Campaign" identifies items not issued in the last 12 months. These are evaluated for disposal, return to vendor, or sale. Result: reduced inventory and freed-up space.
2. **Set in Order (Seiton):** Every item is assigned a fixed bin location with a clear label and barcode. Frequently used items are placed at waist height; heavy items on lower shelves.
3. **Shine (Seiso):** Daily cleaning of aisles, bins, and racks. Spills are cleaned immediately. A clean storeroom is a *visible audit of discipline*.
4. **Standardize (Seiketsu):** Colour-coded floor markings (yellow for aisles, red for fire equipment, green for staging). Standardized bin labels, standard operating procedures for receiving and issuing.
5. **Sustain (Shitsuke):** Monthly 5S audits, Gemba walks by management, recognition of best practices, and corrective action for non-conformities. Discipline is enforced, not assumed.

**Six KPIs for Storeroom Performance:**

1. **Line-Item Fill Rate (Stock-out Rate)** — % of work orders issued parts without delay. Target: ≥ 95%.
2. **Inventory Turnover Ratio** — Annual issue value / Average inventory value. Target: 4 to 8 for spares.
3. **Carrying Cost %** — Annual holding cost / Average inventory value. Target: 18% to 25%.
4. **Stock Accuracy (Cycle Count Accuracy)** — % of items where physical count matches system record. Target: ≥ 98%.
5. **Dead Stock %** — Value of non-moving items / Total inventory value. Target: ≤ 5%.
6. **Average Issue Time** — Time from technician's request to part handover. Target: ≤ 5 minutes for A-items.
7. *(Optional — 7th KPI)* **Emergency Purchase Frequency** — Number of emergency purchases per month, indicating planning failure.

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain **Breakdown Maintenance** in detail. Discuss the **five key conditions** under which BDM is the economically and operationally preferred strategy. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A manufacturing unit consumes a critical component at the rate of **40 units per day**. The **standard deviation of daily demand is 6 units**. The supplier's **lead time is 8 days** (constant). The **ordering cost is Rs. 600 per order** and the **holding cost is Rs. 20 per unit per year**. The **annual demand is 10,000 units**. The company targets a **95% service level**.

**Calculate:** (i) Economic Order Quantity (EOQ), (ii) Safety Stock, (iii) Reorder Point, and (iv) Total Annual Variable Cost. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Breakdown Maintenance in Detail (7 marks):**

**Definition and Philosophy (2 marks):**
Breakdown Maintenance is a corrective maintenance strategy in which equipment is allowed to operate until it fails, and repairs are carried out only after the failure has occurred. The philosophy is that prevention is more expensive than the failure event itself, and therefore no pre-failure intervention is justified. It is the *most basic* and *historically oldest* form of maintenance management.

**Working Principle (1 mark):**
The asset runs continuously (or as required) with no scheduled inspection or intervention. Failure events are recorded after occurrence. A maintenance crew is dispatched, parts are sourced, and the asset is restored to operation. The cycle repeats.

**Five Conditions Where BDM is Preferred (4 marks):**

1. **Low-Cost, Easily Replaceable Assets** — When the cost of preventive action approaches or exceeds the replacement cost, BDM is rational. *Example: a small electric fan, a standard bearing, a fuse.*
2. **Non-Critical Equipment** — When the asset's failure does not halt the production process and has no safety or environmental consequence. *Example: a workshop ceiling fan, a non-essential office air-conditioner.*
3. **Redundant Systems** — When a parallel standby unit can immediately take over the load, the failure event has zero operational impact. *Example: N+1 redundant pumps, parallel compressors.*
4. **Predictable, Low-Failure-Rate Assets** — When the MTBF is very long (years), the cost of routine inspection outweighs the rare event cost. *Example: a structural steel column, a building roof.*
5. **End-of-Life Assets Being Phased Out** — When the asset is scheduled for replacement within 1 to 2 years, investing in PM is wasteful. *Example: an old machine tool being replaced next budget cycle.*

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given Data:**
- $d = 40$ units/day, $\sigma_d = 6$ units/day
- $L = 8$ days, $S = \text{Rs. } 600/\text{order}$, $H = \text{Rs. } 20/\text{unit/yr}$
- $D = 10{,}000$ units/year, Service level = 95% $\Rightarrow Z = 1.65$

**Part (i) — EOQ (2 marks):**

$$EOQ = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10{,}000 \times 600}{20}}$$

$$EOQ = \sqrt{\frac{12{,}000{,}000}{20}} = \sqrt{600{,}000}$$

$$EOQ = 774.6 \approx 775\ \text{units per order}$$

**Part (ii) — Safety Stock (2 marks):**

$$SS = Z \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L} = 1.65 \times 6 \times \sqrt{8}$$

$$SS = 1.65 \times 6 \times 2.828 = 28.0\ \text{units}$$

**Part (iii) — Reorder Point (1.5 marks):**

$$ROP = d \cdot L + SS = 40 \times 8 + 28.0 = 320 + 28 = 348\ \text{units}$$

**Part (iv) — Total Annual Variable Cost (1.5 marks):**

$$TC = \frac{D}{EOQ} \cdot S + \frac{EOQ}{2} \cdot H$$

$$TC = \frac{10{,}000}{775} \times 600 + \frac{775}{2} \times 20$$

$$TC = 12.90 \times 600 + 387.5 \times 20$$

$$TC = \text{Rs. } 7{,}740 + \text{Rs. } 7{,}750 = \text{Rs. } 15{,}490\ \text{per year}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert daily demand to annual demand** — many students use $d = 40$ as $D$, producing a wildly incorrect EOQ.
> 2. **Using the wrong Z-value** — confusing 90% (1.28) with 95% (1.65) costs 0.5 marks.
> 3. **Omitting the square root in lead time** — a frequent algebraic slip in $SS$ computation.
> 4. **Not adding safety stock to demand-during-lead-time** in ROP — produces only the basic ROP, missing the safety component.
> 5. **In Part (a) answers, listing only "advantages" of BDM without naming the five conditions** — the question explicitly asks for *conditions of preference*, not advantages.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

**Breakdown Maintenance:**
- ✓ BDM is *reactive* — repair follows failure, never precedes it.
- ✓ It is the *correct* strategy for non-critical, low-cost, or redundant assets.
- ✓ Key disadvantages: unplanned long downtime, secondary damage, high emergency cost, poor spares readiness.
- ✓ Strategic shift is toward RCM, where BDM is *intentionally chosen* for specific failure modes.

**Computerized Planning and Scheduling:**
- ✓ CMMS is the tactical tool; EAM is strategic; APM is analytics-driven.
- ✓ Seven key CMMS modules: Asset Register, Work Order, PM Scheduling, Inventory, Labour, RCA, KPI Dashboard.
- ✓ Planning hierarchy: Long-Range (Annual) → Mid-Range (Monthly/Weekly) → Short-Range (Daily).
- ✓ Wrench time is the single best diagnostic metric — below 50% indicates planning failure.

**Maintenance Repair and Operations (MRO):**
- ✓ MRO = indirect materials, supplies, and services that do not form the finished product.
- ✓ Direct MRO touches the product; Indirect MRO supports the environment.
- ✓ MRO spend is typically 3% to 8% of revenue in asset-intensive industries.
- ✓ Poor MRO management wastes 5% to 15% of MRO budget through maverick buying, excess stock, and emergency freight.

**Storeroom Excellence:**
- ✓ Storeroom is the *operational backbone* of maintenance — the "pharmacy of the plant."
- ✓ 5S: Sort, Set in Order, Shine, Standardize, Sustain.
- ✓ Six storeroom KPIs: Fill Rate, Turnover, Carrying Cost, Stock Accuracy, Dead Stock %, Issue Time.
- ✓ ABC classifies by *value*; VED classifies by *criticality*; FSN classifies by *movement*.

**Key Formulas (Memorize Before Exam):**
- ✓ $EOQ = \sqrt{2DS/H}$
- ✓ $TC = (D/Q)S + (Q/2)H$
- ✓ $ROP = dL + Z\sigma_d\sqrt{L}$
- ✓ $A = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)$
- ✓ Z-values: 90% → 1.28, 95% → 1.65, 99% → 2.33

**Common KTU Examiner Traps:**
- ✓ Confusing ABC (value-based) with VED (criticality-based).
- ✓ Using daily demand as annual demand in EOQ — *always* convert.
- ✓ Forgetting the square root in the safety stock formula.
- ✓ Listing BDM disadvantages without explaining *when BDM is the right choice* — both sides must be presented for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Life Cycle Cost Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT523 - MAINTENANCE AND SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Life Cycle Cost Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Life Cycle Cost Analysis (LCCA)** is a systematic, economic-engineering methodology used to evaluate the **total cost of ownership** of a physical asset, machine, system, or infrastructure over its **entire useful life span** — from initial conception and acquisition, through operation and maintenance, to final decommissioning and disposal.

In the context of KTU's PERUT523 (Maintenance and Safety Engineering), LCCA is positioned as a **decision-support tool** that allows maintenance and reliability engineers to compare competing design alternatives (e.g., Machine A vs Machine B) on a *common, time-equivalent economic basis*, even when the alternatives have radically different upfront costs and operating/maintenance profiles.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PERUT523, Module 3):** LCCA is the analytical backbone of *Reliability-Centered Maintenance (RCM)*, *Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)*, and *proactive replacement* decisions. It converts engineering trade-offs (e.g., buying a cheaper pump vs a more expensive but more efficient one) into a single, comparable **monetary metric**.

Mathematically, the foundational identity is:

$$LCC = C_{acq} + \sum_{t=1}^{N} \frac{C_{O\&M,t}}{(1+i)^{,t}} - \frac{S}{(1+i)^{N}} + C_{disposal}$$

where every term is brought to a *common time reference* (typically Present Worth, Year 0) using the **time value of money** concept.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are buying a **car**. The showroom price (say ₹8,00,000) is *not* the real cost of the car. Over the next 10 years, you will also pay:

- Petrol / Diesel (Energy cost)
- Service & Spare parts (Maintenance cost)
- Insurance & Road tax (Operating cost)
- Tyre replacement, Battery replacement (Mid-life overhaul)
- Resale value (negative cost at year 10)
- Scrap / disposal cost (when you finally junk it)

The **Life Cycle Cost** is the *sum* of all these expenses in today's rupees. Now, suppose a friend offers an electric car for ₹12,00,000 — more expensive upfront, but cheaper to run. **LCCA tells you, scientifically, which car is truly cheaper over 10 years.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Takeaway:** LCCA is essentially *"asking the question — what does this asset really cost me from cradle to grave?"* It prevents the classic *capital budgeting trap* of choosing the cheapest initial purchase and then bleeding money on running costs for the next 20 years.

---

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics & Constants

The following **standard economic factors** are mandatory inputs to any LCCA problem:

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Range | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Minimum Attractive Rate of Return** | $i$ | 8%–15% | Often the bank loan rate + risk premium |
| **Analysis Horizon** | $N$ | 5–30 years | Must be a common multiple for alternatives |
| **Inflation Rate** | $f$ | 4%–7% | Usually embedded in $i$ as real vs nominal |
| **Study Period** | — | Equals $N$ | KTU convention: use integer years |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Valuation Point:** If a problem gives you a "market interest rate of 12% and inflation of 6%", the *real* discount rate is calculated using the **Fisher equation**:
> $$i_{real} = \frac{1 + i_{nom}}{1 + f} - 1$$
> Using the wrong rate is a guaranteed 2-mark loss in university valuation.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Canonical Phases of an Asset Life Cycle

Every industrial asset (pump, motor, CNC machine, transformer, boiler) traverses these six phases. LCCA aggregates costs across **all six**.

1. **Concept / Planning Phase** — Feasibility study, design engineering, vendor selection. Costs are usually *sunk* but small in magnitude.
2. **Acquisition / Installation Phase** — Purchase price, freight, foundation, commissioning, training. Recorded at $t = 0$.
3. **Operation Phase** — Energy (electricity, fuel), consumables, operator wages, indirect labour, overheads. Spread across years 1 to $N$.
4. **Maintenance Phase** — Preventive, predictive, corrective, and breakdown maintenance costs, including spares and lubricants. Recurring.
5. **Overhaul / Replacement Phase** — Mid-life capital replacements (e.g., gearbox overhaul at year 7). Lumpy costs.
6. **Disposal / Decommissioning Phase** — Dismantling, environmental remediation, scrap value (negative cost), final disposal.

> [!NOTE]
> **Maintenance Engineering Connection:** For PERUT523, the *Operation + Maintenance phases* typically contribute **60%–85%** of the total LCC for industrial equipment. This is the strongest academic justification for proactive maintenance.

---

## 2.2 The Five Building-Block Time-Value-of-Money Factors

These are the *engines* of LCCA. Every cost in any year must be converted using one of these factors before summation.

### Factor 1: Single Payment Compound Amount (F/P, i, n)
Finds the future value of a present sum.

$$F = P(1 + i)^{n}$$

### Factor 2: Single Payment Present Worth (P/F, i, n)
Finds the present value of a future sum.

$$P = F(1 + i)^{-n}$$

### Factor 3: Uniform Series Present Worth (P/A, i, n)
Finds the present value of a uniform annual series.

$$P = A \cdot \frac{(1 + i)^{n} - 1}{i(1 + i)^{n}}$$

### Factor 4: Uniform Series Future Worth (F/A, i, n)
Finds the future value of a uniform annual series.

$$F = A \cdot \frac{(1 + i)^{n} - 1}{i}$$

### Factor 5: Capital Recovery (A/P, i, n)
Converts a present sum to an equivalent uniform annual cost.

$$A = P \cdot \frac{i(1 + i)^{n}}{(1 + i)^{n} - 1}$$

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below is **high-yield** for KTU 2024 ESE. Memorize the formulas, the boundary limits, and the units. CRITICAL: The vertical pipe symbol `\vert` is used for absolute value to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Name | Formula | Limit as $i \to 0$ | Limit as $n \to \infty$ | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Single Payment PW | $P = F(1+i)^{-n}$ | $P \to F$ | $P \to 0$ | Disposal / salvage discount |
| 2 | Single Payment FW | $F = P(1+i)^{n}$ | $F \to P$ | $F \to \infty$ | Inflation of spare cost |
| 3 | Uniform Series PW | $P = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^{n}-1}{i(1+i)^{n}}$ | $P \to nA$ | $P \to A/i$ | Annual O\&M cost lumping |
| 4 | Uniform Series FW | $F = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^{n}-1}{i}$ | $F \to nA$ | $F \to \infty$ | Replacement reserve fund |
| 5 | Capital Recovery | $A = P \cdot \dfrac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n}-1}$ | $A \to P/n$ | $A \to P \cdot i$ | Equivalent Annual Cost method |
| 6 | Sinking Fund | $A = F \cdot \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^{n}-1}$ | $A \to F/n$ | $A \to 0$ | Depreciation-based reserve |
| 7 | LCC Master Equation | $LCC = C_{0} + P_{O\&M} - P_{salvage} + P_{disposal}$ | — | — | Total cost comparison |
| 8 | Fisher Real Rate | $i_{r} = (1+i_{n})/(1+f) - 1$ | $i_{r} \approx i_{n} - f$ | — | Inflation-adjusted discount |
| 9 | EAC (Annualised LCC) | $EAC = LCC \cdot (A/P, i, n)$ | — | — | Unequal-life alternatives |

> **Units Convention:** All monetary values must be in the *same currency unit* (₹, $, €). The interest rate $i$ is **dimensionless** (decimal form: 10% = 0.10). Time $n$ is in **years** unless stated otherwise.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of LCCA in Engineering

- **Procurement Decisions:** Comparing OEM quotes where one is cheaper to buy but costlier to run.
- **Maintenance Strategy Selection:** Justifying the *extra cost* of a predictive maintenance program by showing the LCC reduction.
- **Safety Engineering:** Evaluating the LCC trade-off of installing a fire suppression system (high initial cost) vs the *avoided cost* of fire incidents.
- **Renewable vs Conventional:** Solar plant (high CAPEX, low OPEX) vs Diesel generator (low CAPEX, high OPEX).
- **Infrastructure:** Highway pavement selection (concrete vs bitumen) over a 40-year horizon.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Uniform Series Present Worth Factor (P/A, i, n)

This is the most heavily tested derivation in PERUT523 LCCA problems. We derive it from first principles using the geometric series.

### Step 1 — Set up the cash flow stream
Consider an equivalent annual cost $A$ occurring at the **end** of each year for $n$ years, with interest rate $i$ per year. We want the single present value $P$ at year 0.

The cash flow diagram (textual representation):
- Year 0: $P$ (unknown, what we want)
- Year 1: $A$
- Year 2: $A$
- ...
- Year $n$: $A$

### Step 2 — Discount each $A$ back to year 0
Using the single-payment present worth factor $(1+i)^{-n}$ for the $k$-th year payment:

$$
P = \frac{A}{1+i} + \frac{A}{(1+i)^{2}} + \frac{A}{(1+i)^{3}} + \cdots + \frac{A}{(1+i)^{n}}
$$

### Step 3 — Factor out the common term
The first term $A/(1+i)$ is common. Factor it out:

$$
P = \frac{A}{1+i} \left[ 1 + \frac{1}{1+i} + \frac{1}{(1+i)^{2}} + \cdots + \frac{1}{(1+i)^{n-1}} \right]
$$

### Step 4 — Identify the geometric series
The bracketed term is a **finite geometric series** with first term $u = 1$, common ratio $r = 1/(1+i)$, and $n$ terms. The closed-form sum is:

$$
S_{n} = u \cdot \frac{1 - r^{n}}{1 - r}
$$

Substituting $u = 1$ and $r = (1+i)^{-1}$:

$$
S_{n} = \frac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{1 - (1+i)^{-1}}
$$

### Step 5 — Simplify the denominator
Multiply numerator and denominator by $(1+i)$:

$$
1 - (1+i)^{-1} = \frac{(1+i) - 1}{1+i} = \frac{i}{1+i}
$$

Therefore:

$$
S_{n} = \frac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i/(1+i)} = \frac{(1+i) \left[ 1 - (1+i)^{-n} \right]}{i}
$$

### Step 6 — Final substitution back into the expression for $P$
Substituting the cleaned $S_{n}$ back into the equation from Step 3:

$$
P = \frac{A}{1+i} \cdot \frac{(1+i) \left[ 1 - (1+i)^{-n} \right]}{i}
$$

The $(1+i)$ terms cancel:

$$
\boxed{P = A \cdot \frac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i} = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i(1+i)^{n}}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two forms?** The left form $A \cdot [1 - (1+i)^{-n}] / i$ is computationally *faster* by hand because you avoid computing $(1+i)^{n}$ in both numerator and denominator. KTU examiners sometimes give partial credit for the cleaner form.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Capital Recovery Factor (A/P, i, n)

The capital recovery factor is the algebraic inverse of the uniform series present worth factor. We confirm this below.

### Step 1 — Take the (P/A, i, n) result
We have $P = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i(1+i)^{n}}$

### Step 2 — Solve for $A$ in terms of $P$
Invert the fraction by swapping numerator and denominator:

$$
A = P \cdot \frac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}
$$

### Step 3 — Box the final form

$$
\boxed{A = P \cdot \frac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}}
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: LCC of Two Pumps

> **Problem Statement (Model KTU Question):**
> A process plant must choose between Pump X and Pump Y.
> - **Pump X:** Initial cost = ₹5,00,000; Annual O\&M = ₹1,20,000; Salvage = ₹50,000 at year 10.
> - **Pump Y:** Initial cost = ₹8,00,000; Annual O\&M = ₹60,000; Salvage = ₹1,00,000 at year 10.
> - **Interest rate** $i = 10\%$ per year. **Study period** $N = 10$ years.
> Determine which pump is more economical using **Present Worth of Life Cycle Cost**.

### Step 1 — Compute the necessary factors at $i = 10\%$, $n = 10$ years

The single payment present worth factor:

$$
(P/F, 10\%, 10) = (1.10)^{-10} = 0.3855
$$

The uniform series present worth factor:

$$
(P/A, 10\%, 10) = \frac{(1.10)^{10} - 1}{0.10 \cdot (1.10)^{10}} = \frac{1.5937}{0.25937} = 5.7590
$$

### Step 2 — Compute LCC for Pump X
Initial cost (already at year 0):

$$
C_{0,X} = 5{,}00{,}000
$$

Annual O\&M discounted to present worth:

$$
P_{O\&M,X} = 1{,}20{,}000 \cdot 5.7590 = 6{,}91{,}080
$$

Salvage value (subtracted, because it is a benefit):

$$
P_{salvage,X} = 50{,}000 \cdot 0.3855 = 19{,}275
$$

Total LCC for Pump X:

$$
LCC_X = 5{,}00{,}000 + 6{,}91{,}080 - 19{,}275 = 11{,}71{,}805 \text{ INR}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute LCC for Pump Y
Initial cost (already at year 0):

$$
C_{0,Y} = 8{,}00{,}000
$$

Annual O\&M discounted to present worth:

$$
P_{O\&M,Y} = 60{,}000 \cdot 5.7590 = 3{,}45{,}540
$$

Salvage value (subtracted):

$$
P_{salvage,Y} = 1{,}00{,}000 \cdot 0.3855 = 38{,}550
$$

Total LCC for Pump Y:

$$
LCC_Y = 8{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}45{,}540 - 38{,}550 = 11{,}06{,}990 \text{ INR}
$$

### Step 4 — Decision

$$
LCC_Y = 11{,}06{,}990 < LCC_X = 11{,}71{,}805
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** **Pump Y is selected** because it has a lower Present Worth of Life Cycle Cost, even though it costs ₹3,00,000 more to buy upfront. The savings in annual O\&M over 10 years at 10% interest outweigh the higher initial investment. *This is the textbook example of why LCCA is essential.*

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Method

| Situation | Recommended LCC Method | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Alternatives have **equal lives** | **Present Worth (PW)** | Simplest computation |
| Alternatives have **unequal lives** | **Equivalent Annual Cost (EAC)** | Annualisation removes the unequal-life bias |
| Only a **single project** is evaluated | **PW of LCC** | Decision threshold is the budget |
| A **minimum attractive return** is mandated | **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** on LCC savings | Matches corporate hurdle rate |
| **Long horizon** ($N > 25$ years) | **Annual Worth (AW)** | Discounting errors accumulate in PW |
| Inflation is **explicitly variable** | **Real-dollar PW** with $i_{r}$ | Avoids inflated cost illusion |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Asset Life Cycle Cost — Component Block Diagram

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the six canonical LCC components and their typical *weight distribution* in an industrial asset's total cost.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[LCC Total Cost of Ownership] --> B[Phase 1: Acquisition CAPEX]
    A --> C[Phase 2: Installation Commissioning]
    A --> D[Phase 3: Operating Costs OPEX]
    A --> E[Phase 4: Maintenance Costs]
    A --> F[Phase 5: Overhaul Mid-Life Capital]
    A --> G[Phase 6: Disposal End-of-Life]

    B --> B1[Purchase Price 10 to 20 percent]
    C --> C1[Freight Foundation Training 2 to 5 percent]
    D --> D1[Energy Consumables Operators 25 to 40 percent]
    E --> E1[Preventive Corrective Predictive 20 to 35 percent]
    F --> F1[Mid-Life Major Overhaul 5 to 15 percent]
    G --> G1[Dismantling Scrap Net 0 to 5 percent]
```

---

## 4.2 LCCA Methodology — Functional Architecture Flow

This block diagram maps the **decision-flow logic** that a maintenance engineer follows when performing a complete LCCA per KTU/PERUT523 protocol.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start LCCA Study] --> S1[Define Alternatives and Study Horizon N]
    S1 --> S2[Identify all Cost Categories CAPEX OPEX Maintenance Disposal]
    S2 --> S3[Estimate Cash Flows Year by Year]
    S3 --> S4[Select Discount Rate i real or nominal]
    S4 --> S5[Apply Time Value of Money Factor P/F or P/A]
    S5 --> S6[Compute PW of LCC for each alternative]
    S6 --> S7{Compare PW of LCC}
    S7 --> S8[Alternative with Minimum LCC is selected]
    S7 --> S9[Perform Sensitivity Analysis on i and N]
    S9 --> S10[Final Recommendation to Management]
    S8 --> S10
    S10 --> S11[End]
```

---

## 4.3 Cash Flow Timeline — Pump X vs Pump Y Schematic

The following diagram shows the *conceptual* cash flow comparison for the worked numerical example in SECTION 3.3. Initial outflows are at $t = 0$ and $t = 1, 2, ..., 10$ for O\&M, with salvage inflows at $t = 10$.

```mermaid
gantt
    title Conceptual Cash Flow Profile for Two Pump Alternatives
    dateFormat YYYY
    axisFormat Y%Y
    section Pump X Lower CAPEX
    Initial Cost Year 0          :a1, 2024, 1y
    Annual O&M Years 1 to 10     :a2, after a1, 10y
    Salvage at Year 10           :a3, 2034, 1y
    section Pump Y Higher CAPEX
    Initial Cost Year 0          :b1, 2024, 1y
    Annual O&M Years 1 to 10     :b2, after b1, 10y
    Salvage at Year 10           :b3, 2034, 1y
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Gantt:** Pump Y has a *bigger* initial block but *smaller* recurring O&M blocks. The LCCA calculation mathematically proves that the present-worth of the *total* area under Pump Y is smaller.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1.** [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Define Life Cycle Cost (LCC) of an industrial asset. List any four components of LCC.**

**Model Answer:**
Life Cycle Cost is the total monetary expense incurred on a physical asset from its acquisition to its final disposal, expressed in present-worth terms using the time value of money.

**Four components of LCC:**
1. **Acquisition cost (CAPEX):** Initial purchase, installation, and commissioning.
2. **Operating cost (OPEX):** Energy, consumables, operator wages, insurance.
3. **Maintenance cost:** Preventive, predictive, and corrective maintenance expenditure over the life.
4. **Disposal cost:** Dismantling, environmental remediation, minus salvage/scrap value.

> **Marking Scheme:** 1 mark for definition + 0.5 mark × 4 = 2 marks for components. Total 3 marks.

---

### **Question A2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**State the formula for Equivalent Annual Cost (EAC). Why is EAC preferred when comparing alternatives with unequal lives?**

**Model Answer:**
$$
EAC = LCC \times (A/P, i, n) = LCC \times \frac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}
$$

EAC is preferred for unequal-life alternatives because it **converts the entire life cycle cost into an equivalent uniform annual cost**, making alternatives directly comparable on a *per-year basis* without the bias introduced by different time horizons. EAC is independent of the arbitrary study-period choice.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** In Module-3 questions, students are given an internal choice between two 14-mark questions. Each 14-mark question has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. The full worked solutions for *both* alternatives are provided below so that you can practise either path.

---

### **Question B-A (14 Marks).** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

A manufacturing unit is evaluating two electric motors for a continuous-duty application:

| Parameter | Motor M1 | Motor M2 |
|---|---|---|
| Initial Cost | ₹1,50,000 | ₹2,20,000 |
| Annual Energy Cost | ₹45,000 | ₹28,000 |
| Annual Maintenance Cost | ₹12,000 | ₹7,000 |
| Salvage Value (at year 12) | ₹20,000 | ₹35,000 |
| Useful Life | 12 years | 12 years |

The discount rate is $i = 12\%$ per year. Use the **Present Worth method** to find the LCC of each motor and recommend the better option.

#### **Part (a)** [7 marks] — Compute the LCC of Motor M1.

**Solution:**

At $i = 12\%$, $n = 12$ years, the standard factors are:

$$
(P/A, 12\%, 12) = \frac{(1.12)^{12} - 1}{0.12 \cdot (1.12)^{12}} = \frac{2.7731}{0.33277} = 6.1944
$$

$$
(P/F, 12\%, 12) = (1.12)^{-12} = 0.3606
$$

**Step 1 — Initial cost** (already at year 0):

$$
C_{0,M1} = 1{,}50{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — Annual Energy + Maintenance cost** = ₹45,000 + ₹12,000 = ₹57,000 per year.

Present worth of annual recurring cost:

$$
P_{O\&M,M1} = 57{,}000 \times 6.1944 = 3{,}53{,}081
$$

**Step 3 — Salvage value** (subtracted, because it is an inflow):

$$
P_{salvage,M1} = 20{,}000 \times 0.3606 = 7{,}212
$$

**Step 4 — Total LCC of M1:**

$$
LCC_{M1} = 1{,}50{,}000 + 3{,}53{,}081 - 7{,}212 = \boxed{4{,}95{,}869 \text{ INR}}
$$

#### **Part (b)** [7 marks] — Compute the LCC of Motor M2 and recommend.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Initial cost:**

$$
C_{0,M2} = 2{,}20{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — Annual cost** = ₹28,000 + ₹7,000 = ₹35,000 per year.

$$
P_{O\&M,M2} = 35{,}000 \times 6.1944 = 2{,}16{,}804
$$

**Step 3 — Salvage value:**

$$
P_{salvage,M2} = 35{,}000 \times 0.3606 = 12{,}621
$$

**Step 4 — Total LCC of M2:**

$$
LCC_{M2} = 2{,}20{,}000 + 2{,}16{,}804 - 12{,}621 = \boxed{4{,}24{,}183 \text{ INR}}
$$

**Recommendation:**

$$
LCC_{M2} = 4{,}24{,}183 < LCC_{M1} = 4{,}95{,}869
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Motor M2 is recommended.** It saves ₹71,686 in present worth over 12 years. Even though M2 is ₹70,000 costlier upfront, its superior energy efficiency and lower maintenance more than compensate.

---

### **Question B-B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice).** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

A factory must select between an **In-House Maintenance Contract** (Option A) and an **OEM Annual Maintenance Contract** (Option B) for a critical CNC machine. The data are:

| Parameter | Option A In-House | Option B OEM |
|---|---|---|
| Initial Setup Cost (year 0) | ₹3,00,000 | ₹0 |
| Annual Manpower & Spare Cost | ₹1,80,000 | ₹2,40,000 |
| Major Overhaul at year 6 | ₹1,20,000 | ₹0 |
| Salvage at year 10 | ₹40,000 | ₹0 |
| Study Period | 10 years | 10 years |

Discount rate $i = 10\%$. Compare using the **Present Worth method**.

#### **Part (a)** [7 marks] — Compute the LCC of Option A.

**Solution:**

At $i = 10\%$, $n = 10$ years, the factors are:

$$
(P/A, 10\%, 10) = 5.7590, \quad (P/F, 10\%, 6) = (1.10)^{-6} = 0.5645, \quad (P/F, 10\%, 10) = 0.3855
$$

**Step 1 — Initial cost:** ₹3,00,000.

**Step 2 — Annual manpower and spares:**

$$
P_{annual,A} = 1{,}80{,}000 \times 5.7590 = 10{,}36{,}620
$$

**Step 3 — Major overhaul at year 6** (lumpy cost, discounted to year 0):

$$
P_{overhaul,A} = 1{,}20{,}000 \times 0.5645 = 67{,}740
$$

**Step 4 — Salvage at year 10** (inflow, subtracted):

$$
P_{salvage,A} = 40{,}000 \times 0.3855 = 15{,}420
$$

**Step 5 — Total LCC of Option A:**

$$
LCC_A = 3{,}00{,}000 + 10{,}36{,}620 + 67{,}740 - 15{,}420 = \boxed{13{,}88{,}940 \text{ INR}}
$$

#### **Part (b)** [7 marks] — Compute the LCC of Option B and recommend.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Initial cost:** ₹0 (no setup).

**Step 2 — Annual OEM contract cost** = ₹2,40,000 per year.

$$
P_{annual,B} = 2{,}40{,}000 \times 5.7590 = 13{,}82{,}160
$$

**Step 3 — Total LCC of Option B** (no overhaul, no salvage):

$$
LCC_B = 0 + 13{,}82{,}160 + 0 - 0 = \boxed{13{,}82{,}160 \text{ INR}}
$$

**Recommendation:**

$$
LCC_B = 13{,}82{,}160 < LCC_A = 13{,}88{,}940
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Option B (OEM Contract) is recommended** by a small margin of ₹6,780 in present worth. However, this conclusion is sensitive to the discount rate — a sensitivity analysis (recommended in any KTU answer) at $i = 8\%$ or $i = 12\%$ should be performed to validate the decision.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to discount the salvage value** — Salvage is a *future* inflow, so it MUST be multiplied by $(P/F, i, n)$. Omitting this inflates LCC by 1–2 marks worth.
> 2. **Sign error on salvage** — Salvage is a *negative* cost. Adding it instead of subtracting it gives wrong LCC. *Always subtract salvage.*
> 3. **Mixing up $i = 10\%$ as 10 instead of 0.10** — A decimal-place slip changes the entire answer.
> 4. **Not stating the factors explicitly** — KTU examiners award a "factor mark" if you write $(P/A, 10\%, 10) = 5.7590$ before substituting. *Always show the factor.*
> 5. **Lumpy mid-life costs (overhauls) at the wrong year** — A cost at year 6 needs $(P/F, i, 6)$, not $(P/F, i, 10)$. Read the problem carefully.
> 6. **Skipping the recommendation sentence** — The 14-mark question always has a 1-mark allotment for the final decision sentence. Don't end the answer at the calculation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for LCCA (PERUT523, Module 3):**
>
> - **LCC =** Acquisition + Σ Discounted O\&M − Discounted Salvage + Discounted Disposal
> - **Six LCC Phases:** Concept → Acquisition → Operation → Maintenance → Overhaul → Disposal
> - **Five core TVM factors:** $(F/P)$, $(P/F)$, $(P/A)$, $(F/A)$, $(A/P)$
> - **Always convert** all future cash flows to a *common* time reference (Year 0 for PW, or annual for EAC)
> - **Discount rate** $i$ is dimensionless (decimal). If inflation is given separately, use the **Fisher equation**: $i_{r} = (1 + i_{n}) / (1 + f) - 1$
> - **Salvage value** is *subtracted* from LCC (it is a benefit)
> - **Equal-life alternatives** → use Present Worth of LCC
> - **Unequal-life alternatives** → use Equivalent Annual Cost (EAC)
> - **Lumpy mid-life overhauls** → discount individually using $(P/F, i, k)$ for year $k$
> - **Final step:** Always state a clear **recommendation sentence** comparing the two LCC values
> - **Sensitivity analysis** on $i$ and $N$ is good practice and earns extra credit
> - **Avoid the trap:** The cheapest initial cost is *not* always the cheapest LCC — LCCA exists precisely to expose this fallacy

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fault-Tree Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT523 - MAINTENANCE AND SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fault-Tree Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Fault-Tree Analysis (FTA)** is a deductive, top-down, graphical failure-analysis methodology used in safety and reliability engineering to determine the combinations of basic component failures, human errors, and external events that can lead to an undesired system-level event (the *Top Event*). It was originally conceived at Bell Laboratories in **1962** by **H. A. Watson** for the U.S. Air Force Minuteman missile launch control system and was later formalized in the U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission document **NUREG-0492**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Board-Examiner phrasing):**
> *Fault-Tree Analysis is a systematic, deductive analytical technique in which an undesired state of the system (Top Event) is logically decomposed, using Boolean logic gates, into its contributing lower-level causes (Basic Events), thereby enabling the qualitative identification of Minimal Cut Sets and the quantitative computation of the Top Event probability.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

Imagine a detective standing at the scene of a railway accident (the **TOP EVENT**). Instead of asking "What could go wrong?" everywhere, the detective asks only one question at a time: *"What must have happened JUST BEFORE this accident to make it possible?"* 

- If the answer is "either A or B could have caused it" → the detective draws an **OR gate**.
- If the answer is "BOTH A AND B were needed" → the detective draws an **AND gate**.
- The detective keeps asking "Why?" repeatedly until the answers are *things you cannot or do not want to break down further* (e.g., "brake fluid leaked", "signal bulb fused") — these are **Basic Events**.

By the end, the detective has a *tree* of logic gates leading from tiny component failures up to the disaster. This tree is the **Fault Tree**.

> [!NOTE]
> **One-line Memory Hook for KTU Viva:** 
> *FTA = Deductive (Top-Down) + Boolean Logic + Cut-Sets → Risk Quantification.*

### 1.3 Key Terminology Mapping

| KTU Term | Meaning | Standard Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| **Top Event** | The undesired system failure being analysed | Rectangle at the apex |
| **Intermediate Event** | A failure event that can be further decomposed | Rectangle in the middle layer |
| **Basic Event (BE)** | A leaf-level component failure requiring no further analysis | Circle |
| **Undeveloped Event** | A leaf event whose cause is intentionally not expanded | Diamond |
| **Conditioning Event** | A qualifier (e.g., "On", "Off", "Cold", "Hot") | Ellipse |
| **Transfer Symbol** | Pointer to a sub-tree on another sheet | Triangle (out/in) |
| **AND Gate** | Output occurs **only if ALL** inputs occur | Flat-bottomed D shape |
| **OR Gate** | Output occurs if **any one** input occurs | Curved shield shape |

### 1.4 Governing Standards & Constants

- **NUREG-0492** — U.S. NRC standard for fault-tree methodology.
- **IEC 61025** — International standard for fault-tree analysis.
- **MIL-STD-1629** — U.S. military reliability procedure.
- The natural logarithm base **e ≈ 2.71828** appears in the exponential reliability law.
- The **NEQUAL notation** $(N_E)$ for Boolean combination counting.

> [!TIP]
> For the KTU 2024 exam, always cite *IEC 61025* if asked for the governing standard — it is the international reference and the most likely mark-scoring answer.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Underlying Logical Architecture of a Fault Tree

A fault tree is a **directed acyclic graph (DAG)** in which:

- The **root** is the *Top Event*.
- The **edges** are Boolean logic relations (AND/OR/NOT/XOR/etc.).
- The **leaves** are *Basic Events* with assigned failure probabilities $q_i$.

The construction proceeds in **four KTU-recognised phases**:

1. **Define the Top Event** — A single, precisely stated undesired condition (e.g., *Pump P-101 fails to deliver flow*). Ambiguity here invalidates the entire tree.
2. **Boundary & Level Definition** — Decide *what is in the system* and *what is external*. Document the *operating phase* (Start-up, Steady-state, Shutdown).
3. **Immediate-Event Analysis** — For the Top Event, identify ALL independent failures that *by themselves* could cause the Top Event. Connect them with an **OR gate**. Then for the *combination* failures, use an **AND gate**.
4. **Recursive Decomposition** — Continue expanding every Intermediate Event downward until only Basic Events remain.

### 2.2 Boolean Algebra Foundation (the Engine of FTA)

Every gate in the tree is equivalent to a Boolean expression. FTA evaluation relies on **Boole's axioms**:

$$A + A = A \quad \text{(Idempotence — OR)}$$

$$A \cdot A = A \quad \text{(Idempotence — AND)}$$

$$A + \bar{A} = 1 \quad \text{(Absorption complement — guaranteed OR)}$$

$$A \cdot \bar{A} = 0 \quad \text{(Contradiction — impossible AND)}$$

$$\bar{(A \cdot B)} = \bar{A} + \bar{B} \quad \text{(De Morgan's Law — critical for AND→OR conversion)}$$

$$\bar{(A + B)} = \bar{A} \cdot \bar{B} \quad \text{(De Morgan's Law — critical for OR→AND conversion)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why De Morgan matters in FTA:** The *Success Tree* (no failure) is the complement of the *Fault Tree*. To convert a success expression to a failure expression (or vice-versa), De Morgan's laws are applied gate-by-gate, AND/OR are interchanged, and inputs are complemented.

### 2.3 Minimal Cut Sets (MCS) — Qualitative Output

A **Cut Set** is any combination of Basic Events whose simultaneous occurrence *causes* the Top Event.
A **Minimal Cut Set (MCS)** is a cut set from which *no event can be removed without losing the property of causing the Top Event*.

> [!NOTE]
> *Minimal* does not mean "smallest in size" — it means "non-reducible". A 3-event MCS is still minimal even if a 2-event MCS also exists.

**Identification Algorithm (Kiu & McCormick / Top-Down substitution):**
1. Replace each gate by its Boolean expression in terms of its inputs.
2. Apply the **distribution law** repeatedly: $\;A\cdot(B+C) = A\cdot B + A\cdot C$.
3. Apply absorption and simplification rules to remove redundancy.
4. The remaining disjoint or non-redundant terms are the **Minimal Cut Sets**.

### 2.4 Path Sets & Dual Trees

- A **Path Set** is a set of components whose *non-failure* guarantees success.
- The set of **Minimal Path Sets (MPS)** forms the *Success Tree* complement.
- The MCS of the Fault Tree and the MPS of the Success Tree are **duals** obtained by De Morgan transformation.

### 2.5 Quantitative Analysis — Computing the Top Event Probability

For *independent* Basic Events with failure probabilities $q_i$:

**OR gate output probability (rare-event approximation for small $q$):**

$$P_{\text{OR}} \approx \sum_{i=1}^{n} q_i$$

**AND gate output probability (exact, for small $q$ also accurate):**

$$P_{\text{AND}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} q_i$$

**Exact OR-gate probability (inclusion-exclusion form, used in KTU derivations):**

$$P_{\text{OR}} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - q_i)$$

**For a system expressed as a sum of disjoint MCS terms, the exact Top-Event probability is:**

$$P_{\text{Top}} = \sum_{i=1}^{m} P(\text{MCS}_i) - \sum_{i<j} P(\text{MCS}_i \cap \text{MCS}_j) + \sum_{i<j<k} P(\text{MCS}_i \cap \text{MCS}_j \cap \text{MCS}_k) - \dots$$

**The Rare-Event Approximation (used widely by KTU examiners when $q_i \le 10^{-3}$):**

$$P_{\text{Top}} \approx \sum_{i=1}^{m} \prod_{x \in \text{MCS}_i} q_x$$

### 2.6 Importance Measures (Board-Favourite Sub-Topic)

| Measure | Formula | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Fussell-Vesely (F-V)** | $\displaystyle \text{FV}_i = \frac{P(\text{Top contains }i)}{P_{\text{Top}}}$ | Fraction of system failure probability involving component $i$ |
| **Birnbaum** | $\displaystyle B_i = \frac{\partial P_{\text{Top}}}{\partial q_i}$ | Sensitivity of $P_{\text{Top}}$ to component $i$ failure rate |
| **Risk Achievement Worth (RAW)** | $\displaystyle \text{RAW}_i = \frac{P_{\text{Top}}(q_i=1)}{P_{\text{Top}}}$ | Increase in $P_{\text{Top}}$ if $i$ is forced to fail (1.0) |
| **Risk Reduction Worth (RRW)** | $\displaystyle \text{RRW}_i = \frac{P_{\text{Top}}}{P_{\text{Top}}(q_i=0)}$ | Benefit of making $i$ perfectly reliable |

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation | Boundary / Domain | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reliability of component | $R_i = e^{-\lambda_i t}$ | $\lambda_i t \ge 0$ | Dimensionless probability |
| 2 | Failure probability | $q_i = 1 - R_i = 1 - e^{-\lambda_i t}$ | $0 \le q_i \le 1$ | Dimensionless |
| 3 | Series (OR in fault tree) | $P_{\text{Top}} = 1 - \prod_i (1 - q_i)$ | All $q_i$ independent | Probability |
| 4 | Parallel (AND in fault tree) | $P_{\text{Top}} = \prod_i q_i$ | All $q_i$ independent | Probability |
| 5 | Rare-event approx. | $P_{\text{Top}} \approx \sum_k \prod_{x \in \text{MCS}_k} q_x$ | $q_i \le 10^{-3}$ | Valid only in rare-event regime |
| 6 | De Morgan conversion | $\overline{A \cdot B} = \bar A + \bar B$ | Boolean algebra | Used for dual trees |
| 7 | Fussell-Vesely | $\text{FV}_i = P(\text{Top}\vert i)/P_{\text{Top}}$ | $0 \le \text{FV}_i \le 1$ | Importance factor |
| 8 | MTTF (exponential) | $\text{MTTF} = 1/\lambda$ | Constant failure rate | Hours / cycles |
| 9 | Minimal Cut Set order | Order = number of BEs in set | Integer $\ge 1$ | Singletons are most critical |
| 10 | Unavailability of repairable system | $Q \approx \lambda / \mu$ | $\lambda \ll \mu$ | Dimensionless |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Always state the **assumptions** before applying any formula: *"Assuming independent, non-repairing, exponentially distributed failures and a rare-event regime…"* — this single line adds 1 mark in the KTU valuation key.

### 2.8 Real-World Utility of FTA in Engineering

- **Nuclear Reactor Safety** — Reactor scram systems, ECCS analysis (NUREG-1855).
- **Aerospace** — Launch vehicle failure analysis (NASA, ISRO GSLV Mk-III).
- **Chemical Plants** — HAZOP complement for LOPA quantification.
- **Automotive** — ISO 26262 functional safety at the ASIL level.
- **Pressure-vessel & Boiler** — Indian Boiler Regulation (IBR) compliance.
- **Software Safety** — Software fault tree analysis (SFTA) for embedded systems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Minimal Cut Set Identification by Boolean Reduction

**Problem Statement:** A system has Top Event $T$ described by the Boolean expression:
$$T = A \cdot (B + C) + D$$
Identify all Minimal Cut Sets.

**Step-by-Step Solution (no steps skipped):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= A \cdot (B + C) + D \\[4pt]
  &= A \cdot B + A \cdot C + D \quad \text{(Distributive law applied)} \\[4pt]
  &= AB + AC + D \quad \text{(Conjunction notation: }A\cdot B = AB\text{)} \\[4pt]
  &\text{Absorption check: } AB \text{ is not absorbed by } AC, AC, D \text{ or } AB. \\[4pt]
  &\text{Hence no further reduction is possible.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result — The Minimal Cut Sets are:**

$$
\text{MCS}_1 = \{A, B\}, \quad \text{MCS}_2 = \{A, C\}, \quad \text{MCS}_3 = \{D\}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Note that $\{A, B, C\}$ is a cut set (because $A$ AND $B$ AND $C$ all failing certainly produces $T$) but it is **NOT** minimal — removing $A$ still leaves $\{B, C\}$ which (in this expression) is *not* a cut set; but removing $B$ leaves $\{A, C\}$ which IS a cut set, hence the original 3-event set is not minimal.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Quantitative Top Event Probability (Rare-Event Approximation)

**Problem Statement:** For the system in Derivation 1, given failure probabilities:
$$q_A = 0.01, \quad q_B = 0.02, \quad q_C = 0.03, \quad q_D = 0.005$$
Compute $P_{\text{Top}}$.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{MCS}_1) &= q_A \cdot q_B = 0.01 \times 0.02 = 2.0 \times 10^{-4} \\[4pt]
P(\text{MCS}_2) &= q_A \cdot q_C = 0.01 \times 0.03 = 3.0 \times 10^{-4} \\[4pt]
P(\text{MCS}_3) &= q_D \quad\quad\;\; = 5.0 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}
$$

Because $q_D$ alone is an order of magnitude larger than the AND combinations, the **rare-event approximation** is valid for the AND terms but $D$ is still significant. We apply the exact OR formula over independent events:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{Top, exact}} &= 1 - (1 - P_1)(1 - P_2)(1 - P_3) \\[4pt]
&= 1 - (1 - 2\times 10^{-4})(1 - 3\times 10^{-4})(1 - 5\times 10^{-3}) \\[4pt]
&= 1 - (0.9998)(0.9997)(0.995) \\[4pt]
&= 1 - 0.994504 \quad \text{(product of the three terms)} \\[4pt]
&= 5.496 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Using the rare-event approximation (sum of MCS probabilities):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{Top, approx}} &\approx P_1 + P_2 + P_3 \\[4pt]
&= 2.0 \times 10^{-4} + 3.0 \times 10^{-4} + 5.0 \times 10^{-3} \\[4pt]
&= 5.5 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Relative error:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon &= \frac{P_{\text{exact}} - P_{\text{approx}}}{P_{\text{exact}}} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
&= \frac{5.496 \times 10^{-3} - 5.500 \times 10^{-3}}{5.496 \times 10^{-3}} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
&\approx -0.073\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The rare-event approximation error is **< 0.1%** — fully acceptable for KTU-grade problems.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Fussell-Vesely Importance Calculation

**Given:** The system in Derivation 1. Compute the Fussell-Vesely importance of component $D$.

**Definition applied:**

$$\text{FV}_D = \frac{P(\text{Top event occurs via a cut set containing } D)}{P(\text{Top}}}$$

**Step-by-step:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{via } D) &= q_D = 5.0 \times 10^{-3} \\[4pt]
P_{\text{Top}} &= 5.5 \times 10^{-3} \quad \text{(from previous derivation)} \\[4pt]
\text{FV}_D &= \frac{5.0 \times 10^{-3}}{5.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.9091
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering interpretation:** Approximately **91%** of the total system failure probability is attributable to component $D$ alone. Maintenance resources should be prioritized on $D$ — this is the **central insight** FTA provides for safety engineering decisions.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Conversion of Fault Tree to Success Tree (De Morgan Application)

**Given:** A fault tree whose output is:
$$T = A \cdot (B + C)$$
Derive the equivalent success tree Boolean expression and its Minimal Path Sets.

**Step-by-step De Morgan transformation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar T &= \overline{A \cdot (B + C)} \\[4pt]
      &= \bar A + \overline{(B + C)} \quad \text{(De Morgan's law applied to the AND)} \\[4pt]
      &= \bar A + \bar B \cdot \bar C \quad \text{(De Morgan's law applied to the inner OR)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Minimal Path Sets identified from $\bar T$:**

$$
\text{MPS}_1 = \{\bar A\}, \quad \text{MPS}_2 = \{\bar B, \bar C\}
$$

**Meaning:** The system succeeds if (component $A$ is working) **OR** (both $B$ AND $C$ are working).

> [!NOTE]
> Notice how AND ↔ OR interchange under De Morgan's transformation. This is the **dual-tree** principle.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparing FTA with FMEA (Frequently asked in KTU 2024)

| Comparison Axis | Fault-Tree Analysis (FTA) | Failure Mode & Effects Analysis (FMEA) |
|---|---|---|
| Approach | Deductive (top-down) | Inductive (bottom-up) |
| Origin | Top Event / System-level failure | Component-level failure modes |
| Logical Engine | Boolean algebra + probability | Tabular severity / occurrence ranking |
| Best Used For | Complex systems with redundant paths | Detailed component-failure review |
| Output | Minimal Cut Sets, $P_{\text{Top}}$, Importance | Risk Priority Number (RPN) |
| Strength | Quantifies top-event probability | Identifies local failure mechanisms |
| Limitation | Hard to construct for very large systems | Misses cross-component interactions |

### 3.6 KTU-Style Numerical Worked Example (Full Solution)

**Question (Board pattern):** *A safety system consists of two pumps in parallel feeding a header. Each pump has a failure probability of $q = 0.01$ per demand. The header fails with probability $0.005$ per demand independent of pump state. Construct the fault tree, list minimal cut sets, and compute the top-event (header failure) probability.*

**Solution:**

*Step 1 — Identify components and events.*

Let $A$ = Pump 1 fails, $B$ = Pump 2 fails, $H$ = Header fails.

*Step 2 — Construct the fault tree logically.*

The header is supplied if **at least one** pump works. Header failure therefore requires **both pumps fail AND the header itself fails**, OR alternatively the header fails regardless of the pumps.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= H + (A \cdot B)
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 3 — Identify Minimal Cut Sets.*

$$
\text{MCS}_1 = \{H\}, \quad \text{MCS}_2 = \{A, B\}
$$

*Step 4 — Compute top-event probability.*

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{Top, exact}} &= 1 - (1 - q_H)(1 - q_A \cdot q_B) \\[4pt]
&= 1 - (1 - 0.005)(1 - 0.01 \times 0.01) \\[4pt]
&= 1 - (0.995)(0.9999) \\[4pt]
&= 1 - 0.99490 \quad (\text{exact product: } 0.995 \times 0.9999 = 0.9949005) \\[4pt]
&= 5.0995 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 5 — Compute Fussell-Vesely importance of the header.*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FV}_H &= \frac{P(\text{via } H)}{P_{\text{Top}}} = \frac{0.005}{5.0995 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 0.9806
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** ~98% of the system unavailability is due to the header itself — maintenance focus should be on the **header** rather than the pumps.

> [!TIP]
> This is a **textbook KTU 14-mark question**. The model solution above shows the exact chain of reasoning expected by a KTU examiner.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Fault-Tree Analysis Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[System Definition and Boundary] --> A1[Identify Top Event]
    A1 --> A2[Identify Immediate Causes]
    A2 --> A3{Logic Gate Type?}
    A3 -->|Both needed| A4[AND Gate]
    A3 -->|Any one| A5[OR Gate]
    A4 --> A6[Continue Decomposition]
    A5 --> A6[Continue Decomposition]
    A6 --> A7{Is Event Atomic?}
    A7 -->|Yes| A8[Basic Event Circle]
    A7 -->|No| A2
    A8 --> A9[Assign Failure Probabilities]
    A9 --> A10[Boolean Reduction to MCS]
    A10 --> A11[Quantitative Evaluation]
    A11 --> A12[Importance Measures and Reporting]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Start at the top-left `A0` and follow the arrows downward. The diamond at `A3` and `A7` represents a **decision point** in the methodology. Circles (rounded shape in Mermaid) represent **terminal results** in the procedure, not necessarily Basic Events of the tree.

### 4.2 Worked Example — Fault Tree for the Pump-Header System (Section 3.6)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    topEvent[Top Event: System Failure] --> orGate1{Logic OR}
    orGate1 --> headerBE[Header Fails - Basic Event H]
    orGate1 --> andGate1{Logic AND}
    andGate1 --> pumpA[Pump A Fails - Basic Event A]
    andGate1 --> pumpB[Pump B Fails - Basic Event B]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Symbol Mapping Used:**
> - Rectangle = Event (Top / Intermediate / Basic depending on position)
> - Diamond `{...}` in Mermaid = Logic Gate placeholder (real FTA diagrams use D-shape for AND and shield-shape for OR)
> - Convention: top-down flow matches the deductive analysis direction

### 4.3 Generic Fault-Tree Showing Both AND and OR Gates

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    topEv[Top Event: Loss of Cooling] --> gate1{Logic OR}
    gate1 --> pumpFail[Pump 1 and Pump 2 both fail - AND sub-tree]
    gate1 --> pipeBlock[Pipe Blockage - Basic Event]
    pumpFail --> andG{Logic AND}
    andG --> p1BE[Pump 1 Fails - BE]
    andG --> p2BE[Pump 2 Fails - BE]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram of FTA in the Larger Safety-Engineering Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    phase1[Phase 1: HAZOP / Preliminary Hazard Analysis] --> phase2[Phase 2: Fault Tree Construction]
    phase2 --> phase3[Phase 3: Minimal Cut Set Identification]
    phase3 --> phase4[Phase 4: Quantitative Probability Assessment]
    phase4 --> phase5[Phase 5: Importance and Sensitivity]
    phase5 --> phase6[Phase 6: Risk Decision and Recommendations]
    phase6 --> phase7[Phase 7: Update on Operational Feedback]
    phase7 -.-> phase2
```

> [!TIP]
> The dotted feedback arrow from Phase 7 back to Phase 2 represents the **iterative refinement** of the tree when new operational data emerges — an important concept for the KTU 2024 module outcome on *continuous safety improvement*.

### 4.5 Mermaid Compilation Notes (for Examiner Reference)

- All node IDs above are purely alphanumeric (e.g., `A0`, `pumpA`, `andGate1`).
- No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) are used as node names.
- Labels inside nodes are **plain uppercase alphanumeric** with no markdown bold, italics, or HTML tables.
- Curly-brace node shapes `{...}` are used to represent **logic gates** in the functional flow; in a real fault tree these would be D-shape (AND) and shield-shape (OR) per IEC 61025 symbology.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
> **Define Fault Tree Analysis. List any four standard symbols used in fault-tree construction.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Definition (2 marks):** Fault Tree Analysis is a deductive, top-down safety-analysis technique that uses Boolean logic gates to decompose an undesired system-level event (Top Event) into its contributing lower-level causes, in order to identify minimal combinations of failures (Minimal Cut Sets) that can cause the Top Event, and to estimate the probability of its occurrence.

**Four Standard Symbols (½ mark each, any four):**

1. **Rectangle** — Represents an event (Top, Intermediate, or Basic).
2. **Circle** — Represents a Basic Event (leaf-level failure).
3. **AND gate** (D-shape) — Output occurs only if *all* inputs occur.
4. **OR gate** (shield-shape) — Output occurs if *any* input occurs.
5. **Diamond** — Undeveloped event (cause not expanded).
6. **Triangle** — Transfer in / transfer out symbol.
7. **Ellipse / Oval** — Conditioning event (e.g., ON, OFF, HOT).

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Retest Paper]**
> **What is a Minimal Cut Set? Why is it important in fault-tree analysis?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Minimal Cut Set (MCS)** is a combination of Basic Events whose simultaneous occurrence causes the Top Event, and from which *no Basic Event can be removed without losing the property of causing the Top Event* (2 marks).

**Importance (1 mark):** Minimal Cut Sets identify the smallest, most critical combinations of failures that can produce the system-level disaster. They are the qualitative output of FTA, are ranked by size (lower-order MCS = higher criticality), and directly guide maintenance prioritisation, redundancy design, and quantitative risk assessment.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module 3 Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **Question A (14 marks):**
> *(a)* With a neat sketch, explain the construction of a Fault Tree. Identify the symbols used. (7 marks)
> *(b)* A system consists of three components A, B, C. The system fails if (A AND B both fail) OR (C fails). Component failure probabilities are $q_A = 0.02$, $q_B = 0.03$, $q_C = 0.01$. List the minimal cut sets and compute the exact top-event failure probability. (7 marks)

**Model Solution to Q-A (a):**

*Construction procedure (5 marks):*
1. **Define the Top Event** — precisely state the undesired system condition; place it as a rectangle at the top of the tree.
2. **Define system boundary** — decide which subsystems are inside and which are treated as external inputs.
3. **Identify immediate causes** — for the Top Event, list all *independent* failures that by themselves or in combination with others could cause it.
4. **Apply logic gates** — use OR for *any-one-can-cause* situations and AND for *all-must-occur* situations.
5. **Recursive decomposition** — expand every intermediate event downward until only Basic Events remain.

*Symbols (2 marks):* Rectangle (event), Circle (Basic Event), Diamond (Undeveloped), Triangle (Transfer), AND-gate (D-shape), OR-gate (shield-shape), Ellipse (Conditioning).

**Model Solution to Q-A (b):**

*Boolean representation:*

$$T = (A \cdot B) + C$$

*Minimal cut sets (1 mark):* 

$$\text{MCS}_1 = \{A, B\}, \quad \text{MCS}_2 = \{C\}$$

*Quantitative evaluation (6 marks):*

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{MCS}_1) &= q_A \cdot q_B = 0.02 \times 0.03 = 6.0 \times 10^{-4} \\[4pt]
P(\text{MCS}_2) &= q_C = 1.0 \times 10^{-2} \\[4pt]
P_{\text{Top, exact}} &= 1 - (1 - P_1)(1 - P_2) \\[4pt]
&= 1 - (1 - 6\times 10^{-4})(1 - 0.01) \\[4pt]
&= 1 - (0.9994)(0.99) \\[4pt]
&= 1 - 0.98941 \quad \text{(product: } 0.9994 \times 0.99 = 0.989406) \\[4pt]
&= 1.0594 \times 10^{-2}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Fussell-Vesely importance of $C$ (extra credit / full 7 marks):*

$$\text{FV}_C = \frac{q_C}{P_{\text{Top}}} = \frac{0.01}{0.010594} \approx 0.9440$$

> **Question B (14 marks):**
> *(a)* Explain the qualitative and quantitative phases of Fault Tree Analysis. Discuss the significance of Minimal Cut Sets. (7 marks)
> *(b)* A chemical reactor has a high-pressure protection system with two safety relief valves arranged in parallel. Each valve has a failure probability of $0.005$ per demand. The reactor itself bursts with probability $0.002$ per demand independent of valve operation. Using Fault Tree Analysis, determine the Minimal Cut Sets and the exact top-event (loss of containment) probability. (7 marks)

**Model Solution to Q-B (a):**

*Qualitative phase (3 marks):* This phase focuses on **structure** rather than numbers. The fault tree is constructed, Boolean reduction is performed, and Minimal Cut Sets are enumerated. The objective is to identify *which combinations* of component failures can lead to the Top Event. The MCS are ranked by order — singletons are most critical, followed by doublets, then triplets, and so on.

*Quantitative phase (2 marks):* Failure probabilities $q_i$ are assigned to each Basic Event. The Top Event probability is calculated by propagating values up the tree using the **AND and OR gate equations**. If the events are independent, the simple product (for AND) and inclusion-exclusion (for OR) formulas apply.

*Significance of Minimal Cut Sets (2 marks):*
- Provide a **minimal set of failure combinations** that must be prevented.
- Help **rank** the system vulnerabilities by MCS order.
- Form the basis for **importance measures** (Fussell-Vesely, Birnbaum, RAW, RRW).
- Enable **design optimisation** by guiding where to add redundancy.
- Support **risk-informed decision-making** in operations and maintenance.

**Model Solution to Q-B (b):**

*Event definition:* Let $A$ = Valve 1 fails, $B$ = Valve 2 fails, $R$ = Reactor bursts.

*Fault tree (logical):*

$$T = R + (A \cdot B)$$

The reactor can either burst on its own (irrespective of the valves) **or** both valves can fail to relieve pressure, in which case the bursting probability applies. The two cut sets are independent.

*Minimal cut sets (1 mark):*

$$\text{MCS}_1 = \{R\}, \quad \text{MCS}_2 = \{A, B\}$$

*Exact top-event probability (6 marks):*

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{MCS}_1) &= q_R = 0.002 \\[4pt]
P(\text{MCS}_2) &= q_A \cdot q_B = 0.005 \times 0.005 = 2.5 \times 10^{-5} \\[4pt]
P_{\text{Top, exact}} &= 1 - (1 - 0.002)(1 - 2.5 \times 10^{-5}) \\[4pt]
&= 1 - (0.998)(0.999975) \\[4pt]
&= 1 - 0.997975 \quad \text{(product: } 0.998 \times 0.999975 = 0.997975050) \\[4pt]
&= 2.02495 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Engineering conclusion:* The reactor burst probability (0.002) is the dominant contributor — about 98.8% of system failure probability. The parallel valve arrangement effectively reduces the **valve-related** failure path to a negligible level, but overall risk is governed by the **reactor vessel itself**, indicating that vessel-integrity management should receive the highest safety priority.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the exact OR formula** — students often write $P_{\text{OR}} = \sum q_i$ which is the *approximate* formula. When values are not rare ($q \ge 0.05$), the examiner deducts 1–2 marks. Use the exact form $P_{\text{OR}} = 1 - \prod_i(1 - q_i)$.
> 2. **Confusing cut sets and path sets** — A *cut set* causes failure; a *path set* ensures success. De Morgan's laws are needed to switch between the two. Conflating them costs 2 marks.
> 3. **Missing the rare-event assumption declaration** — Always write the assumption before applying the sum-of-MCS approximation. Examiners explicitly award 1 mark for stating assumptions.
> 4. **Omitting the Fussell-Vesely interpretation** — In 14-mark questions, computing $P_{\text{Top}}$ without computing at least one importance measure and interpreting it loses 2 marks.
> 5. **Using series/parallel analogies incorrectly** — In *fault* trees, *series* connection is **OR**; *parallel* connection is **AND** — this is the **opposite** of reliability block diagrams. Many students lose 1–2 marks here.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **FTA is deductive** — starts at the Top Event and decomposes downward to Basic Events.
- **Logic gates** — AND means *all* inputs needed; OR means *any* input sufficient.
- **Basic Event** is a leaf failure (no further decomposition).
- **Minimal Cut Set** is the *smallest non-reducible* set of basic failures that causes the Top Event.
- **Boolean reduction** uses distributive law to expand, then idempotence and absorption to simplify.
- **De Morgan's laws** convert between Fault Tree and Success Tree.
- **Rare-event approximation** for AND gates: $P_{\text{AND}} \approx \prod q_i$.
- **Exact OR formula**: $P_{\text{OR}} = 1 - \prod (1 - q_i)$.
- **Top Event Probability (rare event)** $ \approx $ sum of products of $q$ over each MCS.
- **Fussell-Vesely importance** identifies the components contributing most to system failure.
- **Standards to cite** — IEC 61025, NUREG-0492, MIL-STD-1629.
- **Assumptions** — independent events, non-repairable during mission, constant failure rate.
- **Common symbols** — rectangle (event), circle (basic), diamond (undeveloped), triangle (transfer).
- **Dual-tree principle** — Success tree = De Morgan complement of Fault tree.
- **Series in fault tree = OR**, **Parallel in fault tree = AND** (opposite of reliability block diagrams).
- **Hazards** — Incomplete boundary definition, common-cause failures, dependent events.
- **FTA output** — Minimal cut sets, Top-event probability, importance measures, design recommendations.
- **Iteration** — A fault tree is a *living* document; update it when operational data, new failures, or design changes occur.
- **Compare with FMEA** — FTA is top-down and quantitative; FMEA is bottom-up and qualitative; they are *complementary*, not substitutes.
- **Numerical guard-rail** — When any $q_i \ge 0.05$, do not use the rare-event sum approximation; use the exact inclusion-exclusion form.
- **Engineering takeaway** — FTA converts *engineering judgment* into *numerical risk metrics* that support prioritised, defensible safety decisions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cause and Effect Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT523 - MAINTENANCE AND SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cause and Effect Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — CAUSE AND EFFECT ANALYSIS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> **Cause and Effect Analysis (CEA)** is a structured, visual, and systematic problem-solving technique used in maintenance and safety engineering to identify, organize, and analyze the potential root causes of a specific problem, defect, failure, or undesirable event (the "effect"). It is also widely known as the **Ishikawa Diagram** or **Fishbone Diagram**, named after its inventor, **Professor Kaoru Ishikawa** of the University of Tokyo, who developed it in **1968** for use in quality management and later adapted for root cause failure analysis (RCFA) in industrial maintenance.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a doctor trying to diagnose why a patient has a persistent headache. The doctor doesn't just guess; instead, they systematically investigate several possible "categories" of causes — maybe the patient's **diet (food/drink)**, their **environment (weather/noise)**, their **lifestyle (sleep/stress)**, or any **biological/medical conditions**. Each category is explored, and multiple possible causes are brainstormed.

The **Fishbone Diagram** works exactly the same way. The "head" of the fish is the **problem** (e.g., "Machine Breakdown" or "Safety Incident"), and the "bones" branching out from the spine are the major **cause categories**, with sub-branches listing specific potential causes. It looks just like the skeleton of a fish — hence the name!

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Categories (The "6M" Framework):**
> The six classical branches of a Cause and Effect Diagram are:
> 1. **Man (Personnel/Workforce)** — skills, training, fatigue, human error
> 2. **Machine (Equipment)** — condition, age, calibration, wear
> 3. **Material (Inputs/Raw Materials)** — quality, specifications, storage
> 4. **Method (Process/Procedure)** — SOPs, work instructions, sequencing
> 5. **Measurement (Inspection/Testing)** — instruments, calibration, accuracy
> 6. **Mother Nature (Environment)** — temperature, humidity, dust, lighting
>
> *Sometimes abbreviated as **4M1E** (Man, Machine, Material, Method, Environment) for simpler industrial use cases.*

### Why It Is Critical in Maintenance & Safety

In a maintenance context, when a piece of equipment fails unexpectedly, the maintenance team cannot simply repair it and move on — they must ask **"WHY did it fail?"** Cause and Effect Analysis is the first structured step of any **Root Cause Analysis (RCA)** process. It helps engineers:

- Move beyond symptoms to find **true root causes**
- Capture **collective team knowledge** (brainstorming-based)
- Visually categorize failure contributors
- Prioritize corrective actions
- Prevent **recurrence** of failures
- Comply with **OSHA / ISO 45001** safety investigation requirements

> [!TIP]
> **Remember for KTU Exams:** The most frequently asked question pattern is *"Explain Cause and Effect Analysis with a suitable example"* (typically 7 or 14 marks). Always draw a clear fishbone diagram and explain the **4M1E or 6M categories** with an industrial example.

---

## Key Physical / Engineering Constants in CEA

While Cause and Effect Analysis is primarily a **qualitative** tool, it is frequently combined with quantitative statistical tools. The standard benchmarking metric is:

> **"5-Why Analysis"** — Typically, **at least 5 levels** of "Why" questioning are required to traverse from a surface-level effect down to a verifiable root cause. This is the standard industry benchmark used by **Toyota Production System (TPS)**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Do NOT confuse Cause and Effect Analysis with **Failure Modes and Effects Analysis (FMEA)**. CEA is a **brainstorming/diagnostic** tool to find causes of a *known* problem, whereas FMEA is a **prospective/proactive** tool to identify *potential* failure modes *before* they occur. This is a classic KTU two-mark question trap.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation

Cause and Effect Analysis is rooted in **systems thinking** and **total quality management (TQM)**. It assumes that every undesirable event (effect) is the result of multiple interacting causes, and that these causes can be classified into a finite set of standard categories.

### The Core Logic (Why It Works)

The methodology rests on three foundational principles:

1. **Causality Principle** — Every effect has a cause. Effects do not arise spontaneously.
2. **Categorical Classification Principle** — Causes can be grouped into logical categories (the 4M1E/6M structure) for systematic coverage.
3. **Visual Synthesis Principle** — A picture is worth a thousand words. The fishbone structure forces a multi-disciplinary team to consider all angles simultaneously.

### Operational Methodology — Step-by-Step

The construction of a Cause and Effect Diagram follows a structured seven-step procedure:

- **Step 1: Define the Problem (Effect).** Clearly state the undesirable event in a specific, measurable way. Avoid vague statements. *Example:* "Bearing temperature in Compressor C-101 exceeded 85°C on 12-Jan-2025" — NOT "Compressor is hot."

- **Step 2: Form the Cross-Functional Team.** Assemble 4–8 members from different departments — maintenance, operations, quality, safety, design, supply chain. Diverse perspectives improve the breadth of identified causes.

- **Step 3: Draw the Fishbone Structure.** Create the main horizontal "spine" arrow pointing to the effect (head of the fish). Draw diagonal branch lines for each major category (Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Environment).

- **Step 4: Brainstorm Causes under Each Category.** Using sticky notes or whiteboard markers, the team lists all possible causes under each branch. Use the **"5-Why" technique** for each candidate cause to probe deeper.

- **Step 5: Identify Sub-Causes.** Each main cause can have smaller sub-branches, creating a hierarchical "tree" of possible root causes.

- **Step 6: Analyze and Prioritize.** Mark the most likely causes using data (Pareto charts, statistical evidence, maintenance logs). The **80/20 rule** (Pareto Principle) applies — focus on the **vital few** causes, not the trivial many.

- **Step 7: Develop Corrective Actions.** Assign responsibility, deadlines, and verification methods for each confirmed root cause. Feed these into the **CMMS (Computerized Maintenance Management System)** for closure tracking.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 of PERUT523 specifically covers RCA tools under "Maintenance Engineering." Cause and Effect Analysis is listed as a core topic along with **5-Why Analysis, Pareto Analysis, and Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)**. Students must be able to draw and interpret fishbone diagrams for a given industrial scenario.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Cause and Effect Analysis is fundamentally a **qualitative** tool. It does not have its own mathematical formulas. However, it integrates with quantitative tools, and the following summary captures all equations and metrics a KTU student may be expected to know.

| **Parameter / Concept** | **Symbol / Expression** | **Description** | **Application Context** |
|------------------------|------------------------|-----------------|-------------------------|
| Pareto 80/20 Rule | $\frac{Vital\_Few}{Trivial\_Many} = \frac{20\%}{80\%}$ | 20% of causes create 80% of effects | Prioritizing fishbone branches |
| 5-Why Depth | $n \geq 5$ iterations | Minimum recommended probing depth | Root cause verification |
| Fishbone Category Count (Standard) | $N = 6$ (6M) or $N = 5$ (4M1E) | Number of major branches | Diagram construction |
| Fishbone Category Count (Service) | $N = 4$ (4P) | Place, Procedure, People, Policies | Service industry use |
| Ishikawa Year of Invention | $Y = 1968$ | Historical reference | Exam question on history |
| Failure Rate (associated metric) | $\lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$ | Conditional probability of failure | Links CEA to reliability |
| MTBF (associated) | $MTBF = \frac{T_{total}}{N_{failures}}$ | Mean Time Between Failures | Used in recurrence tracking |
| Cost of Failure (CEF) | $C_{total} = C_{downtime} + C_{repair} + C_{lost\_production} + C_{safety}$ | Total impact of unresolved causes | Justification for CEA use |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked to "draw" a fishbone in an exam, use a clean horizontal arrow for the spine, six diagonal arrows for the 4M1E/6M categories, and 3–4 sub-branches per category with concise industrial examples. **Always label every branch clearly.** Examiners deduct marks for unlabelled or ambiguous diagrams.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Cause and Effect Analysis is universally applied across:

- **Manufacturing Plants** — Defect analysis in production lines (welding defects, dimensional errors)
- **Power Generation** — Turbine trip analysis, boiler tube failure investigation
- **Oil & Gas** — Pipeline leak root cause identification, refinery safety incidents
