# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Model Question]
**Q: Define OSHA. Briefly explain the General Duty Clause.**

**Model Answer:**

**OSHA (Occupational Safety and Health Administration)** is a U.S. federal regulatory agency established under the **Occupational Safety and Health Act of 1970** (Public Law 91-596). It operates under the **U.S. Department of Labor** with the statutory mandate to ensure safe and healthful working conditions by setting and enforcing standards, providing training, outreach, education, and compliance assistance.

The **General Duty Clause**, codified at **Section 5(a)(1)** of the OSH Act, states that *"each employer shall furnish to each of his employees employment and a place of employment which are free from recognized hazards that are causing or are likely to cause death or serious physical harm to his employees."*

This clause is the **catch-all safety obligation** that applies even when no specific 29 CFR standard addresses a particular hazard. The four legal elements for a General Duty Clause citation are:
1. The employer failed to keep the workplace free of a recognized hazard.
2. The hazard was recognized by the employer, the industry, or the government.
3. The hazard was causing or likely to cause death or serious physical harm.
4. A feasible and useful method existed to correct the hazard.

**[3 Marks — Definition 1, Clause Text 1, Four Elements 1]**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model Question]
**Q: List any six employer obligations under the OSH Act Section 5(a).**

**Model Answer:**

The six principal employer obligations under Section 5(a) of the Occupational Safety and Health Act of 1970 are:

1. **General Duty Clause (5(a)(1))** — Furnish a workplace free from recognized hazards that may cause death or serious physical harm.
2. **Compliance with Standards (5(a)(2))** — Comply with all applicable occupational safety and health standards promulgated under the Act.
3. **Posting Requirement (5(a)(3))** — Post and keep posted the official OSHA poster *"It's the Law"* in a conspicuous place where workers can see it.
4. **Recordkeeping (5(a)(4))** — Maintain accurate records and make reports as required by 29 CFR Part 1904 (OSHA 300 Log).
5. **Notice of Violations** — Notify employees of the existence of any standard that applies to their workplace.
6. **Medical and Exposure Records Access** — Provide employees and their designated representatives access to medical and exposure records (29 CFR 1910.1020).

**[3 Marks — Six obligations listed and explained]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q: Explain in detail the Occupational Safety and Health Act of 1970. Discuss the structure, function, and enforcement mechanism of OSHA. What are the major OSHA standards applicable to general industry?**

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Genesis, Structure, and Function of OSHA [7 Marks]

**1. Historical Background (2 Marks):**
The OSH Act was signed into law on **December 29, 1970** by President Richard Nixon and became effective on **April 28, 1971**. It was enacted in response to rising workplace fatalities (estimated at **14,000 annually** in 1969) and the absence of a uniform federal safety law. The **1968 Farmington Mine Disaster** killing 78 miners served as a major catalyst.

**2. Statutory Authority (1 Mark):**
OSHA is established under **Public Law 91-596**, codified at **29 U.S.C. § 651 et seq.**, and operates under the constitutional authority of the **Commerce Clause** since workplace safety substantially affects interstate commerce.

**3. Mission and Functions (2 Marks):**
OSHA's four core functions are:
- **Standard Setting** — Promulgating mandatory safety and health standards.
- **Enforcement** — Conducting workplace inspections and issuing citations.
- **Training and Education** — Operating the OSHA Training Institute and awarding Harwood Grants.
- **Outreach and Cooperation** — Administering Voluntary Protection Programs (VPP), SHARP, and consultation services.

**4. Organizational Structure (2 Marks):**
OSHA operates through:
- **Assistant Secretary of Labor for Occupational Safety and Health** (agency head).
- **Four Directorates** — Standards and Guidance; Enforcement; Cooperative and State Programs; Administration.
- **Regional Offices** (10 regions) and **Area Offices** (85+).
- **State Plans** (22 states + PR + DC) covering both private and public sector.

#### Part (b) — OSHA Enforcement Mechanism and General Industry Standards [7 Marks]

**1. Enforcement Procedure (3 Marks):**
The enforcement cycle includes:
- **Priority Setting** — Imminent Danger → Catastrophe → Complaint → Programmed.
- **Inspection** — Conducted by Compliance Safety and Health Officers (CSHOs) with proper credentials and warrant.
- **Citation** — Issued within 6 months of violation, with proposed penalty.
- **Employer Response** — 15 working days to contest or accept.
- **OSHA Review Commission** — Hearings before Administrative Law Judges.
- **Abatement** — Hazard correction within OSHA-prescribed timeline.

**2. Penalty Structure (2 Marks):**
- **Serious/Other-Than-Serious:** Up to **$16,131** per violation.
- **Willful/Repeated:** Up to **$161,323** per violation.
- **Failure to Abate:** Up to **$16,131 per day**.

**3. Major General Industry Standards (29 CFR Part 1910) (2 Marks):**
- **Subpart D** — Walking-Working Surfaces.
- **Subpart I** — Personal Protective Equipment (PPE).
- **Subpart J** — Lockout/Tagout (LOTO) for hazardous energy control.
- **Subpart S** — Electrical Safety.
- **Subpart Z** — Hazard Communication (HazCom) and Toxic Substances.
- **Subpart 146** — Permit-Required Confined Spaces.
- **Subpart 147** — Control of Hazardous Energy (LOTO Standard).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> Students often confuse **OSHA** (the agency) with **NIOSH** (the research institute). Remember: **OSHA = Enforcer (Department of Labor)**, **NIOSH = Researcher (Department of Health and Human Services, CDC)**. Confusing them costs **1–2 marks** in Part A questions. Also, in penalty problems, do **not** apply only the maximum statutory figure — always apply the **Gravity → Good Faith → Size** reduction sequence, or the answer will be marked wrong.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE [KTU University Exam — June 2022]

**Q: Define workplace safety. Explain the inspection process, citation types, and recordkeeping requirements under OSHA. Calculate the total penalty for a serious violation with maximum gravity, 30% good faith reduction, and 15% size reduction, given the maximum penalty is $16,131.**

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Workplace Safety, Inspection, and Citation [7 Marks]

**1. Definition of Workplace Safety (1 Mark):**
**Workplace safety** is the discipline concerned with preserving and protecting human and facility resources in the workplace through the prevention of accidents, injuries, illnesses, and fatalities. It encompasses engineering controls, administrative procedures, training, and PPE in accordance with regulatory standards.

**2. OSHA Inspection Process (3 Marks):**
- **Step 1 — Pre-Inspection Priority Setting:** Imminent Danger situations receive immediate response; fatalities within 24 hours; complaints and referrals receive programmed inspections.
- **Step 2 — Opening Conference:** The CSHO presents credentials and explains the inspection scope, the employer may request a **warrant** (per *Marshall v. Barlow's Inc.*, 1978).
- **Step 3 — Walkaround:** Accompanied by employer and employee representatives, including photographs and measurements.
- **Step 4 — Employee Interviews:** Held privately to gather candid information.
- **Step 5 — Closing Conference:** Preliminary findings and timelines are discussed.

**3. Citation Types (3 Marks):**
- **Imminent Danger Citation** — Hazard reasonably certain to cause death or serious harm immediately.
- **Serious Violation** — Substantial probability of death or serious physical harm.
- **Other-Than-Serious** — Minimal direct or immediate hazard.
- **Willful Violation** — Employer knowingly disregarded the standard.
- **Repeated Violation** — Substantially similar violation within the last 5 years.
- **Failure to Abate** — Continued non-compliance past the abatement date.

#### Part (b) — Recordkeeping and Penalty Calculation [7 Marks]

**1. Recordkeeping Requirements (29 CFR Part 1904) (2 Marks):**
- **OSHA Form 300** — Log of work-related injuries and illnesses.
- **OSHA Form 300A** — Annual summary posted from February 1 to April 30.
- **OSHA Form 301** — Incident report for each recordable injury/illness.
- **Reporting** — Fatalities within **8 hours**; in-patient hospitalizations, amputations, eye loss within **24 hours**.

**2. Penalty Calculation (5 Marks):**

**Given:** Maximum serious violation penalty = $16,131; Gravity = Maximum (100%); Good Faith = 30%; Size Reduction = 15%.

**Step 1 — Gravity-Based Penalty:**

$$P_{\text{gravity}} = 1.00 \times \$16,131 = \$16,131$$

**Step 2 — Apply Good Faith Reduction (30%):**

$$P_{\text{after GF}} = \$16,131 \times (1 - 0.30)$$

$$= \$16,131 \times 0.70$$

$$= \$11,291.70$$

**Step 3 — Apply Size Reduction (15%):**

$$P_{\text{final}} = \$11,291.70 \times (1 - 0.15)$$

$$= \$11,291.70 \times 0.85$$

$$= \$9,597.95$$

$$\boxed{\text{Final Adjusted Penalty} = \$9,597.95}$$

**Step 4 — Cross-check the Multiplicative Formula:**

$$P_{\text{final}} = P_{\text{max}} \times (1 - GF) \times (1 - Size)$$

$$= \$16,131 \times 0.70 \times 0.85$$

$$= \$16,131 \times 0.595$$

$$= \$9,597.945 \approx \$9,597.95 \;\;\checkmark$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Penalty Problems:**
> (1) Always show all **three reduction steps** explicitly; do not bundle them into a single calculation. (2) Always quote the **maximum statutory figure** as of the relevant fiscal year — penalties are inflation-adjusted annually. (3) For *Failure to Abate* problems, remember it is **per day**, not per violation, which is a common student error. (4) Do not confuse *Willful* and *Repeated* — Willful is about **intent to violate**, Repeated is about **prior identical citation within 5 years**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Frequently Asked Definitions (Quick-Fire Recall)

| Term | One-Line Definition |
|------|---------------------|
| **OSHA** | Occupational Safety and Health Administration (U.S. DOL agency) |
| **OSH Act** | Occupational Safety and Health Act of 1970, P.L. 91-596 |
| **CSHO** | Compliance Safety and Health Officer (OSHA inspector) |
| **NIOSH** | National Institute for Occupational Safety and Health (research) |
| **VPP** | Voluntary Protection Programs (recognition) |
| **SHARP** | Safety and Health Achievement Recognition Program |
| **LOTO** | Lockout/Tagout (29 CFR 1910.147) |
| **GDC** | General Duty Clause (Section 5(a)(1)) |
| **PEL** | Permissible Exposure Limit (toxic exposure threshold) |
| **HazCom** | Hazard Communication Standard (29 CFR 1910.1200) |
| **PSM** | Process Safety Management of Highly Hazardous Chemicals |
| **Citation** | Formal notice of violation issued by OSHA |
| **FTA** | Failure to Abate (Section 17(b)) |
| **State Plan** | OSHA-approved state-level safety program (Section 18) |
| **CPI-U** | Consumer Price Index for All Urban Consumers (penalty inflation) |
| **OSHA 300** | Annual log of work-related injuries and illnesses |

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (High-Density Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Rapid-Revision Checklist for OSHA (Module 4):**
>
> - ☐ **OSH Act 1970** signed **December 29, 1970**, effective **April 28, 1971**.
> - ☐ OSHA is under the **U.S. Department of Labor**; NIOSH is under **HHS/CDC** — never confuse them.
> - ☐ **Section 5(a)(1)** is the **General Duty Clause** — the catch-all safety obligation.
> - ☐ **Section 5(a)(2)** requires compliance with all 29 CFR standards.
> - ☐ **29 CFR Parts 1910, 1926, 1915, 1917, 1918, 1928** are the main OSHA standard subparts.
> - ☐ **Fatality reporting deadline = 8 hours**; hospitalization/amputation = **24 hours**.
> - ☐ **Penalty reductions** follow: **Gravity → Good Faith → Size** in that order.
> - ☐ **Failure to Abate** penalty is **per day**, not per violation.
> - ☐ **Willful violations** carry the **highest financial and criminal exposure** (up to $161,323 + potential imprisonment).
> - ☐ **State Plans** must be *"at least as effective as"* the federal program (Section 18).
> - ☐ **Marshall v. Barlow's Inc. (1978)** — employers may require OSHA to obtain a **warrant** for routine inspections.
> - ☐ **Section 11(c)** provides **whistleblower protection** against employer retaliation.
> - ☐ Penalty formula: $P_{\text{final}} = P_{\text{max}} \times (1 - GF) \times (1 - Size)$.
> - ☐ **LOTO (29 CFR 1910.147)** is the most-cited standard in maintenance engineering contexts.
> - ☐ OSHA does **not** cover **self-employed individuals, family farms, or mining** (covered by MSHA).
> - ☐ **VPP Star Sites** have ~**50% lower DART rates** than industry averages — a quantitative testimonial to voluntary cooperation.

topics.title: HASAWA
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT523 - MAINTENANCE AND SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** HASAWA

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Health and Safety at Work etc. Act 1974** (commonly abbreviated as **HASAWA**, **HSW Act**, or **HSWA 1974**) is the primary, foundational piece of legislation governing occupational health, safety, and welfare in the United Kingdom. It is an **enabling Act** of Parliament that establishes a comprehensive legal framework for securing the health, safety, and welfare of persons at work, and for protecting the health and safety of the public from hazards arising from work activities.

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PERUT523 – Maintenance and Safety Engineering**, HASAWA is studied as a **benchmark model legislation** that has shaped modern international occupational safety law, including India's Factories Act 1948, OSHA 1970 (USA), and the European Union Framework Directive 89/391/EEC.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HASAWA Key Identifier:** *Chapter 37 of the United Kingdom Public General Acts, enacted on 31 July 1974, brought into force on 1 April 1975 (after a transitional period).* This Act consolidated and replaced numerous earlier, fragmented pieces of safety legislation.

### Formal KTU-Standard Definition

> **"The Health and Safety at Work etc. Act 1974 is an Act to make further provision for securing the health, safety, and welfare of persons at work, for protecting others against risks to health or safety in connection with the activities of persons at work, for controlling the keeping and use and preventing the unlawful acquisition, possession, and use of dangerous substances, and generally for the purpose of promoting an improved working environment."**
> — *Long Title of the Act, Chapter 37, HMSO, UK Parliament.*

### 1.2 The Six Foundational Objectives of HASAWA

The Act establishes **six cardinal objectives** that an examiner will frequently test under Bloom's Level 1 (Remember) and Level 2 (Understand):

| # | Objective | Engineering Interpretation |
|---|-----------|-----------------------------|
| 1 | Securing health, safety, and welfare of persons at work | Direct protection of the workforce during operations and maintenance |
| 2 | Protecting others against risks from work activities | Public safety (e.g., nearby residents of a chemical plant) |
| 3 | Controlling dangerous substances | Hazardous materials management (COSHH regulations later) |
| 4 | Preventing unlawful use of dangerous substances | Anti-terrorism & diversion prevention |
| 5 | Promoting an improved working environment | Ergonomics, ventilation, lighting, sanitation |
| 6 | Establishing a unified enforcement authority | Creation of the **Health and Safety Executive (HSE)** |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: "The Building's Fire Safety Code"**
>
> Imagine a large residential apartment complex. To make it safe for everyone — the residents (employees), visitors, neighbours, and even the maintenance crew — the law requires a single, comprehensive **building safety code**. This code says: the *builder* must use non-toxic materials; the *owner* must install smoke detectors and fire escapes; the *residents* must not block exits or tamper with alarms; the *visitors* must follow the rules; and an independent **fire inspector** (the enforcing authority) audits the building regularly and can fine the owner for violations.
>
> **HASAWA works exactly this way for every workplace in Britain.** The *employer* is the building owner, the *employees* are the residents, the *HSE* is the fire inspector, and the *Act itself* is the single, unified safety code.

### 1.4 Why HASAWA is the Cornerstone of UK Safety Law

Before 1974, UK safety law was a patchwork of sector-specific acts (Factories Act 1961, Mines and Quarries Act 1954, Offices, Shops and Railway Premises Act 1963, etc.). HASAWA revolutionized this by:

- **Universal Coverage:** Applying to *all* employers across *all* industries (not just factories).
- **Self-Regulation Philosophy:** Imposing a **general duty of care** rather than prescriptive rules.
- **Risk-Based Approach:** Requiring employers to assess and control risks, rather than follow rigid checklists.
- **Robust Enforcement:** Establishing criminal liability backed by unlimited fines and imprisonment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Statistic for KTU Examinations:** HASAWA applies to virtually every workplace in Great Britain. The **Health and Safety Executive (HSE)** reports that the Act protects approximately **30+ million workers** across an estimated **1.6 million employers** in regulated workplaces.

### 1.5 Salient Features at a Glance

The Act contains **85 sections** organised into **5 Parts** plus **Schedules**. For the KTU Module 4 examination, the following structural pillars are mandatory knowledge:

1. **Part I — Health, Safety and Welfare in Connection with Work, and the Control of Dangerous Substances** (Sections 1–34)
2. **Part II — The Employment Medical Advisory Service** (Sections 35–46)
3. **Part III — The Health and Safety Commission and the Health and Safety Executive** (Sections 47–60)
4. **Part IV — Acquisition and Use of Dangerous Substances** (Sections 61–80)
5. **Part V — Miscellaneous and General** (Sections 81–85)

The **duties** are codified primarily in **Sections 2 through 9** — these are the most heavily tested provisions in any KTU Part A (3-mark) or Part B (14-mark) question.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Decomposition of HASAWA

HASAWA is a **framework Act** — it sets out the *principles* and *enabling powers*, and delegates the detailed technical rules to **Statutory Instruments (SIs)** and **Approved Codes of Practice (ACOPs)** issued by the HSE. This is a critical conceptual point.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Enabling Act Doctrine:** HASAWA does not, by itself, contain every technical safety rule. It delegates rule-making authority to the **Secretary of State** (Sections 10–14) and to **Appropriate Ministers**, who then issue detailed **Regulations** under the powers granted by the Act. For example, the **Management of Health and Safety at Work Regulations 1999**, **Workplace (Health, Safety and Welfare) Regulations 1992**, **Provision and Use of Work Equipment Regulations 1998 (PUWER)**, and **Control of Substances Hazardous to Health Regulations (COSHH)** are all made under HASAWA.

### 2.2 The Tripartite Stakeholder Model

HASAWA is built on three stakeholder groups. An examiner can ask you to *name, explain, and compare* the duties imposed on each. The structure is:

- **Duty Holder Group A — Creator / Designer:** Manufacturers, designers, importers, suppliers (Section 6)
- **Duty Holder Group B — Operator / User:** Employers, self-employed, persons in control of premises (Sections 2, 3, 4, 5, 9)
- **Duty Holder Group C — Workforce / Public:** Employees, visitors, contractors, members of the public (Sections 7, 8)

## 2.3 Section-by-Section Duty Analysis (KTU High-Yield Sections)

This is the **"formula sheet" equivalent** of HASAWA. Memorise this table — it is the single most tested component of this topic.

> [!NOTE]
> **The asterisk (\*) in the table marks sections that are routinely examined in KTU Past Year Papers.**

### 2.3.1 KTU High-Yield Duty Provision Matrix

| Section | Duty Holder | Subject Matter | Core Obligation | Penalty for Breach |
|---------|-------------|----------------|------------------|---------------------|
| **Sec 2 \*** | Employer → Employees | General duty of care to own workforce | Provide and maintain safe plant, safe systems, safe personnel, safe premises; ensure safe handling of articles and substances; provide information, instruction, training, supervision | Criminal — fine, imprisonment up to 2 years (indictable) |
| **Sec 3 \*** | Employer / Self-Employed → Non-Employees | Duty to persons not in employment (visitors, public, contractors) | Ensure activities do not expose non-employees to risks to their health and safety | Criminal — fine, imprisonment up to 2 years |
| **Sec 4 \*** | Persons in control of non-domestic premises | Duty regarding premises used as workplace | Take such measures as are reasonable for a person in their position to ensure safe means of access and egress, and that the premises are safe | Criminal |
| **Sec 5** | Person in control of certain non-domestic premises (common parts) | Maintenance of common parts of shared workplace premises | Maintain premises in a condition that is, so far as is reasonably practicable, safe and without risks to health | Criminal |
| **Sec 6 \*** | Manufacturers, Designers, Importers, Suppliers, Hirers | Duty re: articles and substances for use at work | Ensure articles/substances are safe and without risk when properly used; provide adequate information and test results | Criminal — fine, imprisonment up to 3 months (summary) or 2 years (indictable) |
| **Sec 7 \*** | Employees | Duty to take reasonable care | Take reasonable care for the health and safety of themselves and of other persons who may be affected by their acts or omissions | Criminal (summary) |
| **Sec 8 \*** | Employees / Persons at workplace | Duty not to interfere | Not to intentionally or recklessly interfere with or misuse anything provided in the interests of health, safety, or welfare | Criminal (summary) |
| **Sec 9 \*** | Self-Employed | Duty of self-employed persons | Conduct their undertaking in such a way as to ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that they and others are not exposed to risks | Criminal |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **"So far as is reasonably practicable" (SFAIRP) — THE Magic Phrase of HASAWA**
>
> This phrase appears in **Sections 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, and 9** — and nowhere else in the Act is this phrase more central. It is a **legal test** defined by the **Edwards v National Coal Board [1949]** judgment: it is a *balance of risk × magnitude of harm versus cost, time, and trouble of prevention measures*. The greater the risk, the more the employer must do. It is **not** the same as "physically possible" or "cheap and easy."

### 2.3.2 The "Reasonably Practicable" Doctrine — KTU Conceptual Formula

Although HASAWA contains no mathematical formulas, the test of **"reasonableness"** can be conceptualised as the following legal balance:

$$
\text{Reasonableness} = \frac{\text{Magnitude} \times \text{Likelihood of Harm}}{\text{Cost, Time, and Trouble of Prevention}}
$$

Where:
- **Numerator** = the seriousness (severity) multiplied by the probability (likelihood) of the foreseeable risk
- **Denominator** = the burden (in money, time, effort) of the measures needed to avert that risk
- If the **ratio is high**, the duty is stringent (must do almost everything possible)
- If the **ratio is low**, the duty is relaxed (only gross negligence is actionable)

This is a **qualitative legal standard**, not a numeric calculation, but expressing it as a ratio is useful for KTU answers because it demonstrates analytical depth.

### 2.4 Enforcement Architecture (Sections 18–25, 33–42)

KTU questions on HASAWA often test the **enforcement** half of the Act. The enforcement model is:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two-Tier Enforcement Structure:**
> 1. **The Health and Safety Commission (HSC)** — Now merged into HSE (2008). Originally the policy-making body, comprising representatives of employers, employees, and local authorities.
> 2. **The Health and Safety Executive (HSE)** — The operational body that enforces the Act, conducts inspections, and issues guidance.

### 2.4.1 Enforcement Powers Table (Sections 19–25)

| Power | Section | Description | KTU Significance |
|-------|---------|-------------|------------------|
| Improvement Notice | Sec 21 | Requires an employer to remedy a contravention within a specified period (minimum 21 days) | Test frequently asked — "what is the notice period?" |
| Prohibition Notice | Sec 22 | Immediately stops an activity that poses a risk of serious personal injury | Immediate, no period given |
| Prohibition Notice (Imminent Danger) | Sec 23 | Special category — used when a Coroner inquires into a death and concludes an activity is creating imminent danger | Rarely tested, but a high-mark differentiator |
| Power of Entry | Sec 20 | Authorised inspectors may enter premises at any reasonable time, or in danger, at any time | High-frequency test |
| Power to seize | Sec 20 | May seize articles and substances that are likely to cause serious danger | Moderate frequency |
| Power to give directions | Sec 25 | Can require persons to assist, answer questions, sign declarations | Used in investigations |

### 2.4.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Enforceable Sanctions

| Sanction | Source | Maximum Penalty |
|----------|--------|-----------------|
| Fine (lower court / summary conviction) | Sec 33(1)(a) | £20,000 (statutory maximum) — *before the Health and Safety (Offences) Act 2008* |
| Fine (indictment / Crown Court) | Sec 33(1)(b) | Unlimited fine |
| Imprisonment (summary) | Sec 33(1)(a) | Up to **6 months** (extended to 12 by 2008 Act) |
| Imprisonment (indictment) | Sec 33(1)(b) | Up to **2 years** generally; up to **3 years** for certain offences |
| Corporate Manslaughter (post 2007) | Corporate Manslaughter and Corporate Homicide Act 2007 | Unlimited fine + remedial order |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often write that the maximum fine under HASAWA is £20,000. This was the *statutory maximum for summary conviction only*. After the **Health and Safety (Offences) Act 2008**, summary fines are uncapped. For indictable offences, fines have *always* been unlimited. Examiners deduct marks for failing to note this evolution.

### 2.5 Approved Codes of Practice (ACOPs) — Section 17

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Section 17 of HASAWA** empowers the HSC/HSE to approve Codes of Practice. While **not legally binding** in the strict sense, ACOPs have **special legal status** under Section 17(2): *"A failure to comply with a provision of an approved code of practice is not in itself an offence, but such a failure may be taken into account by a court in determining whether or not a person has committed an offence."*

This means: in a KTU answer, you can confidently state that **ACOPs are persuasive in court but not mandatory** — but a deviation from an ACOP must be justified by equal or better precautions.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | How HASAWA Operates |
|----------|---------------------|
| **Construction** | Underpins CDM Regulations 2015, Working at Height Regulations 2005 |
| **Manufacturing** | Forms basis for PUWER 1998, LOLER 1998, COSHH 2002 |
| **Petrochemical** | Supports COMAH Regulations 2015, DSEAR 2002 |
| **Rail** | Basis for Railways and Other Guided Transport Systems (ROGS) |
| **Maintenance Engineering** | ALL maintenance activities fall under Section 2 duties + LOLER/PUWER |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivation & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation of the HASAWA Legal Framework

Because HASAWA is a legal text rather than a physical law, "derivation" here means **the logical hierarchy of the framework** — i.e., how the law is *built from first principles* up to specific workplace obligations. The KTU examiner rewards answers that demonstrate this layered understanding.

### 3.1.1 Step-1: The Constitutional Foundation

$$
\boxed{\text{Magna Carta (1215)} \longrightarrow \text{Bill of Rights (1689)} \longrightarrow \text{Health and Safety at Work etc. Act (1974)}}
$$

**Explanation of Step 1:** HASAWA is grounded in the long English constitutional tradition that the State has a duty to protect life and limb. The **Magna Carta's Article 39** ("no free man shall be… harmed… save by the lawful judgment of his peers or by the law of the land") establishes the principle that bodily harm must be legally justified. HASAWA is the modern industrial application of this ancient principle.

### 3.1.2 Step-2: The Robens Report (1972) — The Genesis of HASAWA

The **Committee on Safety and Health at Work**, chaired by **Lord Robens**, published its landmark report *"Safety and Health at Work: Report of the Committee 1970–1972"* (Cmnd. 5034, HMSO, July 1972). This report is the **intellectual parent** of HASAWA.

$$
\boxed{\text{Robens Report} \rightarrow \text{HASAWA 1974}}
$$

**Key Robens Recommendations — Listed for KTU 1-Mark Recall Questions:**

1. **Unified System:** Replace the fragmented, industry-specific safety statutes with a single, comprehensive Act covering all work activities.
2. **Self-Regulation:** Shift the legal burden onto employers and employees, supported by an advisory inspectorate, not a prescriptive police force.
3. **Tripartite Governance:** Establish a **Health and Safety Commission (HSC)** with representation from employers, trade unions, and local authorities.
4. **Performance Standards:** Move from prescriptive rules (the "how") to performance-based goals (the "what").
5. **Employee Participation:** Promote workforce involvement in safety decisions at the workplace level.

### 3.1.3 Step-3: Derivation of the General Duty Concept

The "general duty" approach in HASAWA is a **legal engineering technique** for keeping legislation future-proof. The derivation is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step A:} \quad & \text{Identify the problem — workplace injuries and occupational diseases.} \\
\textbf{Step B:} \quad & \text{Set a general standard of conduct — "ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable…"} \\
\textbf{Step C:} \quad & \text{Identify duty-holders — employers, employees, self-employed, manufacturers.} \\
\textbf{Step D:} \quad & \text{Define the legal test — "reasonable practicability" (Edwards v NCB, 1949).} \\
\textbf{Step E:} \quad & \text{Provide enforcement teeth — inspectors, notices, criminal prosecution.} \\
\textbf{Step F:} \quad & \text{Delegate detailed rules to SIs and ACOPs to keep the Act flexible.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual Explanation of Each Step:**

- **Step A** establishes *why* the law exists (problem statement).
- **Step B** creates the *aspirational standard* (the legal duty).
- **Step C** distributes *responsibility* (the duty holders).
- **Step D** sets the *test* for compliance (the "reasonably practicable" benchmark).
- **Step E** ensures *enforcement* (the HSE and the courts).
- **Step F** provides *flexibility* (delegated legislation — SIs, ACOPs).

### 3.1.4 Step-4: Derivation of the Duty Chain (the "Criss-Cross" Logic)

HASAWA creates a **two-way duty chain** between the workplace and the workforce:

$$
\boxed{\text{Employer} \;\underset{\text{Sec 2}}{\overset{\text{Sec 2}}{\rightleftarrows}}\; \text{Employee}}
$$

- **Rightward arrow (Sec 2):** Employer owes a duty of care TO employees.
- **Leftward arrow (Sec 7 & 8):** Employees owe reciprocal duties of care and non-interference BACK to the employer and fellow workers.

Additionally, the Act creates a **lateral duty chain** between the workplace and the public:

$$
\boxed{\text{Employer} \;\underset{\text{Sec 3}}{\overset{\text{Sec 3}}{\rightleftarrows}}\; \text{Public/Visitors/Contractors}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Triangular Duty Model (KTU Examiner's Favourite):**
> 
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> \text{Manufacturer} & \xrightarrow{\text{Sec 6 — provides safe articles}} \text{Employer} \\
> \text{Employer} & \xrightarrow{\text{Sec 2 — provides safe workplace}} \text{Employee} \\
> \text{Employer} & \xrightarrow{\text{Sec 3 — protects non-employees}} \text{Public}
> \end{aligned}
> $$
> 
> This is the *flow of responsibility* that examiners love to draw in a 14-mark question. If the student draws this and labels the relevant section numbers, full marks are typically awarded.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Application of HASAWA to a Maintenance Engineering Scenario

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Engineering Scenario:** A maintenance engineer is tasked with repairing a high-pressure steam boiler in a chemical plant. Apply HASAWA to this situation.

| Action / Object | HASAWA Section | Specific Obligation | Engineering Practice |
|------------------|-----------------|---------------------|----------------------|
| Provision of a safe boiler (designed with safety valves) | Sec 2(2)(a) | Safe plant and equipment | Inspect before issue; ASME/IBR certification |
| Written safe system of work for boiler entry | Sec 2(2)(b) | Safe system of work | Permit-to-Work (PTW) system |
| Competent fitter assigned to the task | Sec 2(2)(c) | Competent persons | NVQ-level trained, authorised |
| Safe access via fixed scaffold to boiler platform | Sec 2(2)(d) | Safe access and egress | Scaffold inspection, harness |
| Boiler room temperature controlled | Sec 2(2)(e) | Safe working environment | Ventilation, thermal control |
| Information on residual pressure/temperature given to fitter | Sec 2(2)(c) | Information, instruction, training | Toolbox talk, isolation certificate |
| Fitter wears PPE and follows lock-out-tag-out (LOTO) | Sec 7 | Employee's duty to take care | Hard hat, boiler suit, insulated gloves |
| Fitter does not bypass the pressure safety valve | Sec 8 | No interference | Disciplinary action if violated |
| Visitor (auditor) is briefed on the boiler room hazards | Sec 3 | Duty to non-employees | Visitor sign-in, escort, brief |
| Boiler is purchased from a manufacturer with CE/UKCA mark and manual | Sec 6 | Manufacturer's duty | Declaration of Conformity |
| Inspector from HSE visits | Sec 20, 22 | Power of entry / Prohibition Notice | Stop work, present compliance docs |
| Best practice: follow the ACOP on pressure systems | Sec 17 | Approved Code of Practice | HSE's *Safety of Pressure Systems* ACOP |

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Risk Assessment Linkage (Section 3 Risk Duty)

HASAWA Section 3 is a *bridge* to the **Management of Health and Safety at Work Regulations 1999**, which make **risk assessment** a legal duty. The derivation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Identify the hazards (e.g., steam, confined space, electrical).} \\
\textbf{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Decide who might be harmed and how (engineer, contractor, visitor).} \\
\textbf{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Evaluate the risks and decide on precautions (hierarchy of control).} \\
\textbf{Step 4:} \quad & \text{Record findings and implement (Risk Assessment Form, RAMS).} \\
\textbf{Step 5:} \quad & \text{Review and update the assessment (post-incident, change of process).}
\end{aligned}
$$

**The Hierarchical Logic of Section 2(2) — In Order of the Statute Itself:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text{Sec 2(2)(a) — Safe PLANT and MACHINERY} \\
& \text{Sec 2(2)(b) — Safe SYSTEMS of work} \\
& \text{Sec 2(2)(c) — INFORMATION, INSTRUCTION, TRAINING, SUPERVISION} \\
& \text{Sec 2(2)(d) — Safe PLACE of work with safe ACCESS/EGRESS} \\
& \text{Sec 2(2)(e) — Safe WORKING ENVIRONMENT with adequate WELFARE} \\
& \text{Sec 2(3) — Written SAFETY POLICY (5+ employees)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Mneumonic "PSSIPS-PSW"** — a KTU student's shortcut: **P**lant, **S**ystems, **S**upervision, **I**nformation, **P**remises, **S**urroundings, **P**olicy, **S**igned-welfare.

### 3.4 Derivation of the Enforcement Offence Chain (Section 33)

The offence-creation hierarchy of HASAWA Section 33 follows a strict **three-tier** logic:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Tier 1 (Sec 33(1)(a)):} \quad & \text{Summary offence — tried in magistrates' court.} \\
& \text{Penalty: Statutory fine + 6 months imprisonment (12 months after 2008).} \\
\textbf{Tier 2 (Sec 33(1)(b)):} \quad & \text{Indictable offence — tried in Crown Court.} \\
& \text{Penalty: Unlimited fine + 2 years imprisonment (3 years for some).} \\
\textbf{Tier 3 (Sec 33(3)):} \quad & \text{Employer-defence — "due diligence defence."} \\
& \text{Employer can avoid conviction by proving ALL reasonable steps were taken.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual Walk-Through:**

- **Tier 1** represents minor breaches tried summarily, where the legal process is quick.
- **Tier 2** represents serious breaches (fatalities, major injuries) tried on indictment, where the maximum penalty is severe.
- **Tier 3** is the **"due diligence defence"** — a critical concept. It is **not** a defence of "we didn't know"; it is a defence of "we took *every reasonable step* in advance." This shifts the burden of proof onto the employer to *demonstrate* safety efforts.

### 3.5 Connection to the Factories Act 1948 (India) — Comparative Analysis for KTU

Because KTU is an Indian university, examiners sometimes (10–15% of question papers) ask students to **compare HASAWA with the Indian Factories Act 1948**. The KTU 2024 Scheme expects a tabular comparison:

| Parameter | HASAWA 1974 (UK) | Factories Act 1948 (India) |
|-----------|------------------|------------------------------|
| Scope | All workplaces (any industry) | Only registered "factories" with 10+ workers (using power) or 20+ (without power) |
| Coverage | Universal, broad | Sectoral, narrow |
| Primary duty | General duty on employer + employees | Specific duties on occupier, manager |
| Penalties | Unlimited fine + imprisonment | Specified fines; moderate imprisonment |
| Self-regulation philosophy | Yes (Robens model) | No — prescriptive |
| Policy statement | Sec 2(3) — written safety policy (5+ employees) | Sec 7A — written safety policy (50+ workers) |
| Worker participation | Safety Representatives and Safety Committees Regulations 1977 | Joint Safety Committees in some states |
| Hazardous substances | COSHH 2002 (delegated) | Manufacture, Storage and Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules 1989 |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question A1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]

**"Define the term 'reasonably practicable' as used in the Health and Safety at Work etc. Act 1974. Why is this phrase legally significant?"**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

The phrase **"so far as is reasonably practicable"** is a legal test, originally defined in the case of **Edwards v National Coal Board [1949] 1 All ER 743**. It requires a **balance** to be struck between:

1. The **magnitude of the risk** (severity × likelihood) — established in the *numerator* of the test.
2. The **cost, time, and trouble** of eliminating or reducing that risk — in the *denominator*.

If the **risk is high**, the duty is stringent — the employer must take almost every conceivable precaution. If the risk is small, only modest precautions are required. The phrase is legally significant because it:

- Provides **flexibility** (no rigid technical specifications)
- Shifts the **burden of proof** onto the duty-holder to demonstrate that risks have been balanced
- Forms the **standard of care** used by courts when assessing criminal liability under Sections 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, and 9 of the Act.

---

### Question A2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 2]

**"List the key duties of an EMPLOYEE under HASAWA 1974. State the section numbers."**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

Under HASAWA 1974, employees owe **three** primary duties:

1. **Section 7 — Duty to take reasonable care:** Every employee must take reasonable care for the health and safety of **themselves** and of **other persons** who may be affected by their acts or omissions at work.
2. **Section 8 — Duty not to interfere:** No employee shall intentionally or recklessly interfere with, or misuse, anything provided in the interests of health, safety, or welfare (e.g., fire extinguishers, guards, PPE, safety signs).
3. **Co-operation Duty (implied under Section 7):** Employees are also expected to co-operate with the employer to enable the employer to comply with statutory duties.

A breach of Sections 7 or 8 is a **summary criminal offence**, prosecutable in the magistrates' court.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark ESE Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> In 14-mark HASAWA questions, the most common reasons for losing marks are:
> 1. **Forgetting the section numbers** — a 7-mark sub-part without statutory citation may lose 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Conflating "reasonably practicable" with "absolutely safe"** — examiners award zero if the student writes that the employer must "guarantee" safety.
> 3. **Omitting the Robens Report** — examiners expect you to mention the genesis of the Act in Module 4 essays.
> 4. **Writing only about Section 2** — the question almost always requires discussion of **multiple stakeholders** (employer + employee + manufacturer).
> 5. **Forgetting the role of the HSE** — at least one sub-part will ask you to describe the enforcement body.

### Question B-A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper 3]**

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** *"Explain the salient features of the Health and Safety at Work etc. Act 1974. Discuss the role played by the Robens Committee Report 1972 in the evolution of this Act."*

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** *"Describe in detail the duties of an EMPLOYER under HASAWA 1974. How are these duties enforced in practice?"*

#### Model Answer — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**1. Salient Features of HASAWA 1974 (4 marks):**

- **Comprehensive Scope:** The Act covers **all** work activities in Great Britain — manufacturing, construction, mining, offices, retail, transport, healthcare, and even domestic service in defined circumstances.
- **Tripartite Duty Structure:** Imposes enforceable duties on (i) employers (Sections 2, 3, 4, 5), (ii) employees (Sections 7, 8), and (iii) the self-employed (Section 9), and on manufacturers and suppliers (Section 6).
- **Enabling Act:** HASAWA does not prescribe every technical rule; it delegates detailed regulations (Statutory Instruments) and codes (ACOPs) to the HSE.
- **Risk-Based Philosophy:** Compliance is judged by the test of "reasonableness" rather than rigid checklists.
- **Strong Enforcement:** Establishes the **Health and Safety Commission (HSC)** and the **Health and Safety Executive (HSE)** with powers of entry, inspection, improvement notices, prohibition notices, and prosecution.
- **Criminal Liability:** Breaches attract unlimited fines and imprisonment, signalling the seriousness of the obligations.

**2. Role of the Robens Committee Report 1972 (3 marks):**

- The Robens Committee, chaired by **Lord Robens**, was appointed in 1970 and reported in July 1972. It criticised the existing system for being a **fragmented patchwork of industry-specific statutes** (Factories Act 1961, Mines and Quarries Act 1954, Offices, Shops and Railway Premises Act 1963, etc.).
- The Report recommended a **single, unified Act** for all workplaces, based on a **self-regulatory philosophy** and **performance standards** rather than prescriptive regulations.
- It also proposed the creation of a **national authority** combining tripartite representation of employers, employees, and local authorities.
- The HASAWA 1974 was the **direct legislative child** of the Robens Report — its structure, philosophy, and institutional design are all Robens-derived.
- **Conclusion:** HASAWA is the practical implementation of the Robens vision, and no answer on this topic is complete without referencing the Report.

#### Model Answer — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**1. Duties of an Employer under HASAWA 1974 (5 marks):**

The duties are concentrated in **Sections 2, 3, 4, 5, and Section 2(3)**:

- **Section 2 — Duty to Employees:** The employer must ensure, **so far as is reasonably practicable**, the health, safety, and welfare at work of all employees. Specifically:
  - **Section 2(2)(a):** Provide and maintain **safe plant and machinery**.
  - **Section 2(2)(b):** Provide **safe systems of work**.
  - **Section 2(2)(c):** Ensure **safety** in the **handling, storage, and transport** of articles and substances; and provide adequate **information, instruction, training, and supervision**.
  - **Section 2(2)(d):** Maintain any **place of work** in a safe condition, with safe **means of access and egress**.
  - **Section 2(2)(e):** Provide and maintain a **safe working environment** with adequate **welfare facilities** (washing, sanitation, rest rooms, drinking water, first aid).
  - **Section 2(3):** Prepare a **written safety policy** (if 5 or more employees are employed) and bring it to the notice of all employees.
  - **Section 2(4):** **Consultation** with safety representatives appointed by recognised trade unions.

- **Section 3 — Duty to Non-Employees:** Extend the same standard of care to **visitors, contractors, members of the public** who may be affected by the work activity.

- **Section 4 — Duty Regarding Premises:** Persons who have, to any extent, **control of non-domestic premises used as a workplace** must take reasonable measures to ensure safe access/egress and a safe workplace.

- **Section 5 — Duty Regarding Common Parts:** Maintain the **common parts** of shared premises (e.g., lifts, staircases, corridors) in a safe condition.

**2. Enforcement in Practice (2 marks):**

- **Health and Safety Executive (HSE)** enforces the Act through **inspections** (Section 20), **Improvement Notices** (Section 21, minimum 21 days to comply), and **Prohibition Notices** (Section 22, immediate cessation of activity).
- Breach is a **criminal offence** under Section 33, with **unlimited fines** and **imprisonment up to 2 years** on indictment.
- The **"due diligence defence"** under Section 33(3) allows an employer to avoid conviction by proving all reasonable steps were taken.
- Prosecutions are brought by the HSE (or by the **Crown Prosecution Service** for serious cases) and heard in **Magistrates' Courts (summary)** or **Crown Court (indictment)**.

---

### Question B-B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – May 2025, Model Paper 4]**

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** *"Discuss the duties of manufacturers and suppliers under HASAWA 1974. Why are these duties considered the upstream foundation of workplace safety?"*

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** *"Explain the enforcement architecture of HASAWA 1974. Describe the difference between an Improvement Notice and a Prohibition Notice, with examples."*

#### Model Answer — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**1. Duties of Manufacturers, Designers, Importers, Suppliers, and Hirers (5 marks):**

Codified primarily in **Section 6** of HASAWA 1974, the duties of the upstream supply chain are:

- **Section 6(1)(a) — Design and Construction Duty:** Any person who designs, manufactures, imports, or supplies any **article for use at work** must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that the article is **properly designed and constructed** so that it will be safe and without risk to health when properly used.
- **Section 6(1)(b) — Testing and Examination Duty:** The article must be **adequately tested and examined** to ensure these safety standards.
- **Section 6(1)(c) — Information Duty:** Adequate **information** must be provided about the conditions necessary to ensure the article remains safe during use (e.g., operating manuals, safety data sheets, maintenance schedules).
- **Section 6(2)(a) — Erection and Installation Duty:** When designing or manufacturing an article for use at work, the duty-holder must also ensure that the article can be **erected, installed, dismantled, and maintained safely**.
- **Section 6(2)(b) — Research Duty:** The duty-holder must carry out (or arrange for) any **research** necessary to identify and eliminate or minimise risks.
- **Section 6(3) — Substances Duty:** Similar duties apply to **substances** — the manufacturer/supplier must ensure the substance is safe when properly used, that it is adequately tested, and that relevant information is provided.
- **Section 6(4) — Supplier's Implied Warranty:** A supplier is deemed to have warranted that the article/substance is safe, with due diligence protections for retailers who do not modify the product.

**2. Upstream Foundation Argument (2 marks):**

- Workplace safety **begins at the design stage**. Even the most diligent employer cannot make an inherently unsafe machine safe at the point of use.
- Therefore, the law places the **earliest possible duty** on the **creator** of the risk. This is called the **"upstream duty"** concept in safety engineering.
- It is a **preventive**, not reactive, philosophy — preventing the hazard from ever entering the workplace.
- **Case law (e.g., *Budden v BP Oil [1980]*):** A petrol pump that exploded due to a manufacturing defect led to prosecution of the manufacturer under Section 6.

#### Model Answer — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**1. Enforcement Architecture (3 marks):**

- The **Health and Safety Commission (HSC)** (now merged into HSE) was the **policy-making body** comprising representatives of employers, employees, and local authorities.
- The **Health and Safety Executive (HSE)** is the **operational enforcement body** with the following powers under HASAWA:
  - **Section 20 — Power of Entry:** Inspectors may enter any workplace at any reasonable time (or at any time in case of imminent danger), inspect, examine, take measurements, photographs, and samples, and seize dangerous articles.
  - **Section 21 — Improvement Notices**
  - **Section 22 — Prohibition Notices**
  - **Section 25 — Power to give directions and require assistance.**
- The **Crown Prosecution Service (CPS)** prosecutes the most serious cases.

**2. Improvement Notice vs Prohibition Notice (4 marks):**

| Parameter | Improvement Notice (Sec 21) | Prohibition Notice (Sec 22) |
|-----------|-----------------------------|------------------------------|
| **Purpose** | To require **remedy of a contravention** | To require **immediate cessation of an activity** |
| **Trigger** | A contravention of the Act or its regulations | Activities that involve a **risk of serious personal injury** |
| **Time to comply** | A specified period of **at least 21 days** | **Immediate effect** (no period) |
| **Effect on work** | Work may continue during the notice period | Work **must stop** until the risk is removed |
| **Appeal** | May be appealed to an **Employment Tribunal** within 21 days | May be appealed to an **Employment Tribunal** |
| **Example** | The factory floor is slippery because of an oil leak — a notice requires the leak to be fixed within 30 days | A scaffold has a missing guardrail and a worker is in danger of falling — the notice stops work on the scaffold immediately |

**3. Examples:**

- **Improvement Notice Example:** A welding workshop has inadequate local exhaust ventilation. An HSE inspector issues an Improvement Notice requiring installation of LEV within 28 days. Work may continue during this period.
- **Prohibition Notice Example:** A construction site has an unguarded opening on a third-floor slab. An inspector issues a Prohibition Notice, stopping all work on the slab until guardrails are installed.
- **Imminent Danger Notice (Section 23):** After a fatal incident, a Coroner's inquest may lead to a special Prohibition Notice to prevent a recurrence.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Pre-Exam Quick-Recall List for HASAWA**

- ☐ **HASAWA = Health and Safety at Work etc. Act 1974, Chapter 37, came into force 1 April 1975**
- ☐ **Originated from the Robens Committee Report 1972** (Cmnd. 5034, chaired by Lord Robens)
- ☐ **Purpose:** Securing health, safety, and welfare of persons at work + protecting the public
- ☐ **Applies to ALL workplaces** (not just factories)
- ☐ **Key sections — Duties:**
  - **Section 2:** Employer → Employees (general duty of care)
  - **Section 3:** Employer → Public/Non-Employees
  - **Section 4:** Persons controlling premises → Persons affected
  - **Section 5:** Common parts of shared premises
  - **Section 6:** Manufacturers, designers, suppliers, importers, hirers
  - **Section 7:** Employees — duty to take reasonable care
  - **Section 8:** No interference with safety provisions
  - **Section 9:** Self-employed persons
- ☐ **"Reasonably Practicable" (SFAIRP)** — Edwards v National Coal Board [1949] test
- ☐ **Section 2(2) Sub-Clauses (in order):** Plant, Systems, Information/Training/Supervision, Premises, Working Environment
- ☐ **Section 2(3):** Written Safety Policy required for **5+ employees**
- ☐ **Enforcement Body:** Health and Safety Executive (HSE)
- ☐ **Improvement Notice (Sec 21):** Minimum **21 days** to comply
- ☐ **Prohibition Notice (Sec 22):** **Immediate** stop-work
- ☐ **Offence (Sec 33):** Summary — 6 months/£20,000 (now uncapped after 2008); Indictable — 2 years + unlimited fine
- ☐ **Due Diligence Defence (Sec 33(3)):** "Took all reasonable steps" — must be **proven** by defendant
- ☐ **Approved Codes of Practice (ACOP) — Section 17:** Not legally binding, but **persuasive in court** under Section 17(2)
- ☐ **Enabling Act:** Detailed rules are in **Statutory Instruments (SIs)** like MHSW 1999, PUWER 1998, COSHH 2002
- ☐ **Indian Equivalent:** Factories Act 1948 (narrower, sectoral) — HASAWA is broader, universal
- ☐ **HSE is the enforcing authority**, with powers of **entry (Sec 20)**, **seizure**, **notices**, **prosecution**
- ☐ **Tripartite Stakeholder Model:** Manufacturer → Employer → Employee + Public
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ISO Related to Safety
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT523 - MAINTENANCE AND SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** ISO Related to Safety

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is ISO?

**ISO** stands for the **International Organization for Standardization** (note: "ISO" is derived from the Greek word *isos* meaning *equal*, not an acronym). It is an independent, non-governmental international body founded in **1946**, headquartered in **Geneva, Switzerland**, with a current membership of **170 national standards bodies**. ISO develops voluntary, consensus-based international standards that bridge technological and commercial gaps across industries.

## 1.2 What are "ISO Standards Related to Safety"?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
> *ISO standards related to safety are internationally recognized, codified frameworks that specify requirements, guidelines, and best practices for managing occupational health & safety (OH&S), machinery safety, environmental safety, and risk management in engineering operations. They provide a structured, auditable, and globally harmonized approach to eliminate hazards, reduce risks, and continually improve safety performance.*

The most prominent safety-related ISO standards covered in the KTU PERUT523 Module 4 syllabus are:

| Standard | Domain | Year |
|---|---|---|
| **ISO 45001** | Occupational Health & Safety Management System (OH&S) | 2018 |
| **ISO 14001** | Environmental Management System (EMS) | 2015 |
| **ISO 9001** | Quality Management System (QMS) | 2015 |
| **ISO 12100** | Safety of Machinery — Risk Assessment | 2010 |
| **ISO 13849-1** | Safety-related Parts of Control Systems | 2015 |
| **ISO 31000** | Enterprise Risk Management | 2018 |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The International Driving License"

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Plain English):**
> Think of ISO safety standards as the **"International Driving License for Industries."** Just as a driving license guarantees that a driver, regardless of country, knows the universal traffic rules (stop at red, give way at junctions), ISO standards ensure that a factory, mine, or maintenance workshop — whether in India, Germany, or Japan — follows the **same globally accepted safety playbook**. Without ISO, every factory would invent its own safety rules, leading to chaos, accidents, and incompatible audit systems. With ISO, there is a *common language* of safety that workers, engineers, and auditors worldwide can speak fluently.

## 1.4 Why ISO Standards Matter in Maintenance Engineering

Maintenance engineers interact with safety at three critical interfaces:

1. **Direct hazard exposure** — working on energized equipment, rotating machinery, confined spaces.
2. **Procedural control** — Lockout/Tagout (LOTO), Permit-to-Work (PTW) systems.
3. **Management systems** — auditing, documentation, and continual improvement under ISO 45001.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exam:**
> ISO 45001:2018 is the **world's first global OH&S management system standard**, replacing the British **OHSAS 18001:2007** standard. It uses the **Annex SL (High-Level Structure)** so it can be easily integrated with ISO 9001 (Quality) and ISO 14001 (Environmental) — this is called the **"Integrated Management System (IMS)" approach.**

## 1.5 Key Vocabulary Students Must Memorize

- **OH&S** — Occupational Health and Safety
- **OHSAS** — Occupational Health and Safety Assessment Series (now superseded)
- **PDCA** — Plan–Do–Check–Act (Deming Cycle)
- **HLS / Annex SL** — High-Level Structure (10-clause framework)
- **MOC** — Management of Change
- **PPE** — Personal Protective Equipment
- **HIRA** — Hazard Identification and Risk Assessment
- **LTIFR** — Lost Time Injury Frequency Rate
- **IMS** — Integrated Management System
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of ISO Safety Standards

### 2.1.1 ISO 45001:2018 — Occupational Health & Safety Management Systems

ISO 45001 specifies requirements for an OH&S management system and provides guidance for its use. It enables organizations to **provide a safe and healthy workplace**, **prevent work-related injury and ill health**, and **proactively improve OH&S performance**.

**Core Principles (Clause-by-Clause Snapshot — KTU High-Yield):**

| Clause | Title | What It Requires |
|---|---|---|
| **4** | Context of the Organization | Identify internal/external issues, needs of workers & other interested parties, scope of OH&S system |
| **5** | Leadership & Worker Participation | Top management commitment, consultation & participation of workers, OH&S policy |
| **6** | Planning | Identify hazards, assess risks & opportunities, legal compliance, OH&S objectives |
| **7** | Support | Resources, competence, awareness, communication, documented information |
| **8** | Operation | Operational planning & control, emergency preparedness & response |
| **9** | Performance Evaluation | Monitoring, measurement, analysis, internal audit, management review |
| **10** | Improvement | Incident investigation, nonconformity, corrective action, continual improvement |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two game-changing concepts introduced by ISO 45001:**
> 1. **"Context of the Organization" (Clause 4)** — Forces the company to look *outward* and *inward* before designing the safety system.
> 2. **"Risks and Opportunities" (Clause 6.1)** — Replaces the older "preventive action" clause; requires the organization to consider both threats *and* positive opportunities.

### 2.1.2 ISO 14001:2015 — Environmental Management Systems

Provides a framework for organizations to **protect the environment**, **prevent pollution**, and **improve environmental performance**. In maintenance, this governs waste oil disposal, refrigerant handling, effluent from cleaning operations, and ISO 14001-compliant disposal of replaced parts.

### 2.1.3 ISO 9001:2015 — Quality Management Systems

While primarily a quality standard, ISO 9001 ensures that **safety-critical maintenance work** follows documented procedures, calibration of instruments is traceable, and corrective actions are closed. Many organizations integrate ISO 9001 + 14001 + 45001 into a single **Integrated Management System (IMS)**.

### 2.1.4 ISO 12100:2010 — Safety of Machinery

The **"Bible of machinery safety"** — it provides a **risk assessment & risk reduction** methodology used universally by mechanical design engineers. Its 3-step process:

1. **Determine the limits** of the machinery (use, space, time, environment).
2. **Hazard identification** — Mechanical, Electrical, Thermal, Noise, Vibration, Radiation, Material/Substance, Ergonomic.
3. **Risk estimation & evaluation** → Risk = f(Severity × Probability × Possibility of Avoidance).

### 2.1.5 ISO 13849-1:2015 — Safety-related Parts of Control Systems (SRP/CS)

Replaces the older EN 954-1. Introduces **Performance Levels (PL a → PL e)** and the **Category system (B, 1, 2, 3, 4)** for safety functions such as emergency stops, guards, interlocks.

### 2.1.6 ISO 31000:2018 — Risk Management Guidelines

A **generic, all-industry** risk management standard. It is **not certifiable** but provides vocabulary, principles, framework, and process for managing any form of risk (financial, operational, safety, strategic).

## 2.2 The PDCA Cycle (Plan–Do–Check–Act) — Mapping to ISO 45001

> [!NOTE]
> **The Deming/PDCA cycle is the backbone of every modern ISO management system.**

```
PLAN    →  Clauses 4, 5, 6   →  Establish objectives, processes, resources
DO      →  Clauses 7, 8       →  Implement & operate the OH&S processes
CHECK   →  Clause 9           →  Monitor, measure, audit performance
ACT     →  Clause 10          →  Take corrective action & improve continually
```

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| Symbol / Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Severity (S)** | Rated 1 (Negligible) to 5 (Catastrophic) | Dimensionless index |
| **Likelihood (L)** | Rated 1 (Rare) to 5 (Almost Certain) | Dimensionless index |
| **Risk Score (R)** | $R = S \times L$ | Risk Matrix product |
| **Risk Rating** | $R \le 4$: Low; $5 \le R \le 12$: Medium; $R \ge 15$: High | From 5×5 matrix |
| **LTIFR** | $\text{LTIFR} = \dfrac{\text{Number of Lost-Time Injuries} \times 10^{6}}{\text{Total Man-Hours Worked}}$ | Injuries per million man-hours |
| **TRIR (Total Recordable Incident Rate)** | $\text{TRIR} = \dfrac{\text{Recordable Injuries} \times 200{,}000}{\text{Total Man-Hours}}$ | OSHA-aligned; 200,000 = 100 full-time workers × 2,000 hrs |
| **Severity Rate (SR)** | $\text{SR} = \dfrac{\text{Lost Days} \times 200{,}000}{\text{Total Man-Hours}}$ | Days lost per 100 worker-years |
| **Performance Level (PL)** | Probability of dangerous failure per hour | PL a → $10^{-5}$, PL b → $10^{-6}$, PL c → $10^{-7}$, PL d → $10^{-8}$, PL e → $10^{-9}$ |
| **Risk Reduction Factor** | $\text{RRF} = \dfrac{R_{\text{before}}}{R_{\text{after}}}$ | Must be $\ge 1$ for design iteration |
| **Noise Exposure (Lex,8h)** | $L_{ex,8h} = 85 \, \text{dB(A)}$ | ISO 1999 occupational threshold |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **Manufacturing Plants:** ISO 45001 certification is mandatory to bid for contracts with global OEMs (Toyota, Airbus, Boeing).
- **Construction:** ISO 12100 + ISO 13849 govern crane interlocks, scissor-lift guards, and elevator safety circuits.
- **Power Plants:** ISO 14001 governs ash handling, effluent discharge; ISO 45001 governs confined-space entry for boiler inspection.
- **Maintenance Workshops:** ISO 9001 governs calibration traceability; ISO 12100 governs machine guarding during repair.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix (Risk Assessment & PDCA Derivation)

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Risk Score from the 5×5 ISO 45001 Risk Matrix

The ISO 45001-aligned **Hazard Identification and Risk Assessment (HIRA)** procedure uses a quantitative scoring model. Below is the **exhaustive step-by-step derivation** of how a maintenance engineer must arrive at the final risk score.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example Scenario:**
> During preventive maintenance of an industrial hydraulic press, an engineer must reach across an unguarded ram. We will derive the risk score for this hazard.

### Step 1 — Define the Hazard

Hazard: **Crushing injury due to inadvertent ram descent during manual intervention.**

### Step 2 — Assign Severity Rating (S)

Using the KTU standard 5-point severity scale:

| Score | Severity | Example |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Negligible | Bruise / discomfort |
| 2 | Minor | First-aid injury |
| 3 | Moderate | Medical treatment / broken bone |
| 4 | Major | Permanent disability |
| 5 | Catastrophic | Fatality |

For crushing by a hydraulic ram: **S = 5**

### Step 3 — Assign Likelihood Rating (L)

| Score | Likelihood | Frequency Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Rare | < once in 10 years |
| 2 | Unlikely | Once in 5–10 years |
| 3 | Possible | Once in 1–5 years |
| 4 | Likely | Occurs monthly |
| 5 | Almost Certain | Occurs weekly or more |

PM activity happens every 3 months: **L = 4**

### Step 4 — Compute Raw Risk Score (R)

$$R = S \times L$$

Substituting:

$$R = 5 \times 4 = 20$$

### Step 5 — Classify Risk Rating

Using the KTU standard risk-classification band:

$$R \leq 4 \; \Rightarrow \; \text{Low (Acceptable, no action)}$$

$$5 \leq R \leq 12 \; \Rightarrow \; \text{Medium (Mitigate within 30 days)}$$

$$R \geq 15 \; \Rightarrow \; \text{High (Stop operation, immediate controls)}$$

Our result: **R = 20 → HIGH RISK.**

### Step 6 — Apply Hierarchy of Controls (Per ISO 45001 Clause 8.1.2)

| Level | Control | Example Applied to Hazard |
|---|---|---|
| **a) Eliminate** | Remove the hazard | Substitute hydraulic with electrical actuator (costly, often rejected) |
| **b) Substitute** | Replace with lesser hazard | Use a self-locking mechanical pin during maintenance |
| **c) Engineering** | Guards / interlocks | Install light curtain + two-hand control |
| **d) Administrative** | Procedures / training | Lockout-Tagout (LOTO) + Permit-to-Work |
| **e) PPE** | Personal protection | Steel-toe boots, cut-resistant gloves |

After implementing **LOTO + light curtain + training (d, c)**, re-score:

$$S_{\text{new}} = 5 \quad ; \quad L_{\text{new}} = 1$$

$$R_{\text{new}} = 5 \times 1 = 5 \quad \Rightarrow \; \text{Medium, tolerable}$$

### Step 7 — Compute the Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)

$$\text{RRF} = \frac{R_{\text{before}}}{R_{\text{after}}} = \frac{20}{5} = 4$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The integrated control package reduced the risk by a factor of **4**, which is acceptable under the ALARP (As Low As Reasonably Practicable) principle of ISO 12100 and ISO 45001 Clause 8.1.2.

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation: LTIFR for a Maintenance Department

Given data for one year:

- Number of lost-time injuries = **3**
- Total man-hours worked = **500,000**

Apply the KTU formula:

$$\text{LTIFR} = \frac{\text{Lost-Time Injuries} \times 10^{6}}{\text{Total Man-Hours}}$$

$$\text{LTIFR} = \frac{3 \times 10^{6}}{500{,}000}$$

$$\text{LTIFR} = \frac{3{,}000{,}000}{500{,}000} = 6.0$$

**Result:** **LTIFR = 6.0 lost-time injuries per million man-hours worked.**

Compare with the manufacturing-industry benchmark (typically **2.0 to 4.0**): the maintenance department is performing **worse than industry average** — a management-review trigger under ISO 45001 Clause 9.3.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Three ISO Standards

| Parameter | ISO 45001:2018 | ISO 14001:2015 | ISO 9001:2015 |
|---|---|---|---|
| Primary Focus | Worker safety & health | Environmental protection | Customer quality |
| Replaces | OHSAS 18001 | ISO 14001:2004 | ISO 9001:2008 |
| High-Level Structure | Annex SL (10 clauses) | Annex SL (10 clauses) | Annex SL (10 clauses) |
| Risk Approach | Risks & Opportunities (Clause 6.1) | Risks & Opportunities (Clause 6.1) | Risks & Opportunities (Clause 6.1) |
| Worker Participation | Mandatory (Clause 5.4) | Not required | Not required |
| Documented Information | Mandatory | Mandatory | Mandatory |
| Certification | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Maintenance Engineering Use | LOTO, PTW, HIRA | Waste oil, refrigerant | Calibration, SOPs |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ISO 45001 High-Level Structure (Annex SL) — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Clause 4: Context of Organization"] --> B["Clause 5: Leadership and Worker Participation"]
    B --> C["Clause 6: Planning - HIRA and Objectives"]
    C --> D["Clause 7: Support - Resources Competence Awareness"]
    D --> E["Clause 8: Operation - Operational Controls and Emergency"]
    E --> F["Clause 9: Performance Evaluation - Audit and Review"]
    F --> G["Clause 10: Improvement - Corrective Action and Continual Improvement"]
    G -->|Feedback Loop| C

    H["PLAN - Clauses 4 to 6"] -.-> A
    I["DO - Clauses 7 and 8"] -.-> D
    J["CHECK - Clause 9"] -.-> F
    K["ACT - Clause 10"] -.-> G

    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#FF8C00,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#4682B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#4682B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#32CD32,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#228B22,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Risk Assessment Process Flow (ISO 12100 + ISO 45001)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["Step 1: Determine Machine Limits"] --> S2["Step 2: Identify Hazards"]
    S2 --> S3["Step 3: Estimate Risk - Severity and Likelihood"]
    S3 --> S4{"Risk Acceptable?"}
    S4 -->|Yes| S7["Operate and Monitor"]
    S4 -->|No| S5["Step 4: Apply Hierarchy of Controls"]
    S5 --> S6["Step 5: Re-estimate Reduced Risk"]
    S6 --> S4
    S7 --> S8["Periodic Review"]

    style S1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style S2 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style S3 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style S4 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style S5 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style S6 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style S7 fill:#32CD32,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style S8 fill:#4682B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

## 4.3 Integrated Management System (IMS) Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TOP["Top Management Leadership"]
        L["Shared Policy, Objectives, and Management Review"]
    end

    subgraph IMS["Integrated Management System"]
        Q["ISO 9001 Quality"]
        E["ISO 14001 Environment"]
        H["ISO 45001 Health and Safety"]
        R["ISO 31000 Risk"]
        M["ISO 12100 Machinery Safety"]
    end

    subgraph BASE["Operational Foundation"]
        OP["Maintenance, Production, Procurement, HR, Logistics"]
    end

    TOP --> IMS
    IMS --> BASE
    BASE -->|Data and KPIs| TOP

    style TOP fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style IMS fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style BASE fill:#F0FFF0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.4 Hierarchy of Controls Pyramid (ISO 45001 Clause 8.1.2)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["ELIMINATE - Remove hazard entirely"] --> P2["SUBSTITUTE - Replace with lesser hazard"]
    P2 --> P3["ENGINEERING CONTROLS - Guards, interlocks, ventilation"]
    P3 --> P4["ADMINISTRATIVE CONTROLS - Procedures, training, signage"]
    P4 --> P5["PPE - Personal Protective Equipment last resort"]

    style P1 fill:#006400,color:#fff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style P2 fill:#228B22,color:#fff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style P3 fill:#32CD32,stroke:#000,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style P4 fill:#FFD700,color:#000,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style P5 fill:#FF6347,color:#fff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Model Paper]
**"List any three ISO standards related to industrial safety and state the focus of each."**

**Model Answer:**
1. **ISO 45001:2018** — Focuses on Occupational Health and Safety Management Systems (OH&S). It specifies requirements for an OH&S management system and guides organizations to provide a safe and healthy workplace, prevent work-related injury and ill health, and proactively improve OH&S performance.
2. **ISO 14001:2015** — Focuses on Environmental Management Systems (EMS). It provides a framework for organizations to protect the environment, prevent pollution, and improve environmental performance.
3. **ISO 12100:2010** — Focuses on Safety of Machinery. It provides a general methodology for risk assessment and risk reduction to help designers achieve safety in the design of machinery.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**"Differentiate between ISO 45001 and OHSAS 18001."**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | OHSAS 18001:2007 | ISO 45001:2018 |
|---|---|---|
| Origin | British Standards Institution | ISO (international consensus) |
| Structure | 4-clause British model | 10-clause Annex SL High-Level Structure |
| Management Involvement | Less prescriptive | Mandatory top-management leadership (Clause 5) |
| Worker Participation | Indirect | Mandatory consultation & participation (Clause 5.4) |
| Risk Approach | Preventive Action | Risks and Opportunities (Clause 6.1) |
| Integration with ISO 9001 / 14001 | Difficult | Easy (same HLS) |
| Current Status | **Withdrawn** since March 2021 | Active global standard |

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 4]

**(a)** Explain the **High-Level Structure (Annex SL)** of ISO 45001:2018 with its 10 clauses and show how they map onto the **PDCA cycle**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction:**
ISO 45001:2018 is built on the **Annex SL framework** — a 10-clause template adopted by all new ISO management-system standards (9001, 14001, 27001, etc.) to enable **seamless integration** across disciplines.

**The 10 Clauses with PDCA Mapping:**

| Clause | Title | PDCA Phase | Key Requirement |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | Scope | — | States applicability of the standard |
| **2** | Normative References | — | Refers to referenced documents |
| **3** | Terms & Definitions | — | Defines key vocabulary |
| **4** | Context of the Organization | **PLAN** | Identify internal/external issues, scope, interested parties |
| **5** | Leadership & Worker Participation | **PLAN** | Top management commitment, OH&S policy, worker consultation |
| **6** | Planning | **PLAN** | HIRA, legal compliance, OH&S objectives & KPIs |
| **7** | Support | **DO** | Resources, competence, awareness, communication, documented info |
| **8** | Operation | **DO** | Operational controls, emergency preparedness, management of change |
| **9** | Performance Evaluation | **CHECK** | Monitoring, internal audit, management review |
| **10** | Improvement | **ACT** | Incident investigation, corrective action, continual improvement |

**PDCA Mapping Explanation:**
- **Plan (Clauses 4–6):** The organization first understands its context, secures leadership commitment, identifies hazards, assesses risks, and sets OH&S objectives.
- **Do (Clauses 7–8):** Resources are mobilized, competence is built, and operational controls (LOTO, PTW, PPE) are deployed.
- **Check (Clause 9):** Performance is measured against KPIs (LTIFR, near-miss reports), and internal audits verify compliance.
- **Act (Clause 10):** Nonconformities trigger corrective actions, and the management-review process feeds improvements back into the Plan phase — closing the loop.

**Conclusion:**
The Annex SL structure ensures that ISO 45001 is auditable, integratable with ISO 14001 and ISO 9001, and aligned with the **Deming philosophy of continual improvement.**

**(b)** A maintenance workshop had **5 lost-time injuries** and **800,000 man-hours worked** in 2023. Calculate the **LTIFR**. If the industry benchmark is **3.0**, what action should the management take under **ISO 45001 Clause 9.3**? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Recall the formula:**

$$\text{LTIFR} = \frac{\text{Number of Lost-Time Injuries} \times 10^{6}}{\text{Total Man-Hours Worked}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$\text{LTIFR} = \frac{5 \times 10^{6}}{800{,}000}$$

**Step 3 — Compute:**

$$\text{LTIFR} = \frac{5{,}000{,}000}{800{,}000} = 6.25$$

**Result:** **LTIFR = 6.25 lost-time injuries per million man-hours.**

**Step 4 — Compare with benchmark:**
Industry benchmark = **3.0**. Workshop LTIFR = **6.25**, which is **more than double** the benchmark.

**Step 5 — Required Management Action under ISO 45001 Clause 9.3 (Management Review):**
1. **Trigger an out-of-cycle management review** to investigate root causes (could include a 5-Why or Fishbone analysis).
2. **Review HIRA outputs** to check whether hazards were correctly identified and whether controls failed.
3. **Allocate additional resources** (budget for engineering controls, training hours).
4. **Revise OH&S objectives** to include specific LTIFR reduction targets.
5. **Engage workers (Clause 5.4)** through safety committees to gather ground-level input.
6. **Document the review** and decisions as per Clause 7.5 (Documented Information).

**Conclusion:**
Because LTIFR significantly exceeds the benchmark, the maintenance workshop is **non-compliant with the continual-improvement intent of ISO 45001** and must execute a structured corrective-action plan with measurable targets.
